<DOCUMENT id="001" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : ColdFusion APSB26-68: Seven CVSS 10.0 Flaws and a Two-Hour Exploitation Window
DATE       : 2026-07-15
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/07/15/coldfusion-apsb26-68-cvss10-bonanza/
CVE        : CVE-2026-48276, CVE-2026-48277, CVE-2026-48281, CVE-2026-48282, cve-2026-48282, CVE-2026-48283, CVE-2026-48286, CVE-2026-48316
CATEGORIES : exploits, web, vuln-research
TAGS       : CVE-2026-48282, CVE-2026-48276, CVE-2026-48277, CVE-2026-48281, CVE-2026-48316, CVE-2026-48283, CVE-2026-48286, adobe, coldfusion, RCE, path-traversal, unrestricted-upload, CISA-KEV, watchtowr
WORD_COUNT : 1843
EXCERPT    : Adobe dropped patches for 11 critical ColdFusion flaws — six of them CVSS 10.0 — on June 30. Attackers had working exploits against CVE-2026-48282 within two hours of disclosure. This is the anatomy of the RDS FILEIO path traversal and the unrestricted upload bugs that made it possible, why ColdFusion keeps getting hammered, and what to do if you're still running a vulnerable instance.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
**CVE:** CVE-2026-48282, CVE-2026-48276, CVE-2026-48277, CVE-2026-48281, CVE-2026-48316, CVE-2026-48283, CVE-2026-48286  
**CVSS:** 10.0 (Critical) — seven of them  
**Disclosed:** June 30, 2026  
**Affected:** ColdFusion 2025 Update 9 and earlier, ColdFusion 2023 Update 20 and earlier, Campaign Classic v7.4.3 build 9396 and earlier  
**Fixed in:** ColdFusion 2025 Update 10, ColdFusion 2023 Update 21, Campaign Classic v7.4.3 build 9397  
**CISA KEV:** CVE-2026-48282 added July 7, 2026  
**Public analysis:** [watchTowr Labs](https://labs.watchtowr.com/its-37oc-and-all-we-can-think-about-is-coldfusion-adobe-coldfusion-security-bulletin-apsb26-68-cve-bonanza/)

---

On June 30, 2026, Adobe published APSB26-68, a security bulletin covering 11 critical vulnerabilities in ColdFusion 2023 and 2025. Six of them carry CVSS 10.0 — the maximum possible score. A seventh, in Adobe Campaign Classic, also scores 10.0. That is not a typo. Seven distinct vulnerabilities that each earn a perfect severity rating, all fixed in the same patch release.

What happened next is a case study in how fast modern exploitation moves. Two days after the bulletin, watchTowr researchers published a technical analysis breaking down the RDS FILEIO path traversal (CVE-2026-48282) and the unrestricted file upload bugs. Within two hours of that publication, KEVIntel's global honeypot network recorded active exploit traffic. An actor operating from Indian infrastructure was already probing every internet-facing ColdFusion server they could find.

CISA added CVE-2026-48282 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on July 7. As of this week, Shadowserver tracks roughly 750 publicly exposed ColdFusion instances. How many are patched is anyone's guess.

This post walks through the two most interesting bugs — the RDS FILEIO path traversal and the unrestricted file upload — then discusses why ColdFusion keeps generating these clusters of maximum-severity vulnerabilities and what defenders can actually do about it.

---

## The Bug That Blew Up First: CVE-2026-48282 (RDS FILEIO Path Traversal)

ColdFusion's Remote Development Services (RDS) is a legacy feature dating back to the Dreamweaver era. It lets an IDE — historically ColdFusion Builder, Dreamweaver, or the Eclipse plugin — communicate with a running ColdFusion server over HTTP-based RPC. Through RDS a developer can browse the filesystem, execute database queries, deploy files, and debug applications.

One of the RDS services is **FILEIO**, a servlet that handles remote file operations: read, write, copy, delete, list directory contents. It was designed for developer convenience, not for exposure to the open internet. But ColdFusion servers often sit behind a web server that passes requests through, and RDS is enabled by default in many configurations.

The vulnerability is a path traversal in the FILEIO handler. The servlet constructs file paths from user-supplied parameters without adequate validation, and the ColdFusion server runs with high integrity (often `NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM` on Windows or root-equivalent on Linux). An unauthenticated attacker sends a crafted POST request to:

```
POST /CFIDE/main/ide.cfm?ACTION=FILEIO
Content-Type: application/octet-stream

<length-prefixed RDS body with path=../../../../../../inetpub/wwwroot/shell.cfm>
```

The server resolves the path, writes the file to the web root, and the attacker accesses it directly via the web server to execute arbitrary CFML code. No login. No interaction. One request.

The request flow looks like this:

```
Attacker                  ColdFusion Server
   │                            │
   │  POST /CFIDE/main/ide.cfm  │
   │  ?ACTION=FILEIO            │
   │  path=../../../www/shell.cfm │
   │───────────────────────────►│
   │                            │
   │                    RdsFrontEndServlet
   │                            │
   │                    ┌───────┴────────┐
   │                    │  FILEIOServlet  │
   │                    │  processCmd()   │
   │                    │  writeFile()    │
   │                    └───────┬────────┘
   │                            │
   │  <length-prefixed RDS      │
   │   response: OK>            │
   │◄───────────────────────────│
   │                            │
   │  GET /shell.cfm            │
   │───────────────────────────►│
   │                            │
   │  HTTP 200 (RCE achieved)   │
   │◄───────────────────────────│
```

watchTowr's analysis confirmed that the FILEIO handler uses a `processCmd()` dispatch that maps the `ACTION` parameter directly to command implementations. The `writeFile` command takes a `path` parameter and writes the request body to that location. Path traversal sequences like `../` are not stripped or validated.

The researchers described it in characteristically blunt terms during their July 2 write-up:

> *"We can write a file anywhere on the filesystem. Since ColdFusion serves CFM templates from its web root, we can simply write a .cfm shell and execute it."*

## The Unrestricted Upload: CVE-2026-48276

While CVE-2026-48282 is the headline grabber due to confirmed active exploitation, CVE-2026-48276 is arguably worse in coverage. It is an unrestricted file upload vulnerability (CWE-434) in a different code path that also allows an unauthenticated attacker to upload a file with a dangerous type.

The key difference: CVE-2026-48276 does not require RDS to be enabled. It targets a separate file upload handler that accepts multipart form data and writes uploaded files to disk without validating the file extension or content type. A `.cfm`, `.jsp`, or `.exe` file can be uploaded and subsequently executed.

Where CVE-2026-48282 requires the RDS feature to be accessible (a condition that varies by deployment), CVE-2026-48276 is reachable on any ColdFusion server that exposes a file upload endpoint — and ColdFusion's default configuration includes several of those.

Both bugs achieve the same end state: unauthenticated remote code execution with a single HTTP request.

## Why Seven CVSS 10.0s in One Bulletin?

This is not an isolated incident. ColdFusion has a long track record of critical vulnerabilities. Sixteen ColdFusion CVEs already sit in the CISA KEV catalog. A 2023 patch batch included multiple CVSS 9.8 RCE flaws. In 2024, attackers exploited ColdFusion vulnerabilities to deploy webshells on government servers.

The pattern is structural, not accidental. Several factors converge:

**Legacy surface area.** RDS, the admin console (`/CFIDE/administrator/`), and various built-in file upload handlers date back 15-20 years. They were designed for a different threat model — internal networks, trusted developers, no internet exposure. They have not been meaningfully refactored.

**Java stack complexity.** ColdFusion runs on a Java server engine that layers CFML on top of the JVM. The attack surface includes the Java servlets that implement RDS, the CFML interpreter itself, the tag libraries, and the connector layer to the web server. Each layer introduces its own parsing and validation logic, and the interactions between them are where path traversals and file type confusion bugs live.

**Slow patching cadence in the installed base.** ColdFusion is disproportionately deployed in enterprise and government environments where patching cycles are measured in weeks or months. Attackers know this. The economics of developing an exploit for ColdFusion are favorable because the window between disclosure and mass patching is reliably long.

**Credentialed vs. unauthenticated confusion.** Several of the CVSS 10.0 bugs in this batch were initially flagged as requiring authentication. Subsequent analysis showed they are reachable without it. The distinction between "requires admin console access" and "requires authentication" gets blurry when the admin console itself is reachable without authentication in default configurations.

## The Two-Hour Window

The timeline of CVE-2026-48282 exploitation is worth restating because it sets a new baseline for how fast the window can close:

- **June 30** — Adobe releases APSB26-68 with patches. The bulletin includes minimal technical detail.
- **July 2** — watchTowr publishes technical analysis of the RDS FILEIO path traversal, including request structures and exploitation mechanics.
- **July 2 (2 hours later)** — KEVIntel's honeypot network detects active exploitation attempts. The attacker infrastructure routes through Indian IP space.
- **July 7** — CISA adds CVE-2026-48282 to the KEV catalog. BOD 26-04 requires federal agencies to patch within 21 days.

The takeaway: the window between "technical details published" and "active scanning at internet scale" is now measured in hours, not days. If you do not have a mechanism to patch critical-severity vulnerabilities within 24 hours of public disclosure, you are racing the attackers and losing.

## Detection

For CVE-2026-48282 (RDS path traversal):

**Network-level:**
```
POST /CFIDE/main/ide.cfm
ACTION=FILEIO
Content-Type: application/octet-stream
```

Monitor for POST requests to `/CFIDE/main/ide.cfm` with `ACTION=FILEIO` originating from IPs not in your trusted development range. In most production environments, there should be zero traffic to this endpoint.

**File-system level:**
```
find /opt/coldfusion/wwwroot -name "*.cfm" -newer /opt/coldfusion/lib/patches.txt
```

Unexpected `.cfm` files appearing in the web root after June 30, 2026, are indicators of compromise. Check file creation timestamps against your patch deployment date.

For CVE-2026-48276 (unrestricted upload):

Monitor for multipart file uploads that result in `.cfm`, `.jsp`, or `.exe` files being written to the web root. Legitimate ColdFusion upload functionality typically restricts uploads to non-executable directories or validates file extensions.

## Mitigation (If You Cannot Patch Immediately)

1. **Disable RDS** if you are not actively using remote development:

   In `ColdFusion Administrator → Server Settings → Settings`, uncheck "Enable RDS". Alternatively, block `/CFIDE/main/ide.cfm` at the web server level:

   ```apache
   # Apache
   <LocationMatch "^/CFIDE/main/ide\.cfm">
       Require ip 10.0.0.0/8
   </LocationMatch>

   # IIS
   <location path="CFIDE/main/ide.cfm">
       <system.webServer>
           <security>
               <ipSecurity allowUnlisted="false">
                   <add ipAddress="10.0.0.0" subnetMask="255.0.0.0" allowed="true"/>
               </ipSecurity>
           </security>
       </system.webServer>
   </location>

   # Nginx
   location ~ ^/CFIDE/main/ide\.cfm {
       allow 10.0.0.0/8;
       deny all;
   }
   ```

2. **Do not expose the ColdFusion admin console to the internet.** The `/CFIDE/administrator/` path should be restricted to internal networks or VPN only. This is not a complete mitigation for CVE-2026-48276 (which does not require RDS), but it reduces the overall attack surface.

3. **Remove sample applications** and the default cfdocs folder if present. Old ColdFusion installs ship with sample files that include additional upload handlers.

4. **Run a ColdFusion-specific WAF rule** blocking path traversal sequences (`../`, `..\`, URL-encoded variants) in the `path` parameter of requests to `/CFIDE/main/ide.cfm`.

## The Pattern and What It Means

ColdFusion is not going away. It runs in too many enterprises, governments, and universities. But it has become a reliable source of maximum-severity, remotely exploitable vulnerabilities that attackers weaponize within hours of disclosure.

The lesson from APSB26-68 is not specific to Adobe or ColdFusion. It is a lesson about legacy surface area on the internet. Any application platform that:
- was designed before 2010,
- exposes administrative or development endpoints via HTTP,
- runs with high OS privileges,
- has a long patch cycle in its user base,

...will eventually produce a bulletin like this one. The only question is when.

For defenders running ColdFusion, the playbook is straightforward but requires discipline: inventory every instance, verify the version, apply Update 10 (2025) or Update 21 (2023) immediately, disable RDS where it is not needed, and monitor for unexpected `.cfm` files appearing in web-accessible directories. The attackers are already scanning. Assume they found you before you read this sentence.

---

*Based on analysis from [watchTowr Labs](https://labs.watchtowr.com/its-37oc-and-all-we-can-think-about-is-coldfusion-adobe-coldfusion-security-bulletin-apsb26-68-cve-bonanza/), [KEVIntel](https://kevintel.io), [Resecurity](https://www.resecurity.com/blog/article/cve-2026-48282-adobe-coldfusion-rds-path-traversal-leading-to-rce), [Hive Pro](https://www.hivepro.com/threat-advisory/coldfusion-path-traversal-cve-2026-48282-under-active-attack), [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/max-severity-adobe-coldfusion-flaw-now-exploited-in-attacks/), and [CISA](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/alerts/2026/07/07/cisa-adds-one-known-exploited-vulnerability-catalog).*
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

<DOCUMENT id="002" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-55200: The Same libssh2 Overflow Came Back to the Same Function
DATE       : 2026-07-01
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/07/01/cve-2026-55200-libssh2-transport-read-oob-write/
CVE        : CVE-2019-3855, CVE-2025-15661, CVE-2026-55199, cve-2026-55200, CVE-2026-55200
CATEGORIES : exploits, supply-chain
TAGS       : cve-2026-55200, libssh2, ssh, integer-overflow, rce, curl, supply-chain
WORD_COUNT : 1276
EXCERPT    : A server-controlled length field, 32-bit math, and no upper bound. libssh2 shipped almost this exact bug in 2019, patched it, and let it back into the same transport read seven years later.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here's what gets me about this one. It isn't that libssh2 has an integer overflow in its packet parser. It's that libssh2 had almost this exact integer overflow in its packet parser in 2019, shipped a fix, and then in 2026 the same class of bug turned up in the same function. CVE-2026-55200 lives in `ssh2_transport_read()`, in `transport.c`. That is the same file and the same read path that gave us CVE-2019-3855 seven years ago.

libssh2 is a client library. Not a server. It implements SSHv2 for programs that need to reach out and talk to a remote host, and it is wired into a lot of them without much fanfare. curl uses it for `sftp://`. Git links it. PHP's ssh2 extension is built on it. Beyond those you find R's ssh package, backup agents, firmware updaters, and a long tail of appliances. Plenty of them ship a statically linked copy, which means a distro package bump never touches them and you may not even remember the library is inside your binary.

So the attack runs backwards from the usual SSH story. You don't attack a server. A malicious or compromised server attacks the client that connects to it. The overflow fires during the transport handshake, before authentication, before your client ever proves who it is. No credentials. No user clicking anything. If you can get a libssh2 program to open a connection to a box you control, you are already sitting on the vulnerable code.

## The bug is boring, which is the problem

Every SSH packet starts with a 4-byte `packet_length` field that declares how many bytes follow. The server picks that number. `ssh2_transport_read()` sanity-checked it, sort of. It rejected values below 1. It never set an upper bound.

Then the size math runs in 32-bit arithmetic. The function adds `packet_length` to a couple of small constants to work out how big a buffer it needs. Feed it `0xffffffff` and that addition wraps past `UINT_MAX` back down to a tiny number. libssh2 allocates a buffer for the tiny number. Later code copies the full, real-sized packet into that undersized buffer.

```c
/* packet_length is fully server-controlled */
packet_length = read_u32(payload);
if (packet_length < 1)
    return LIBSSH2_ERROR_DECRYPT;   /* only the lower bound was checked */

/* 32-bit add wraps when packet_length sits near UINT_MAX */
alloc = packet_length + padding_length + mac_len;   /* tiny after overflow */
buf   = LIBSSH2_ALLOC(session, alloc);              /* undersized allocation */
memcpy(buf, payload, packet_length);                /* writes the real, large length */
```

That is CWE-680, integer overflow leading to a heap buffer overflow, and it is one of the oldest reliable roads to code execution there is. VulnCheck scored it 9.2 under CVSS 4.0. Network vector, and it needs neither privileges nor a user doing anything on the client side.

The fix is a single bounds check. Commit `97acf3d` rejects any `packet_length` above `LIBSSH2_PACKET_MAXPAYLOAD` before the arithmetic gets to run, so the value can never grow large enough to wrap. Maintainers merged it through pull request #2052 on June 12. VulnCheck published the CVE on June 17. The change is going out as libssh2 1.11.2, and everything at or below 1.11.1 is exposed.

## The part I can't get past

Back in March 2019, libssh2 1.8.1 shipped to close out nine flaws at once, and the headliner was CVE-2019-3855: an integer overflow in the transport read that let a malicious server run code on a connecting client. Read that description again. It is this bug. Same function, same primitive, same inverted threat model, same shape of fix. Seven years and one patched CVE later, the upper bound went missing again.

Packet-length validation in an SSH transport is exactly the code you write once, carefully, and never touch again without rereading the old bug reports. Somewhere between 2019 and now, the surrounding function got refactored and the guard that mattered didn't survive the trip.

## What the PoC actually is

On exploitation I'll keep it honest. A proof of concept did drop on June 29, posted to a GitHub archive called exploitarium. What sits in there is a local trigger scaffold and a controlled local RCE harness, not a turnkey remote exploit you point at a victim. The author says entries went out without coordinated reporting, some of them half finished, with an AI doing a chunk of the fuzzing. Turning that into reliable code execution against a real target still depends on the specific binary, its allocator behavior, whatever mitigations it was built with, and how it embeds libssh2. Read "working PoC" with a raised eyebrow. The trigger is real. A dependable remote exploit is more work, and nobody has published that part.

CISA's exploitation field for CVE-2026-55200 still reads none, and no in-the-wild use has been reported as of this writing. That is the current state, not a promise about next week. A pre-auth memory-corruption bug in a library this widely embedded is the kind of thing that stays quiet until it doesn't.

## What to do about it

Patch to a build that carries commit `97acf3d`, which means libssh2 1.11.2 or a distro backport. Debian already has a repaired build in testing and other distros are rolling their own. That covers your system package.

It does not cover the copies you forgot about, and this is the part that bites. Static and bundled libssh2 inside container images, language wheels, vendor binaries, and appliance firmware will not move when you upgrade the system library. Go find them.

```bash
# the system copy your package manager knows about
ldconfig -p | grep libssh2

# copies statically or dynamically linked into binaries under /usr and /opt
find /usr /opt -type f -executable -exec sh -c \
  'ldd "$1" 2>/dev/null | grep -q libssh2 && echo "$1"' _ {} \;
```

Until every copy is patched, put controls on the network side. libssh2 clients should only reach SSH and SFTP endpoints you actually trust, and they should verify host keys instead of accepting them blind, because the whole attack depends on steering a client toward a hostile server through DNS games, a MITM on an untrusted path, or a legitimate host that got compromised. For detection, watch for oversized-packet anomalies during SSH negotiation and for client processes crashing or dumping core inside `ssh2_transport_read()`.

While you are in the library, clear the rest of the batch. CVE-2026-55199 (CVSS 8.2) is a denial of service that traps a client in a CPU loop with a bogus extension count, and CVE-2025-15661 (CVSS 8.3) is an SFTP heap over-read. Same library, same update window, no reason to make two passes.

The lesson here isn't "validate your lengths." Everyone already knows that. It's that a fix landing in a bug tracker doesn't vaccinate the code around it. The 2019 patch fixed the specific overflow it was written for and left the surrounding function free to grow the same bug again. If you maintain a parser that reads attacker-controlled sizes, the check you added years ago is worth rereading, because the person who reintroduces your old vulnerability is usually you.

**Fix commit:** [libssh2 97acf3d](https://github.com/libssh2/libssh2/commit/97acf3dfda80c91c3a8c9f2372546301d4a1a7a8)  
**Patch PR:** [libssh2#2052](https://github.com/libssh2/libssh2/pull/2052)  
**Advisory:** [VulnCheck CVE-2026-55200](https://www.vulncheck.com/advisories/libssh2-out-of-bounds-write-via-unchecked-packet-length-in-transport-c)  
**Prior art:** [CVE-2019-3855](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2019-3855)  
**CVE record:** [CVE-2026-55200](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-55200)
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="003" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-31429: KFENCE Breaks the One Assumption skb_kfree_head Relied On
DATE       : 2026-06-29
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/29/cve-2026-31429-kfence-skb-cross-cache-free/
CVE        : CVE-2026-31429, cve-2026-31429
CATEGORIES : exploits, linux-kernel
TAGS       : cve-2026-31429, kfence, slub, cross-cache, skb, bpf, linux-kernel
WORD_COUNT : 1277
EXCERPT    : A non-power-of-2 cache size was supposed to make SKB frees self-identifying. Then KFENCE started reporting exact allocation sizes, and the heuristic walked straight into a cross-cache free.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here's what gets me about this one. The bug isn't a missing bounds check or a sloppy length calculation. It's two pieces of kernel code that are each correct in isolation, each doing exactly what its author intended, and the collision between them frees a heap object to the wrong slab cache. One piece is a networking performance optimization from 2023. The other is a memory-safety debug feature. The safety feature is what breaks the optimization.

CVE-2026-31429 is a slab cross-cache free in `net/core/skbuff.c`, reported by Antonius (handle w1sdom) at Blue Dragon Security and published April 20, 2026. I'll be straight with you about the severity before we go further. It's rated Medium, the EPSS sits at 0.02 percent, and CISA has not flagged it as exploited. This is not an unprivileged-to-root headline. I'm writing it up anyway because the root cause is one of the cleaner allocator-confusion bugs I've read all year, and the thing it teaches about KFENCE reaches a lot further than this one function.

## The optimization that set the trap

Back in kernel 6.3, commit `bf9f1baa279f` ("net: add dedicated kmem_cache for typical/small skb->head") added a slab cache called `skb_small_head_cache`. The idea was reasonable. Most socket buffer head allocations are small and uniform, so giving them a dedicated cache cuts fragmentation and speeds up the hot path.

The clever part, and the part that matters here, is `SKB_SMALL_HEAD_CACHE_SIZE`. On x86_64 it's 704 bytes. That number is deliberately not a power of two. Generic kmalloc buckets are always powers of two: 512, then 1024, on up. By picking 704 the kernel guaranteed that a small-head allocation could never be confused size-wise with a generic kmalloc object. That uniqueness quietly became load-bearing.

## A free path that trusts arithmetic

When an SKB head is released, `skb_kfree_head()` decides which cache to return it to. The pre-fix version looked like this:

```c
static void skb_kfree_head(void *head, unsigned int end_offset)
{
	if (end_offset == SKB_SMALL_HEAD_HEADROOM)
		kmem_cache_free(net_hotdata.skb_small_head_cache, head);
	else
		kfree(head);
}
```

No metadata lookup. No flag stored on the object. The function infers the origin cache purely from `end_offset`, which is derived from `ksize()` on the buffer. Under normal SLUB behavior `ksize()` returns the bucket size, so a 704-byte request reports 1024, and the offset math never collides with `SKB_SMALL_HEAD_HEADROOM`. The heuristic holds. It's the kind of shortcut that's fine right up until one of its assumptions stops being true.

## KFENCE returns a different number

KFENCE (Kernel Electric-Fence) is a low-overhead sampling allocator that serves a small fraction of allocations from guard-paged memory to catch out-of-bounds and use-after-free bugs in production. Distros ship it on by default. Ubuntu and Fedora both build with `CONFIG_KFENCE=y`.

KFENCE has one behavior that matters here. When it intercepts an allocation, `kfence_ksize()` returns the exact requested size, not the rounded bucket size. For debugging that's the correct choice. You want to know you asked for 704, not 1024. But `skb_kfree_head()` was leaning on the rounding.

Watch the arithmetic fall over. `sizeof(struct skb_shared_info)` is 320 bytes on x86_64. When KFENCE intercepts a 704-byte SKB head allocation:

```
ksize(head)             = 704        (kfence_ksize, exact)
skb_end_offset          = 704 - 320  = 384
SKB_SMALL_HEAD_HEADROOM = 704 - 320  = 384   <-- false match
```

The offset now equals `SKB_SMALL_HEAD_HEADROOM`, so the free path calls `kmem_cache_free(skb_small_head_cache, head)` on an object that actually came from `kmalloc-1k`. SLUB catches it, because modern SLUB validates the owning cache on free:

```
kmem_cache_free(skbuff_small_head, ffff888186d6e000): object belongs to different cache kmalloc-1k
WARNING: mm/slub.c:6258 at warn_free_bad_obj+0x91/0xc0
```

## Reaching the allocation

The reported trigger is the BPF self-test path. `BPF_PROG_TEST_RUN` (bpf(2) command 10) routes through `bpf_prog_test_run_skb()` into `bpf_test_init()`, which does a `kzalloc()` sized to land exactly on `SKB_SMALL_HEAD_CACHE_SIZE`. Blue Dragon's proof of concept loads a three-instruction `BPF_PROG_TYPE_SCHED_CLS` program (load a 64-bit immediate, then exit), then fires `BPF_PROG_TEST_RUN` with 284 bytes of input data, `BPF_F_TEST_RUN_ON_CPU` set to pin execution to CPU 0, and a small repeat count. After that it loops fifty more calls to ride the KFENCE sampling window. KFENCE only samples occasionally, so you spray the call until you win the interception.

The repo ships the PoC and a raw `dmesg.txt`, not a weaponized exploit. Reproduced on 7.0.0-rc5 under QEMU, the warning cascades four times, mangling the SLUB stack-depot tracking metadata on the way out.

## What this actually buys an attacker

This is where I want to push back on anyone about to file it as a root exploit. Loading a `SCHED_CLS` program needs `CAP_BPF` together with `CAP_NET_ADMIN`, or `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` on its own. Through this path you already hold privilege before you trigger anything. So as written, it isn't privilege escalation. It's a primitive.

A cross-cache free is a strong primitive though. Free a `kmalloc-1k` object into `skb_small_head_cache`, get it reallocated as a different object type, and now you're holding a use-after-free with type confusion straddling two caches. The realistic target isn't your laptop. It's a container that was handed `CAP_BPF` and wants to corrupt host kernel memory to break the namespace boundary, or an attacker sitting on `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` who wants to defeat lockdown or quietly unhook an LSM. Whether the `bpf_test_init` path can be massaged into a stable, reusable UAF is the open question, and it's the part I'd actually spend my time on if I were chasing this.

## Fixing it

The upstream fix deletes the heuristic outright:

```c
static void skb_kfree_head(void *head, unsigned int end_offset)
{
	kfree(head);
}
```

`kfree()` already resolves the owning cache from the slab page, so the manual classification was never needed once you stop trusting `end_offset`. Jiayuan Chen authored the patch, Eric Dumazet reviewed it, Jakub Kicinski merged it to mainline as `0f42e3f4fe2a` on April 6, 2026, and Greg Kroah-Hartman backported it on April 18.

If you run Linux, the kernel update is the real fix. Patched releases are 6.12.82, 6.18.23, 6.19.13, and 7.0 mainline. Check what you're on:

```bash
uname -r
```

You can blunt the trigger without rebooting into a fresh kernel by switching KFENCE's sampler off, which removes the exact-size reporting that causes the false match:

```bash
# Temporary: disable KFENCE sampling until you can patch
echo 0 > /sys/module/kfence/parameters/sample_interval
# Or persist at boot via the kernel command line:
#   kfence.sample_interval=0
```

That's a tradeoff, not a fix. You're giving up a useful production memory-safety net to dodge a Medium-severity bug, so only reach for it if patching is genuinely blocked. The better lever for a multi-tenant host is to stop handing `CAP_BPF` and `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` to containers that don't need them, which closes the only path anyone has demonstrated to reach the allocation in the first place.

The thing that stays with me here isn't the corruption. It's that the safer the kernel gets, the more its debug and hardening features quietly change answers that older optimizations were built on top of. KFENCE didn't introduce a vulnerability. It told the truth about an allocation size, and a shortcut from 2023 couldn't handle the truth.

**PoC:** [bluedragonsecurity/CVE-2026-31429-POC](https://github.com/bluedragonsecurity/CVE-2026-31429-POC)  
**Upstream fix:** [mainline commit 0f42e3f4fe2a](https://git.kernel.org/stable/c/0f42e3f4fe2a58394e37241d02d9ca6ab7b7d516)  
**Introduced by:** commit `bf9f1baa279f` ("net: add dedicated kmem_cache for typical/small skb->head")  
**CVE record:** [CVE-2026-31429](https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-31429)
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="004" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-23111: One Flipped Exclamation Mark Gives You the Linux Kernel
DATE       : 2026-06-22
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/22/cve-2026-23111-nftables-catchall-uaf-lpe/
CVE        : cve-2026-23111, CVE-2026-23111
CATEGORIES : exploits, linux
TAGS       : cve-2026-23111, nftables, netfilter, use-after-free, lpe, rop, heap-exploitation
WORD_COUNT : 668
EXCERPT    : A single inverted condition in nf_tables' abort path lets any unprivileged user walk to root with better than 99% reliability. Here's exactly how it works.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
One character. One inverted `!` operator. That's the delta between an unpatched Linux kernel and a root shell.

CVE-2026-23111 is an nf_tables use-after-free that Exodus Intelligence researcher Oliver Sieber found in early 2025 and sat on until the upstream patch landed February 5, 2026. On June 8 they dropped the full technical writeup alongside working exploits that land root on Debian Bookworm, Debian Trixie, Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, and Ubuntu 24.04 LTS with greater than 99% reliability on an idle machine. The CVSS sits at 7.8, but that number undersells it for anything where users can open a shell.

The part that sticks with me isn't just the severity. It's that the bug is completely legible. You can read the vulnerable line, read the fix, and immediately understand why one is wrong and the other is right. That's rarer than you might think for kernel bugs.

## What nftables Is Doing Here

Netfilter's nftables subsystem uses a generational commit model to apply ruleset changes atomically. Every object carries a two-bit genmask indicating which generation it's active in. When you batch changes, they stage into the "next generation" without touching the running ruleset. Committing the batch flips a cursor and everything goes live at once. If any operation in a batch fails, an abort path fires to roll back everything that succeeded.

Sets in nftables can include a "catchall element" --- a wildcard that matches if nothing else does. Verdict maps are sets where each element maps to a verdict (jump to this chain, goto that one). The two features combine: a pipapo-backed verdict map with a catchall element pointing to a chain.

That combination is where this breaks.

## The Bug

When a verdict map is deleted, its catchall element gets deactivated and the target chain's reference counter decrements. Standard cleanup. But if anything else in the same batch fails afterward, nftables runs its abort path to undo the deletion.

The function responsible for undoing that deactivation is nft_map_catchall_activate(). Here's what it actually does:

The condition if (!nft_set_elem_active(ext, genmask)) reads: "if this element is NOT active, skip it." The catchall was just deactivated by the deletion step. So the abort path finds it, sees it's inactive, and skips right over it. The activation code at nft_clear() and nft_setelem_data_activate() never runs. The chain's reference counter stays at zero.

The fix, commit f41c5d151078c5348271ffaf8e7410d96f2d82f8, removes the !. One character.

With the refcount at zero while a rule still holds a valid pointer to the chain, you can delete the chain. When nftables later evaluates packets against that rule and follows the dangling verdict.chain pointer, you have a use-after-free.

## The Four-Batch Trigger

You need CAP_NET_ADMIN, which you get for free by creating a user namespace (unshare -Urmin). On most Debian and Ubuntu installs this works without any prerequisites. Ubuntu 24.04 has an AppArmor restriction on unprivileged user namespaces that you bypass with: aa-exec -p trinity -- unshare -Urmin /bin/sh

**Setup:** Create a table, a regular chain (the victim), a base chain with a rule that jumps to the regular chain, and a pipapo verdict map with a catchall element pointing to the regular chain. At this point the regular chain's use count is 2.

**Batch 1:** Delete the pipapo set. Include a second operation that intentionally fails to trigger the abort path. Refcount stays at 1, catchall stays inactive.

**Batch 2:** Any valid successful transaction. This flips the generation cursor.

**Batch 3:** Delete the pipapo set again. Decrements refcount from 1 to 0.

**Batch 4:** Delete the regular chain. Refcount is 0, kernel frees it. Base chain still has dangling verdict.chain pointer.

## Mitigations

Patch your kernel to upstream commit f41c5d151078c5348271ffaf8e7410d96f2d82f8 (February 5, 2026).

If immediate patching isn't possible, disable unprivileged user namespaces:
sysctl -w kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=0

See full writeup at: https://blog.exodusintel.com/2026/06/08/off-by-exploiting-a-use-after-free-in-the-linux-kernel/
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="005" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : RoguePlanet: Coercing Defender Into Writing SYSTEM-Owned Files (CVE-2026-50656)
DATE       : 2026-06-19
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/19/rogueplanet-cve-2026-50656-defender-toctou-lpe/
CVE        : CVE-2026-33825, CVE-2026-41091, CVE-2026-45498, CVE-2026-50656, cve-2026-50656
CATEGORIES : exploits, windows
TAGS       : cve-2026-50656, windows-defender, lpe, toctou, oplock, ntfs-junction, nightmare-eclipse
WORD_COUNT : 1305
EXCERPT    : A standard user mounts a VHDX, parks Defender's thread on an oplock, and lets the antivirus drop a SYSTEM-owned file into System32. Still no patch.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
I have spent a decade telling clients that Microsoft Defender is one of the better things they can leave running. So there is something funny, and a little grim, about an exploit that turns Defender itself into the process that drops a SYSTEM-owned file into `C:\Windows\System32` for you.

That exploit is RoguePlanet. It got a CVE on June 17, CVE-2026-50656, after the researcher who goes by Nightmare Eclipse published a working proof of concept on June 10 alongside Microsoft's June Patch Tuesday. As I write this on the 19th, there is still no patch. Microsoft has acknowledged the bug, rated it "Exploitation More Likely" on its own index, and said it is "working to provide a high quality security update." That is corporate for "give us a minute."

What makes this one worth your time is not the primitive. The primitive is old. It is the target that should bother you.

## The bug

CVE-2026-50656 is CWE-59, improper link resolution before file access. In plain terms it is a TOCTOU race, time of check to time of use. No memory corruption, no ROP chain, no kernel shellcode. Just a timing gap between when Defender checks where a file is and when it actually touches it, and a standard user standing in that gap with a crowbar.

Defender's scanning engine, `MsMpEng.exe`, runs as NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM. When it decides a file is malicious it does not simply delete it. It quarantines and rewrites paths, all as SYSTEM. If you can make the path it validated resolve somewhere else by the time it commits the operation, you get a SYSTEM-level file write aimed wherever you point it.

## Winning the race on purpose

Race conditions sound flaky. This one mostly is not, and that is the part defenders keep underrating. RoguePlanet does not gamble on timing. It forces the window open and holds it there.

Here is the sequence. A standard user mounts a crafted VHDX, either from local disk or straight off a remote SMB share. On Windows 10 and 11 a normal user is allowed to do that, and Defender scans what shows up. The exploit stages a file Defender will flag, then requests a batch opportunistic lock on the target with `FSCTL_REQUEST_BATCH_OPLOCK`. When `MsMpEng.exe` opens that file to remediate it, the oplock fires and parks Defender's thread mid-operation, holding it still while its handle stays open.

Now the attacker has all the time in the world. With Defender frozen, the exploit renames the original directory out of the way, recreates a directory at the same path, and converts that new directory into an NTFS mount point, a junction, pointing at `C:\Windows\System32`. Then it releases the oplock. Defender wakes up, finishes the privileged operation it had already started, and follows the junction into System32. The file lands as SYSTEM, in a directory a standard user cannot normally write a single byte to.

```c
// Illustrative sketch of the RoguePlanet primitive. Not the published PoC.
// 0. Bait lives on an attacker-mounted VHDX volume (drive X:).
HANDLE hBait = CreateFileW(L"X:\\stage\\payload.dll", /* ... */);

// 1. Arm a batch oplock on the file Defender will open to clean it.
REQUEST_OPLOCK_INPUT_BUFFER in = {
    .StructureVersion = REQUEST_OPLOCK_CURRENT_VERSION,
    .RequestedOplockLevel = OPLOCK_LEVEL_CACHE_HANDLE,
    .Flags = REQUEST_OPLOCK_INPUT_FLAG_REQUEST,
};
DeviceIoControl(hBait, FSCTL_REQUEST_BATCH_OPLOCK, &in, /* async */);

// 2. MsMpEng.exe (SYSTEM) opens the file -> oplock breaks ->
//    Defender's remediation thread is now parked, handle held.

// 3. Swap the path out from under it while it sleeps.
MoveFileW(L"X:\\stage", L"X:\\stage_old");
CreateDirectoryW(L"X:\\stage", NULL);
SetMountPoint(L"X:\\stage", L"\\??\\C:\\Windows\\System32"); // junction

// 4. Release the oplock. Defender resumes, follows the junction,
//    and completes its SYSTEM-level write inside System32.
```

From there it is the usual endgame. Drop a DLL a SYSTEM process will load, or a binary that runs at boot, and you have NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM.

If you have read James Forshaw's work on oplocks and junctions, none of the mechanics are new. Oplock to suspend a privileged file mover, junction to redirect it, has been a Windows LPE staple for years. What is new is that the privileged file mover is the antivirus. The researcher also notes the chain originally reached remote code execution through a malicious VHDX hosted on a remote SMB share, until Microsoft's May mitigations closed those paths and forced a rework down to local privilege escalation.

## Why your patches did not save you

ThreatLocker reproduced RoguePlanet on a fully patched Windows 11 box running the June cumulative update KB5094126 and watched a standard user land a SYSTEM shell. Will Dormann and others confirmed the same on patched machines. The researcher says it fires regardless of whether Real-Time Protection is on or off, which kills the lazy "just turn off RTP" reflex before you reach for it.

Reliability does vary. Nightmare Eclipse reports a 100 percent hit rate on some hardware and a frustrating miss rate on others, exactly what you expect from anything timing dependent. The current PoC does not run on Windows Server, and not because Server is patched. It is because a standard user on Server cannot mount the VHDX that delivers the payload. The researcher believes Server is vulnerable to the same flaw through a different delivery path, and I would not bet against that.

This is also the fourth file I have written up from the same lineage. RoguePlanet is the latest in Nightmare Eclipse's retaliation campaign against Microsoft, the run that already gave us BlueHammer (CVE-2026-33825), RedSun (CVE-2026-41091), and the UnDefend disruptor (CVE-2026-45498). Huntress has documented that earlier tooling inside live intrusions. Microsoft says it has not seen RoguePlanet itself used in the wild. Given the track record, "yet" is doing heavy lifting in that sentence.

## What to actually do

Signatures are a waste of effort here, and the researcher says so himself. Small edits to the PoC defeat any string you write, because the exploitable behavior belongs to Defender, not to the payload. Stop trying to pattern-match the dropper.

The control that buys you the most right now is taking VHD and VHDX mounting away from standard users. The current exploit needs that mount to stage both the bait and the junction, and most users in an enterprise have no business attaching virtual disks. Strip the Mount handler for `.vhd`, `.vhdx`, and `.iso` so double-click mounting dies, and lock the capability down through Group Policy. That breaks the delivery, not the bug, but breaking the delivery is enough for today.

The one that actually held under testing is application allowlisting. ThreatLocker's default deny blocked RoguePlanet outright, and you get the same outcome from WDAC, now branded App Control for Business, or AppLocker in enforced mode. If the SYSTEM-owned payload cannot execute, the SYSTEM-level write is a dead end.

Then hunt the behavior, not the file. The tell is a directory junction or NTFS mount point that appears under a user-writable path, points into System32, and shows up in the same breath as an oplock on a file `MsMpEng.exe` has open. Your EDR can see reparse-point creation and SYSTEM writes landing in System32 from a Defender-initiated operation. That pattern is not something a clean machine does.

And watch the MSRC advisory for CVE-2026-50656. When the fix ships, push it out of band the same day rather than folding it into next month's cycle.

The uncomfortable part is the lesson nobody on the vendor side wants printed. We spent years moving privileged file operations into the security product, on the theory that the security product is the trustworthy one. RoguePlanet is what happens when an attacker agrees with that theory and just asks the product to write somewhere it should not.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="006" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : ITScape (CVE-2026-46316): The First In-Kernel KVM/arm64 Guest-to-Host Escape
DATE       : 2026-06-14
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/14/itscape-kvm-arm64-guest-host-escape-cve-2026-46316/
CVE        : CVE-2026-46316
CATEGORIES : exploits, linux-kernel
TAGS       : CVE-2026-46316, KVM, arm64, UAF, guest-escape, cloud-security
WORD_COUNT : 1545
EXCERPT    : A race condition in KVM's vGIC-ITS cache invalidation produces a double-put UAF that researcher Hyunwoo Kim turned into full host kernel code execution from inside a guest VM.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here's what gets me about this one: it's not a QEMU bug. Every KVM escape write-up you read is almost always about QEMU's device emulation layer, something in virtio or the e1000 model. This one is in-kernel KVM itself, specifically the GICv3 Interrupt Translation Service emulation on arm64, and it gets you to host kernel code execution with root privilege.

Hyunwoo Kim ([@v4bel](https://x.com/v4bel)) dropped CVE-2026-46316, named ITScape, to the oss-security mailing list on June 10, 2026. The patch had already landed in mainline on June 5 (commit [13031fb6b835](https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/commit/?id=13031fb6b8357fbbcded2a7f4cba73e4781ee594)), five days after he submitted it. The PoC is live at [github.com/V4bel/ITScape](https://github.com/V4bel/ITScape). This affects every arm64 KVM host running kernel versions from commit [8201d1028caa](https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/commit/?id=8201d1028caa) (April 25, 2024) up through the day before the fix.

## The Vulnerable Code

KVM on arm64 maintains a per-ITS translation cache, an xarray, that caches the result of mapping a (DeviceID, EventID) pair to a `vgic_irq` struct. When the guest clears the `GICR_CTLR.EnableLPIs` bit on a redistributor, KVM calls `vgic_its_invalidate_all_caches()`, which walks every ITS attached to the VM and drains each cache with `vgic_its_invalidate_cache()`:

```c
static void vgic_its_invalidate_cache(struct vgic_its *its)
{
    struct kvm *kvm = its->dev->kvm;
    struct vgic_irq *irq;
    unsigned long idx;

    xa_for_each(&its->translation_cache, idx, irq) {
        xa_erase(&its->translation_cache, idx);
        vgic_put_irq(kvm, irq);
    }
}
```

There are two problems sitting right next to each other. First, this drain holds only `rcu_read_lock`. It does not hold `its_lock` or `cmd_lock`, so nothing prevents two concurrent contexts from walking the same xarray at the same time. Second, `vgic_put_irq()` is called on `irq`, the `xa_for_each` iteration variable, rather than on the value `xa_erase()` returns. `xa_erase` is atomic. When two contexts race to erase the same index, only one gets the object back. The other gets NULL. But both call `vgic_put_irq()` on the iterator, meaning the reference on that `vgic_irq` drops twice. That's a use-after-free on a `kmalloc-cg-96` slab object, freed via `kfree_rcu`.

The trigger condition is two or more vCPUs clearing their own `GICR_CTLR.EnableLPIs` simultaneously. The `cmpxchg(ENABLE_LPIS -> RWP)` is per-vCPU state, so each vCPU enters the drain once per cycle. But the ITS cache itself is VM-global. Four vCPUs racing into the same xarray gives six concurrent drain pairs. With 128 devices times 1024 events pre-loaded into the cache (about 130,000 entries), the walk is long enough that the overlap window is reliable.

## The Exploit Chain

Kim's PoC is not a straightforward crash dump. It's a six-stage chain that goes from a race condition to arbitrary code execution in the host kernel, confirmed by creating `/ITScape` on the host filesystem owned by uid 0. Here's how each stage connects to the next.

**Stage 1: Fill the cache.** The guest programs MAPD, MAPTI, and INT commands into the ITS command queue for 128 devices with 1024 event IDs each. This populates the translation cache with ~130k entries, all pointing to their respective `vgic_irq` objects. The LPI property table is set to all-disabled to prevent actual interrupt delivery while the cache fills normally.

**Stage 2: Race and free.** Four vCPUs synchronize at a barrier, then simultaneously clear `EnableLPIs`. Multiple vCPUs enter `vgic_its_invalidate_cache` and walk the same xarray concurrently. The double-put fires. The freed `vgic_irq` is a 96-byte `kmalloc-cg-96` object handed to `kfree_rcu`. To actually return it to the free list (not just queue it), the guest performs repeated vCPU exits (`GUEST_SYNC`), which advance host RCU and flush the `KVFREE_RCU_BATCHED` sheaf without any host-side sleep.

**Stage 3: Guest-side cross-cache reclaim.** This is the most novel part. A 64MB `guest_memfd` region was set up at VM creation with `GUEST_MEMFD_FLAG_MMAP | GUEST_MEMFD_FLAG_INIT_SHARED`. When the guest faults in the entire region, the host allocates backing pages for it. One of those backing pages is the cg-96 slab page just freed by the double-put. The guest maps it directly via mmap and writes to every 96-byte slot, crafting a fake `vgic_irq` with controlled field values:

```c
*(volatile u32 *)(obj + 0)  = 0;          // irq_lock = unlocked
*(volatile u32 *)(obj + 4)  = 0x2000;     // intid = LPI (not SPI, avoids broadcast)
*(volatile u64 *)(obj + 32) = sentinel;   // ap_list.prev (leak lands here)
*(volatile u64 *)(obj + 40) = 0;          // vcpu = NULL
*(volatile u64 *)(obj + 48) = target_vcpu;// target_vcpu (controlled)
*(volatile u8  *)(obj + 56) = 0x20;       // active = true
*(volatile u32 *)(obj + 60) = 2;          // refcount >= 1
*(volatile u64 *)(obj + 80) = 0;          // ops = NULL (native path)
```

**Stage 4: KASLR bypass (two-stage).** With `ops = NULL`, `vgic_queue_irq_unlock` skips the function pointer path and falls into `list_add_tail(&irq->ap_list, &vcpu->arch.vgic_cpu.ap_list_head)`. The `list_add` implementation writes `head->prev` into `new->prev`, which is the fake irq's `ap_list.prev` field sitting in guest-writable gmem. Setting `target_vcpu` to a calculated offset gives an arbitrary-read primitive.

Stage 4a: Send MOVI to move the LPI to collection 0, which causes the kernel to write the real vCPU0 kernel address into the fake irq's `target_vcpu`. Read it back from gmem+48.

Stage 4b: Set `target_vcpu` such that `*(target_vcpu + 6656)` resolves to `vCPU0.pmu.overflow_work.func`, which holds `kvm_pmu_perf_overflow_notify_vcpu`, a kernel image address. The `list_add` deposits that value into gmem+32. One `GUEST_SYNC` later, a surviving vCPU reads it and computes `kimage_voffset`. Note: vCPU0 crashes on the subsequent `.text` store in the same `list_add`, but by then the leak has already landed in gmem.

**Stage 5: Write-what-where.** Now `ops` is set to a guest-controlled gmem address (obtained by chaining fake irqs through the real `ap_list`). The `irq_ops.queue_irq_unlock` field sits at offset 16. When the kernel calls `irq->ops->queue_irq_unlock(kvm, irq, flags)`, it lands on a gadget at `ed_deschedule+0xd4` (runtime address computed with the leaked slide). That gadget stores `*(irq + 0x28)` to the address at `*(irq + 0x20)`. Over four sweeps through ITS#1's command queue (ITS#1 has an independent `cmd_lock` since vCPU0 died holding ITS#0's), this overwrites:

- `poweroff_cmd` (`.data`, default `"/sbin/poweroff"`) with `"/bin/touch /ITScape"`
- `arp_tbl.gc_work.work.func` (originally `neigh_periodic_work`) with `orderly_poweroff`

**Stage 6: Delayed host code execution.** vCPU1 exits via `GUEST_DONE`, healing the preempt leak from the gadget path. vCPUs 2 and 3 loop on `GUEST_SYNC` to keep the VM alive. Within about 15 seconds, the neighbor garbage collector workqueue re-arms and calls the overwritten func. The call chain is:

```
overwritten arp_tbl.gc_work.work.func -> orderly_poweroff()
  -> schedule_work(&poweroff_work)
    -> poweroff_work_func()
      -> __orderly_poweroff()
        -> run_cmd("/bin/touch /ITScape")
          -> call_usermodehelper(argv[0], argv, envp, UMH_WAIT_EXEC)
```

`call_usermodehelper` runs as root in the host kernel context. The escape is confirmed when `ls -la /ITScape` shows a zero-byte file owned by uid 0.

## The Fix

Three lines:

```diff
 xa_for_each(&its->translation_cache, idx, irq) {
-    xa_erase(&its->translation_cache, idx);
-    vgic_put_irq(kvm, irq);
+    irq = xa_erase(&its->translation_cache, idx);
+    if (irq)
+        vgic_put_irq(kvm, irq);
 }
```

`xa_erase()` is atomic. Only the context that successfully removes the entry gets the pointer back. By dropping the put only when `xa_erase()` returns non-NULL, the reference is released exactly once regardless of how many concurrent drains race on the same index. Introduced by commit 8201d1028caa on April 25, 2024, fixed by commit 13031fb6b835 on June 5, 2026. Two years of exposure on every arm64 KVM host.

## Who's Affected

This is arm64-only. `arch/arm64/kvm/vgic/` is not compiled for x86. The exploit requires guest root (EL1) to drive GIC/ITS MMIO. On a public cloud arm64 instance, that's exactly what you have. The multi-tenant threat model is direct: a guest escapes, acquires host kernel root, and can access other tenants' memory or workloads on the same physical machine.

AWS Graviton, Alibaba Yitian 710, Ampere Altra, anything running arm64 KVM with untrusted guests should treat this as urgent.

## What to Do

Check your host kernel. If you're on a distro kernel, you need the backport of commit `13031fb6b835` from upstream. For checking directly:

```bash
# Check if patch is present in kernel source
grep -n "irq = xa_erase" arch/arm64/kvm/vgic/vgic-its.c
```

If that grep returns empty, the host is vulnerable. On Ubuntu, this is tracked under USN. On RHEL/Rocky, check errata for the arm64 KVM security advisory.

If you run QEMU with KVM and accept untrusted guests, temporarily limit vCPU count where you can (fewer concurrent drains, tighter race window), though this does not eliminate the vulnerability. The only real fix is the patched kernel. Build from mainline at any commit after June 5, 2026, or wait for your distro's backport.

If you want to watch for exploitation attempts, monitor for unexpected files created at filesystem root on KVM hosts, or for `call_usermodehelper` invocations spawning unexpected processes. Both are noisy indicators. The cleaner approach is to confirm patch status before any of that is relevant.

Kim's write-up and PoC are at [github.com/V4bel/ITScape](https://github.com/V4bel/ITScape). The full technical analysis is in `assets/write-up.md` in that repo. Worth reading the whole thing. The two-stage KASLR bypass and the guest-driven cross-cache via `guest_memfd` are genuinely clever solutions to hard constraints.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="007" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-20245: Cisco's Seventh SD-WAN Zero-Day of the Year Hands You Root From the Management Plane
DATE       : 2026-06-07
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/07/cve-2026-20245-cisco-sdwan-manager-root-rce/
CVE        : CVE-2026-20127, CVE-2026-20182, CVE-2026-20245
CATEGORIES : exploits, network-infrastructure
TAGS       : cisco, sd-wan, command-injection, zero-day, root, mandiant
WORD_COUNT : 1152
EXCERPT    : A netadmin-to-root command injection in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, exploited in the wild with no patch, and it sits on the one box that can reprogram your whole edge.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here is what gets me about this one. Cisco shipped CVE-2026-20245 on June 5 with no patch and no workaround, and it is the seventh SD-WAN zero-day they have confirmed exploited this year. Seven. We are barely into June. The management plane of a major enterprise networking product has become a recurring crime scene, and the vendor's own advisory reads more like an incident response runbook than a security bulletin.

Let me be precise about what this is, because the headlines are doing the thing where "actively exploited zero-day" and "root" get smashed together until the reader assumes the internet is on fire. It is not an unauthenticated bug. CVE-2026-20245 carries a CVSS of 7.8, and the attacker needs netadmin privileges on Catalyst SD-WAN Manager before any of this works. That sounds reassuring until you remember how attackers actually get netadmin on these boxes.

## The bug itself

The vulnerable surface is the command-line interface of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager. The root cause is the oldest story in the book: insufficient validation of user-supplied input. An authenticated attacker uploads a crafted file through the CLI workflow, and a value that should have been treated as inert data gets interpreted as part of a command. From there it executes as root on the underlying appliance.

Classic command injection on a file upload path. No clever memory corruption, no race window, no kernel primitive. Somebody concatenated attacker-controlled bytes into something that eventually hit a shell or an exec, and the input filter either was not there or was not good enough. The reason this matters is not the sophistication. It is the location. SD-WAN Manager is not an edge router. It is the orchestrator that pushes configuration down to every edge device in the fabric. Root there is not root on one box. It is administrative reach over the network's nervous system.

Cisco said as much in the disclosure. They observed limited cases where exploitation resulted in configuration changes pushed to edge devices. Read that twice. The attackers were not just popping a shell to prove they could. They were using the compromised manager to reprogram downstream infrastructure, which is exactly what you would do if you wanted to reroute traffic, weaken a segment, or open a path you could come back to later.

## How an attacker actually reaches it

The netadmin prerequisite is doing a lot of work in the risk conversation, so let me walk the realistic chain. You do not start with netadmin. You arrive at it.

Cisco explicitly tied the prerequisite access to two paths. Stolen credentials is the boring one and probably the common one, given how often these management consoles end up with reused passwords, no MFA on the local admin, or a service account whose secret is sitting in a Git repo somewhere. The more interesting path is chaining. Cisco named CVE-2026-20182 and CVE-2026-20127, both previously disclosed SD-WAN flaws, as ways to obtain the privileged access this bug then converts to root. I wrote up the CVE-2026-20182 vManage authentication bypass back on May 27. At the time it looked like a serious but self-contained problem. Now it is link one of a two-stage chain that ends at root on the management plane. That is how these things age. A bug you patched late becomes the on-ramp for the next one.

So the practical attack looks like this. Get netadmin through an auth bypass or a stolen credential. Authenticate to the SD-WAN Manager CLI. Upload your crafted file. Command injection fires, you are root, and now you own the box that controls the fabric. The whole sequence assumes the attacker already did the hard part, which in modern intrusions is frequently true before anyone notices.

There is no public proof-of-concept as of this writing, and I am not going to invent one. Mandiant reported the flaw, specifically Chester Sng, Pete Boonyakarn, and Logeswaran Nadarajan, and Cisco's PSIRT said it learned about exploitation in June, which strongly suggests the advisory was rushed out because the bug was already being used. When a vendor publishes with no patch ready, that is the tell. They decided telling you was more urgent than waiting to fix it.

## What you can actually do right now

There is no patch. Cisco has indicated a fixed Catalyst SD-WAN Manager release is coming, but at disclosure there was nothing to install and no config knob that turns the bug off. So mitigation here is not patch management. It is incident response posture.

First, preserve evidence before you touch anything. Cisco's guidance is to collect admin-tech data from each control component before any upgrade activity, because if the box is already compromised, upgrading can destroy the forensic trail you will need.

```
# Run on each control component (vManage / SD-WAN Manager node)
request admin-tech
# Pull the resulting bundle off-box to preserved storage before any upgrade
```

Second, hunt for the abuse pattern rather than waiting for a signature. The behavior to look for is unexpected root-level activity on the manager, suspicious file uploads through the CLI workflow, and configuration pushes to edge devices that do not map to a change ticket. That last one is your highest-value signal, because legitimate config pushes are auditable and attacker pushes usually are not.

```
# Review for out-of-band configuration template pushes to edge devices
show running-config | include template
# Cross-reference push events in vManage audit logs against your change records
# Any device config push with no corresponding approved change = investigate
```

Third, close the front door. Since the only known way in requires netadmin, harden that access now. Enforce MFA on every administrative account that can reach the manager, rotate credentials for netadmin and any service accounts, and confirm CVE-2026-20182 and CVE-2026-20127 are fully remediated across the estate, not just on the box you remembered. A patched manager with an unpatched auth bypass in front of it is still exposed.

Fourth, restrict who can even talk to the management interface. SD-WAN Manager should never have its CLI or admin surface reachable from general user networks or the internet. Put it behind a management VLAN with an explicit allowlist, and treat any connection from outside that scope as hostile.

One hard truth to close on. Cisco was blunt that if your logs show abuse, installing the future fix will not by itself clean the environment. Root on the management plane means the attacker may have pushed persistence to edge devices that survives a manager upgrade. If you find evidence of compromise, this is a TAC engagement and a full edge-config review, not a reboot-and-move-on. The patch fixes the door. It does not evict whoever already walked through it.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="008" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-20230: An Unauthenticated SSRF in Cisco Unified CM That Writes Files and Ends at Root
DATE       : 2026-06-07
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/07/cve-2026-20230-cisco-unified-cm-webdialer-ssrf-root/
CVE        : CVE-2025-20309, CVE-2026-20045, CVE-2026-20230
CATEGORIES : exploits, network-infrastructure
TAGS       : cisco, unified-cm, ssrf, file-write, root, webdialer
WORD_COUNT : 1196
EXCERPT    : A no-auth SSRF in Cisco Unified CM turns a crafted HTTP request into an arbitrary file write on the appliance OS, and from there into root. PoC code is already public.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here is what gets me about this one. The whole bug hinges on a service that ships off by default, and yet I would not bet a single dollar that it is off in your environment. CVE-2026-20230 is an unauthenticated server-side request forgery in Cisco Unified Communications Manager, the box that runs enterprise voice for a frightening percentage of the Fortune 500. Cisco disclosed it on June 4, public proof-of-concept code is already circulating, and the gating condition is the WebDialer service being enabled. WebDialer is the kind of feature somebody turned on in 2019 for a click-to-call integration and nobody has thought about since.

Let me be precise, because the score and the rating disagree and that gap is the whole story. The advisory, cisco-sa-cucm-ssrf-cXPnHcW, carries a CVSS base of 8.6. Cisco rated the impact Critical anyway. Those two facts only reconcile when you understand what the CVSS number is measuring and what it is leaving out.

## The bug, and why the math lies a little

At its core this is CWE-918, a textbook SSRF. Unified CM and its Session Management Edition fail to properly validate certain HTTP requests handled by the WebDialer service. An unauthenticated attacker on the network sends a crafted request, the server gets coerced into making a request the attacker controls, and the usual SSRF outcome would be reaching some internal-only resource. That part is ordinary.

What is not ordinary is where it lands. This SSRF does not just let you poke at internal endpoints. It results in an arbitrary file write on the underlying operating system. That is the primitive that matters. Once you can drop bytes of your choosing onto the appliance filesystem as an unauthenticated remote party, the conversation is no longer about information disclosure. It is about code execution, and Cisco says plainly that the file write can be used to escalate to root.

So why is the CVSS only 8.6? Because the vector scores the integrity impact of the file write and stops there. No confidentiality loss in the immediate primitive, no availability loss, just integrity. The root escalation that the file write enables is a second step, and CVSS notoriously undercounts chains where one bug sets up the next. Cisco's analysts looked at the end state, which is full root on a voice appliance sitting in the middle of your network, and slapped Critical on it. They are right. If you triage this by the 8.6 alone you are going to deprioritize an unauthenticated path to root.

## What exploitation looks like

There is public PoC code, and I am not going to republish a weaponized chain here. The shape of it is not a secret though, and walking the logic is more useful than handing out a copy-paste.

The attacker reaches the WebDialer HTTP surface without credentials. The request carries input that should have been treated as inert and instead steers a server-side fetch. Conceptually it looks like the server being told to go retrieve or process something on the attacker's terms:

```
POST /webdialer/Webdialer HTTP/1.1
Host: cucm.victim.example
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

<crafted parameters that coerce a server-side request
 and influence the destination/path of a write>
```

The exact parameters are what the PoC fills in, and I would rather you patch than reconstruct them. The point is that the validation failure lets attacker-controlled data decide where the server reaches and, downstream, where bytes get written. Turn that file write toward something the system later executes with high privilege, a script in a path that runs as root, a config that gets sourced, and you have converted an SSRF into a root shell. That is the move. SSRF to file write to root is a clean three-link chain, and the only thing standing between an attacker and link one is whether WebDialer is listening.

Cisco's PSIRT said at disclosure that it had not seen the flaw used in attacks. I read that as a clock, not a comfort. Public PoC plus an unauthenticated trigger plus an appliance that nobody reboots means the window between "no observed exploitation" and "your honeypot is full of it" is short. The independent researcher who found this worked through SSD Secure Disclosure, which tells you it went through a real coordinated process rather than getting dumped, but coordination ends the moment the PoC is public.

## This is a pattern, not an incident

Unified CM keeps producing unauthenticated, root-level trouble, and that is the part worth sitting with. Last July, Cisco pulled a hard-coded root SSH account that had been left in from development, CVE-2025-20309, a clean 10.0. In January it patched CVE-2026-20045, an unauthenticated RCE across several of its voice products that was already being exploited, serious enough that CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. Now this. Same product family, same theme of a request reaching something it never should have.

I wrote up Cisco's seventh SD-WAN zero-day of the year just two days ago. Different product, identical root cause category: input that should have been data getting treated as instruction. When a vendor produces this volume of "we did not validate the input" bugs across multiple product lines in a single year, the lesson for defenders is not "patch faster." It is "assume the next one is coming and reduce what is reachable now."

## What to actually do

Patch first. For the 14 train the fixed release is 14SU6. For the 15 train the full Service Update, 15SU5, is not due until September 2026, so until then you are on the interim COP patch or you are turning the feature off. Do not wait for September.

If you cannot patch this week, kill WebDialer. Open Cisco Unified CM Administration, switch to Cisco Unified Serviceability, go to Tools then Control Center - Feature Services, and look at Cisco WebDialer Web Service under CTI Services. If it says Started, you are exposed. To disable it properly go to Tools then Service Activation, uncheck Cisco WebDialer Web Service, and save. While you are in there, audit whether any business workflow actually depends on it. If click-to-call is dead weight from a project that wrapped years ago, leave WebDialer off permanently after you patch and shrink your attack surface for free.

Then go hunting, because "no observed exploitation" is a statement about Cisco's visibility, not yours. Pull the Unified CM web logs and look for malformed or unexpected requests against the WebDialer paths. Watch for outbound connections originating from the CUCM appliance itself, since SSRF and post-exploitation both generate traffic a voice server has no business making. And watch the filesystem on the appliance for new or modified files in any path that influences execution or privilege, because that file write is the whole game and it leaves a footprint.

These voice boxes sit quietly in the core of the network, trusted, rarely scanned, and almost never rebuilt. That is exactly why they keep getting popped.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="009" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-45247: A Magento Cache Plugin Hands You RCE Through One Cookie
DATE       : 2026-06-06
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/06/cve-2026-45247-mirasvit-cache-warmer-php-object-injection/
CVE        : CVE-2026-45247, cve-2026-45247
CATEGORIES : exploits, web
TAGS       : CVE-2026-45247, magento, php-object-injection, deserialization, RCE, CWE-502
WORD_COUNT : 1214
EXCERPT    : An unauthenticated PHP object injection in the Mirasvit Cache Warmer extension. One crafted CacheWarmer cookie, no login, code execution on the box. CISA's deadline for this one is today.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here's the part that gets me about CVE-2026-45247. There's no exotic memory primitive, no race window, no kernel internals to reverse. It's a storefront cookie that gets handed straight to PHP's `unserialize()`. That's it. A bug pattern we have been writing about since 2015, still shipping in 2026, in a plugin that sits on roughly 6,000 live Magento stores. CISA gave federal agencies until today, June 6, to patch it. So this is a good time to pull it apart.

CVE-2026-45247 is a CVSS 9.8 unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in Mirasvit Full Page Cache Warmer for Magento 2 and Adobe Commerce. The CVE was disclosed publicly on May 26, Mirasvit shipped the fix in version 1.11.12 on May 25, and CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on June 3 after Imperva and Sansec both reported attacks landing in the wild. The three-day federal remediation clock under BOD 22-01 runs out today.

## What the extension does, and where it goes wrong

Cache Warmer is a performance plugin. It watches which storefront pages are cached, and proactively re-warms them so visitors and Googlebot never hit a cold cache. Useful. The problem is how it tracks state between the crawler and the storefront. It uses a cookie named `CacheWarmer`, and part of that cookie value gets passed into PHP's native `unserialize()` on the storefront request path.

That single design choice is the whole vulnerability. `unserialize()` on attacker-controlled input is CWE-502, deserialization of untrusted data, and it has been on every appsec top-ten list for a decade. When you call `unserialize()` on bytes the client controls, you are letting the client decide which PHP objects get reconstructed in memory, with which property values. No authentication check sits in front of this. No admin session. Any anonymous request to the storefront carrying the right cookie reaches the sink.

Sansec, the Dutch Magento security shop that did the primary writeup, put it plainly: because the value comes straight from the client, the attacker controls the objects PHP reconstructs.

## From object injection to a shell

Object injection on its own is not code execution. You don't get to call `system()` directly from a serialized blob. What you get is control over object instantiation and the magic methods that fire during and after deserialization, things like `__wakeup()` and `__destruct()`. The exploitation work is finding a gadget chain, a sequence of already-loaded classes whose magic methods can be strung together so that controlled property values flow into a dangerous call.

Magento is a gift here. It is an enormous codebase with a deep dependency tree, Laminas, Symfony components, the whole composer graph. That is a huge pool of classes sitting in the autoloader, which means plenty of raw material for a chain that ends in a function call. This is the same reason Magento deserialization bugs have historically gone straight to RCE rather than fizzling out as a crash. The gadgets are already on the box. You are not smuggling in code, you are rearranging code that's already trusted.

Imperva watched the real payloads. The serialized objects they intercepted reached for `system()` and `current()` to run commands, and in several cases the attackers fired benign test commands first to confirm execution worked before doing anything noisy. That's operator behavior, not a scanner spraying garbage. Someone is building a target list of confirmed-exploitable stores.

A request to a vulnerable store looks roughly like this:

```http
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: shop.example.com
Cookie: CacheWarmer:Tzo0MDoi...base64-serialized-PHP-object...
```

The storefront takes the cookie, splits on the `CacheWarmer:` marker, base64-decodes the remainder, and feeds it to `unserialize()`. The decoded bytes are a serialized PHP object graph, something like `O:40:"SomeGadgetClass":2:{...}`, and once PHP reconstructs that graph the gadget chain does the rest.

## Spotting it in your logs

This is one of those rare cases where detection is genuinely easy, because the on-wire signature is distinctive. Serialized PHP objects base64-encode to strings that begin with `Tz`, `Qz`, or `YT`. Those map to the serialized type prefixes `O:` (object), `C:` (custom object), and `a:` (array). So a `CacheWarmer` cookie whose value matches `CacheWarmer:(Tz|Qz|YT)` is a strong exploitation indicator. Legitimate cookie traffic for this plugin should not carry serialized objects from the client at all.

Drop this into your access-log review or your WAF logic:

```bash
# Hunt nginx/apache access logs for the cookie signature
grep -E 'CacheWarmer:(Tz|Qz|YT)' /var/log/nginx/access.log

# Or pull it out of structured logs by header
zgrep -hE 'CacheWarmer:(Tz|Qz|YT)' /var/log/nginx/*.gz
```

If you find hits, treat the box as compromised and start incident response, because the test-command behavior Imperva described means a confirmed-execution store gets revisited. Imperva saw the activity concentrated on gaming and business sites, with the US, UK, France, and Australia taking the most traffic.

## Fix it

Update Mirasvit Full Page Cache Warmer to 1.11.12 or later. Every version before that is vulnerable. The patch was out May 25, so the update has been available longer than the exploitation has been loud.

If you cannot deploy the extension update immediately, the holding move is to block the deserialization path at the edge. Add a WAF rule that rejects any request where the `CacheWarmer` cookie value matches `CacheWarmer:(Tz|Qz|YT)`, since no legitimate client request needs to send a serialized object in that cookie. That is a tight, low-false-positive rule and it kills the public exploitation pattern outright.

Longer term, if you maintain Magento extensions, this is the lesson that refuses to die. Never call `unserialize()` on anything a client can touch. If you must deserialize structured data from a request, use `json_decode()`, or pin `unserialize($data, ['allowed_classes' => false])` so no objects get reconstructed at all. The `allowed_classes` argument has existed since PHP 7.0. Nine years. The fix for the entire bug class is one function argument, and we are still shipping the version without it.

Six thousand stores is the number Sansec could see. Cloudflare and other CDNs hide the real count, so assume it is higher. If you run Magento and you have ever installed a Mirasvit performance extension, check your version today, not after the long weekend.

## Sources

- Sansec research: [https://sansec.io/research/mirasvit-cache-warmer-object-injection](https://sansec.io/research/mirasvit-cache-warmer-object-injection)
- Imperva: [https://www.imperva.com/blog/imperva-customers-protected-against-cve-2026-45247-in-mirasvit-full-page-cache-warmer-for-magento/](https://www.imperva.com/blog/imperva-customers-protected-against-cve-2026-45247-in-mirasvit-full-page-cache-warmer-for-magento/)
- SecurityWeek: [https://www.securityweek.com/mirasvit-vulnerability-exploited-to-execute-code-on-magento-servers/](https://www.securityweek.com/mirasvit-vulnerability-exploited-to-execute-code-on-magento-servers/)
- CISA KEV addition: [https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/alerts/2026/06/03/cisa-adds-one-known-exploited-vulnerability-catalog](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/alerts/2026/06/03/cisa-adds-one-known-exploited-vulnerability-catalog)
- NVD: [https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-45247](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-45247)
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="010" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2025-48595: The Android Framework Integer Overflow That Spyware Got To First
DATE       : 2026-06-04
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/04/cve-2025-48595-android-framework-integer-overflow-lpe/
CVE        : CVE-2025-48595, cve-2025-48595
CATEGORIES : exploits, mobile
